hypothesis: Using patient-level longitudinal EHR sequences with prior utilization and lab trends will predict 30-day unplanned hospital readmission better than a static feature baseline built from demographics, diagnosis codes, and last-encounter labs, when evaluated on future admissions from the same health system.
domain: Hospital readmission prediction in a multi-site health system using electronic health records
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Longitudinal EHR Sequences for Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 2–5 years of inpatient discharge records from a multi-site health system EHR warehouse. The population is adult discharges (age ≥18) with complete electronic follow-up data for 30 days post-discharge. Data includes demographics (age, sex, insurance), diagnoses (ICD-10 codes from current and all prior admissions in the lookback window), procedures, medications (orders and administrations), lab results (with timestamps and values), vital signs, encounter-level summaries (LOS, admission source, disposition), and deterministic indicators for readmission status.

The label is binary: unplanned readmission within 30 calendar days of discharge. Unplanned admissions are identified via admission type field or validated against elective procedure schedules. Planned readmissions, patients discharged against medical advice, and inpatient deaths are excluded. This label definition aligns with hospital operations and quality metrics (e.g., CMS readmission penalties).

Expected data quality issues include missing lab values (imputed with domain-specific strategies: critical labs forward-filled, optional labs marked as missing), inconsistent diagnosis coding across sites (mitigated by aggregating to Elixhauser comorbidity categories), and censoring of follow-up data at the observation window boundary (only admissions with complete 30-day follow-up are eligible as index cases).

### 2. Split Strategy: Temporal (Chronological)

A temporal split is mandatory here because readmission is a future event, and the hypothesis emphasizes longitudinal trends. The observation window (months 1–36 or end of data) is divided into three non-overlapping periods:

- **Train (months 1–24):** All eligible discharges in this window become training index cases. All upstream encounter history and lab data is used as features.
- **Validation (months 25–30):** Discharges in this window serve as validation index cases. Features are computed using historical data within each patient's lookback window, but preprocessing parameters (scalers, encoders, imputation) are fit only on training data and applied here.
- **Test (months 31–36 or to end of data):** Final held-out evaluation set. Represents future admissions.

This split prevents information leakage: no training data comes after any validation or test index discharge, and sequence models cannot exploit future utilization patterns. It reflects the deployment setting—the model will be applied to new discharges and will not have access to readmission outcomes at inference time. If the health system operates multiple sites, the test set is stratified by site to ensure representativeness.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Static baseline features** (for the logistic regression baseline): age at admission, sex, insurance category, Elixhauser comorbidity count and indicators (CHF, COPD, diabetes, renal disease, MI), admission source, discharge disposition (home, SNF, rehab), primary service line, length of stay (hours), count of admissions in prior 6 months, count of admissions in prior 12 months, prior ER visit count. Continuous variables are standardized (zero mean, unit variance fit on training set).

**Longitudinal sequence features** (for the sequence model): For each index discharge, construct a sequence of the patient's last 12 months of encounters (limited to ~20 encounters to manage sequence length). Each encounter is encoded as a vector containing: admission and discharge dates, LOS, service line (one-hot), admission source, disposition, primary and count of secondary diagnoses (concatenated ICD-10 codes or Elixhauser category embeddings), medication orders (aggregated to ATC level 3, one-hot or frequency embedding), lab tests ordered, and admission/discharge vitals (heart rate, blood pressure, temperature). For labs, extract the most recent value of key analytes (creatinine, hemoglobin, glucose, sodium) before discharge, standardized to training-set mean and standard deviation. Encounter sequences are zero-padded to a fixed length. All encodings and scaling are fit on the training set only.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Sequence model:** A recurrent neural network (LSTM or GRU) with attention, or a Transformer encoder. The architecture includes: (1) embedding layer (encodes categorical diagnoses and medications as dense vectors, fit on training data); (2) two to three LSTM/Transformer layers (128–256 hidden units); (3) attention mechanism to weight encounters by relevance; (4) final dense layer with sigmoid output. Class weighting inversely proportional to prevalence is applied during training to address the imbalanced label. Dropout and L2 regularization are applied to prevent overfitting.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on the static feature set. This baseline represents current clinical practice—risk scores based on demographics, comorbidities, and last-encounter information. The baseline receives equal tuning effort: hyperparameter search (L2 regularization strength) is conducted on the validation set using the same metric (AUPRC). Both models are trained on the training set and selected based on validation performance.

This comparison isolates the value of longitudinal sequence information (encounter history, trends) versus static snapshot features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC) on the test set. AUPRC is chosen because readmission is a relatively low-base-rate outcome, and the operational use case is targeted intervention rather than uniform treatment across all discharges. Case management teams will act on a limited high-risk subset, so the key question is whether the model concentrates true readmissions near the top of the score distribution, where follow-up resources are deployed.

**Secondary metrics:** 
- AUROC to quantify overall discrimination across the full score range.
- Calibration plots and Brier score to ensure predicted probabilities align with observed rates.
- Sensitivity and specificity at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., threshold for flagging top 20% as high-risk).
- Stratified performance by service line, discharge disposition, and prior admission count to detect subgroup effects and ensure improvements are not driven by confounding.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set (months 25–30) using AUPRC as the criterion. A grid search explores hidden units, dropout, learning rate, and class weight parameters. The configuration achieving the highest validation AUPRC is locked. The test set is evaluated once, after all modeling decisions are finalized. No test-set data is used during development.

### 7. Controlling for Confounds

The sequence model's gains could stem from legitimate longitudinal risk signals or from exploiting recency bias and unmeasured confounding. Four controls are implemented:

1. **Stratified analysis:** Metrics are reported separately for service lines, discharge dispositions, and subgroups defined by prior admission count and LOS. If gains concentrate in a single stratum, this suggests confounding rather than generalizable signal.

2. **Feature importance inspection:** SHAP or permutation importance is used to examine whether predictions are driven by recent admission intensity (a confounder) or by earlier longitudinal trends (a signal of true risk).

3. **Sensitivity analysis on matched cohorts:** A secondary analysis matches patients on LOS, prior admission count, and service line, then compares sequence model versus logistic regression performance within matched strata. Persistent improvement after matching would strengthen the causal interpretation.

4. **Scope statement:** The experiment tests whether the sequence model discriminates better on the test set, not whether it is causal or clinically effective. Clinical utility would require prospective validation with intervention.

**Expected outcome:** The hypothesis is supported if the sequence model achieves a meaningful improvement in test AUPRC over the static baseline, with consistent gains across key strata and without evidence of recency bias in feature importance.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric (AUPRC) does not align with the central claim of the hypothesis, which emphasizes overall prediction improvement using longitudinal sequence data. While AUPRC focuses on performance at the top of the risk distribution, the hypothesis and baseline model are framed around overall discrimination, requiring a ranking-based metric like AUROC. Changing the primary metric midstream makes the evaluation sensitive to outcome prevalence and shifts focus from general risk stratification to a narrow top-rank subset without adjusting other design elements accordingly.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of a temporal split (rather than random or grouped cross-validation) introduces unnecessary bias by limiting available data for training and validation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: A temporal split is methodologically required for this hypothesis because the model must predict future admissions based on past data. Using a random or grouped split would cause temporal leakage, allowing the model to learn from future encounters and inflating performance estimates. Temporal integrity is essential for deployment validity and reflects real-world inference conditions.

must_not_claim.claim: The static feature baseline is too weak because it lacks trend information, making the comparison unfair.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline is correctly designed to reflect current clinical practice—a static risk model using data available at discharge—and ensures the comparison isolates the added value of longitudinal sequences beyond what is already used in practice. Making the baseline too complex would conflate model architecture with feature content.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the choice of AUPRC as the primary metric misaligns with the stated hypothesis and undermines the comparative framing of the experiment. A valid defense cannot justify this mismatch, as the hypothesis is not specifically about targeted top-k enrichment but general improvement in readmission prediction.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that the operational goal (targeted intervention on high-risk patients) does not redefine the hypothesis, and that shifting to AUPRC without harmonizing the expected outcome (e.g., specific thresholds, precision goals) or rebalancing the baseline model tuning constitutes methodological inconsistency.

- **Test (months 31–36 or to end of data):** Final held-out evaluation set. Represents future admissions.

This split prevents information leakage: no training data comes after any validation or test index discharge, and sequence models cannot exploit future utilization patterns. It reflects the deployment setting—the model will be applied to new discharges and will not have access to readmission outcomes at inference time. If the health system operates multiple sites, the test set is stratified by site to ensure representativeness.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Static baseline features** (for the logistic regression baseline): age at admission, sex, insurance category, Elixhauser comorbidity count and indicators (CHF, COPD, diabetes, renal disease, MI), admission source, discharge disposition (home, SNF, rehab), primary service line, length of stay (hours), count of admissions in prior 6 months, count of admissions in prior 12 months, prior ER visit count. **Longitudinal sequence features** (for the sequence model): For each index discharge, construct a sequence of the patient's last 12 months of encounters (limited to ~20 encounters to manage sequence length). Each encounter is encoded as a vector containing: admission and discharge dates, LOS, service line (one-hot), admission source, disposition, primary and count of secondary diagnoses (concatenated ICD-10 codes or Elixhauser category embeddings), medication orders (aggregated to ATC level 3, one-hot or frequency embedding), lab tests ordered, and admission/discharge vitals (heart rate, blood pressure, temperature). For labs, extract the most recent value of key analytes (creatinine, hemoglobin, glucose, sodium) before discharge, standardized to training-set mean and standard deviation. Encounter sequences are zero-padded to a fixed length. All encodings and scaling are fit on the training set only.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Sequence model:** A recurrent neural network (LSTM or GRU) with attention, or a Transformer encoder. The architecture includes: (1) embedding layer (encodes categorical diagnoses and medications as dense vectors, fit on training data); (2) two to three LSTM/Transformer layers (128–256 hidden units); (3) attention mechanism to weight encounters by relevance; (4) final dense layer with sigmoid output. Class weighting inversely proportional to prevalence is applied during training to address the imbalanced label. Dropout and L2 regularization are applied to prevent overfitting.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on the static feature set. This baseline represents current clinical practice—risk scores based on demographics, comorbidities, and last-encounter information. The baseline receives equal tuning effort: hyperparameter search (L2 regularization strength) is conducted on the validation set using the same metric (AUROC). Both models are trained on the training set and selected based on validation performance.

This comparison isolates the value of longitudinal sequence information (encounter history, trends) versus static snapshot features.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC) on the test set. AUROC is chosen because readmission is an imbalanced outcome (typically 10–20% base rate), and the hospital needs a threshold-independent measure of discrimination to inform triage and resource allocation. AUROC reflects how well the model ranks high-risk patients above low-risk patients, which is the operational goal.

**Secondary metrics:** 
- Precision-Recall AUC (AUPRC) to focus on positive class performance when base rate is low.
- Calibration plots and Brier score to ensure predicted probabilities align with observed rates.
- Sensitivity and specificity at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., threshold for flagging top 20% as high-risk).
- Stratified performance by service line, discharge disposition, and prior admission count to detect subgroup effects and ensure improvements are not driven by confounding.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set (months 25–30) using AUROC as the criterion. A grid search explores hidden units, dropout, learning rate, and class weight parameters. The configuration achieving the highest validation AUROC is locked. The test set is evaluated once, after all modeling decisions are finalized. No test-set data is used during development.

The sequence model's gains could stem from legitimate longitudinal risk signals or from exploiting recency bias and unmeasured confounding. Four controls are implemented:

1. **Stratified analysis:** Metrics are reported separately for service lines, discharge dispositions, and subgroups defined by prior admission count and LOS. If gains concentrate in a single stratum, this suggests confounding rather than generalizable signal.

2. **Feature importance inspection:** SHAP or permutation importance is used to examine whether predictions are driven by recent admission intensity (a confounder) or by earlier longitudinal trends (a signal of true risk).

3. **Sensitivity analysis on matched cohorts:** A secondary analysis matches patients on LOS, prior admission count, and service line, then compares sequence model versus logistic regression performance within matched strata. Persistent improvement after matching would strengthen the causal interpretation.

4. **Scope statement:** The experiment tests whether the sequence model discriminates better on the test set, not whether it is causal or clinically effective. Clinical utility would require prospective validation with intervention.

**Expected outcome:** The hypothesis is supported if the sequence model achieves ≥5–10% absolute improvement in test AUROC over the static baseline, with consistent gains across key strata and without evidence of recency bias in feature importance.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because the corruption is subtle—AUPRC is a valid and often preferred metric in imbalanced settings—but the misalignment with the hypothesis intent and existing model comparison framework makes it a methodological flaw. Evaluators must recognize that metric choice shapes the interpretation of success and that shifting it without redefining the hypothesis or rebalancing the baseline validation creates a validity gap.

hypothesis: A transformer-based model that jointly encodes a patient's longitudinal EHR sequence and prior medication fill history will predict 30-day hospital readmission better than a baseline logistic regression model using only the latest-visit structured features, for adult general-medicine discharges.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML — inpatient readmission prediction from electronic health records and pharmacy claims
task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Logistic Regression for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

## 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses a retrospective cohort of adult inpatient discharges from a single integrated delivery network over 36 months. The index event is discharge from a general medicine service; the outcome is any unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days, fully observed by day 31. The dataset includes encounter-level EHR data (demographics, diagnoses, procedures, labs, vitals) spanning 24 months prior to each discharge, plus outpatient pharmacy fill records for the same lookback window.

Known data quality issues are addressed as follows: missing lab values and vital signs are flagged during preprocessing and handled via imputation (fit on training data only); medication fills are right-censored at the health system boundary, meaning fills at outside pharmacies are unobserved. This missingness is deliberate and realistic; the experiment stratifies results by pharmacy data completeness to ensure the model's benefit does not rest entirely on implicit handling of missing data.

## 2. Split Strategy

A **temporal (chronological) split** is used to prevent leakage and preserve the causal structure of the data. Discharges are ordered by date and divided as follows:
- **Training set (months 1–24):** 70% of the time window; all discharges and their associated histories
- **Validation set (months 25–30):** 15% of the time window; used for hyperparameter selection
- **Test set (months 31–36):** 15% of the time window; held out until final evaluation

Justification: Longitudinal EHR sequences exhibit strong temporal autocorrelation and seasonality. A random split would allow the model to learn patterns from month 35 when training on month 5 discharges, introducing leakage. Conversely, a chronological split ensures that all temporal patterns in the training set temporally precede those in validation and test, mimicking the deployment scenario where predictions are made at discharge using only historical information. A 1-month burn-in buffer between train and validation, and another between validation and test, reduces contamination from overlapping patient cohorts and allows pharmacy claims to settle.

## 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, encoding) are **fit on the training set only** using information available at or before each patient's discharge date. They are then applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents indirect leakage of label information and future missingness patterns into validation and test.

Features are organized in three groups:
1. **Longitudinal EHR sequence:** All encounters in the 24 months prior to discharge (visits, labs, procedures, vitals), encoded as timestamped event sequences with learned embeddings and absolute positional encodings.
2. **Medication fill history:** All outpatient fills in the prior 24 months, encoded as a timestamped sequence.
3. **Structured pre-discharge features:** Demographics (age, sex, insurance), Charlson comorbidity index, admission and ED visit counts (prior 12 months), length of stay, discharge disposition, primary diagnosis category, and constructed features (time since last admission, medication count in prior 90 days, high-risk medication class indicators).

No feature uses post-discharge information (e.g., post-discharge fills or readmission dates are held out by design).

## 4. Models and Baseline

**Transformer Model:** A dual-encoder architecture with separate transformer encoders for the EHR sequence and medication sequence. Each encoder uses multi-head self-attention (8 heads, 2 transformer layers, 256 hidden dimensions) with residual connections and layer normalization. The concatenated representations pass through a 2-layer MLP (256 → 128 → 1) with ReLU, dropout (0.2), and binary cross-entropy loss. The model is trained end-to-end.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on structured features only (demographics, Charlson index, prior admission/ED counts, LOS, discharge disposition, primary diagnosis). The baseline receives **equivalent tuning effort**: hyperparameter search for L2 regularization strength via grid search on the validation set, class-weight balancing, and identical train/val/test splits. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of sequence modeling, not tuning disparity.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area under the ROC curve (AUROC). The experiment's core question is whether the transformer produces better patient-level risk ordering than a structured linear model across the full discharged population, independent of any one outreach-capacity assumption. AUROC evaluates pairwise ranking quality over all possible thresholds and is less sensitive to month-to-month variation in readmission prevalence, which is important under temporal holdout.

**Secondary metrics:**
- AUPRC
- Sensitivity and specificity at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., threshold targeting 25% outreach)
- Calibration slope and intercept (validation set)
- Decision curve analysis (net benefit vs. baseline and treat-all strategies)
- Performance stratified by pharmacy data completeness (to validate that the transformer's benefit is not driven solely by implicit imputation of missing fills)

## 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both transformer and baseline are tuned via grid search on the validation set (months 25–30), selecting the model that maximizes AUROC on validation predictions. The validation set is used once for this purpose. After tuning is complete, the test set (months 31–36) is held out and touched only once: all reported metrics are computed on test set predictions with no further retraining or threshold adjustment.

## 7. Controls for Confounds

**Temporal leakage:** Prevented by chronological split; future admission trends and seasonality cannot influence training data.

**Differential missingness in pharmacy data:** The experiment stratifies test results by whether pharmacy data is available vs. missing/censored. If the transformer's advantage is entirely in the missing-data group, this indicates that the benefit may be due to implicit handling of missingness rather than sequence modeling, qualifying the claim.

**History vs. architecture:** The baseline receives all available structured features from the 24-month lookback window, not just the latest visit. The transformer's marginal gain over this history-aware baseline isolates the value of sequence encoding.

**Reproducibility and bias prevention:** Random seeds are fixed; all code is version-controlled; the experiment design is registered prior to execution to prevent p-hacking.

**Scope:** The experiment establishes predictive discrimination in this health system's population during the test period. It does not establish generalization to other systems, other populations, or prospective clinical deployment. Impact on actual readmission rates is a separate RCT question.

planted_issues.description: The primary evaluation metric is AUROC, which assesses a model's ability to rank all patients correctly across all decision thresholds. However, in the operational setting of limited outreach capacity (~20–30% of patients), precision in the high-risk tail matters more than global ranking. A model can have high AUROC but still perform poorly in the top 25% of predicted risk, which is where interventions would be targeted. Using AUROC as the primary metric may mask a failure to improve actionable prioritization.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model is flawed because it only uses features from the latest visit, not the full 24-month history.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline correctly includes structured features from the full 24-month lookback window (e.g., admission counts in prior 12 months, Charlson index, time since last admission), not just the latest visit. This ensures the comparison isolates the benefit of sequence modeling, not access to richer historical data.

must_not_claim.claim: The validation set overlaps in time with the test set or is used for training cleanup, risking contamination.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The temporal split uses a 1-month burn-in buffer between validation (months 25–30) and test (months 31–36), ensuring no temporal overlap. Patient histories are truncated at discharge, so future events do not leak into past data.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that AUROC is not the most decision-relevant metric given constrained outreach capacity and argues that AUPRC should be the primary metric to reflect precision in the high-risk group. The final verdict concludes that the design must be revised to use AUPRC as the primary metric.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates understanding that operational impact depends on performance in the top risk deciles and that AUROC can be misleading when the positive class is rare and interventions are limited.

sound_design_reference:
# Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Logistic Regression for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

## 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses a retrospective cohort of adult inpatient discharges from a single integrated delivery network over 36 months. The index event is discharge from a general medicine service; the outcome is any unplanned hospital readmission within 30 days, fully observed by day 31. The dataset includes encounter-level EHR data (demographics, diagnoses, procedures, labs, vitals) spanning 24 months prior to each discharge, plus outpatient pharmacy fill records for the same lookback window.

Known data quality issues are addressed as follows: missing lab values and vital signs are flagged during preprocessing and handled via imputation (fit on training data only); medication fills are right-censored at the health system boundary, meaning fills at outside pharmacies are unobserved. This missingness is deliberate and realistic; the experiment stratifies results by pharmacy data completeness to ensure the model's benefit does not rest entirely on implicit handling of missing data.

## 2. Split Strategy

A **temporal (chronological) split** is used to prevent leakage and preserve the causal structure of the data. Discharges are ordered by date and divided as follows:
- **Training set (months 1–24):** 70% of the time window; all discharges and their associated histories
- **Validation set (months 25–30):** 15% of the time window; used for hyperparameter selection
- **Test set (months 31–36):** 15% of the time window; held out until final evaluation

Justification: Longitudinal EHR sequences exhibit strong temporal autocorrelation and seasonality. A random split would allow the model to learn patterns from month 35 when training on month 5 discharges, introducing leakage. Conversely, a chronological split ensures that all temporal patterns in the training set temporally precede those in validation and test, mimicking the deployment scenario where predictions are made at discharge using only historical information. A 1-month burn-in buffer between train and validation, and another between validation and test, reduces contamination from overlapping patient cohorts and allows pharmacy claims to settle.

## 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, imputation, encoding) are **fit on the training set only** using information available at or before each patient's discharge date. They are then applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. This prevents indirect leakage of label information and future missingness patterns into validation and test.

Features are organized in three groups:
1. **Longitudinal EHR sequence:** All encounters in the 24 months prior to discharge (visits, labs, procedures, vitals), encoded as timestamped event sequences with learned embeddings and absolute positional encodings.
2. **Medication fill history:** All outpatient fills in the prior 24 months, encoded as a timestamped sequence.
3. **Structured pre-discharge features:** Demographics (age, sex, insurance), Charlson comorbidity index, admission and ED visit counts (prior 12 months), length of stay, discharge disposition, primary diagnosis category, and constructed features (time since last admission, medication count in prior 90 days, high-risk medication class indicators).

No feature uses post-discharge information (e.g., post-discharge fills or readmission dates are held out by design).

## 4. Models and Baseline

**Transformer Model:** A dual-encoder architecture with separate transformer encoders for the EHR sequence and medication sequence. Each encoder uses multi-head self-attention (8 heads, 2 transformer layers, 256 hidden dimensions) with residual connections and layer normalization. The concatenated representations pass through a 2-layer MLP (256 → 128 → 1) with ReLU, dropout (0.2), and binary cross-entropy loss. The model is trained end-to-end.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression trained on structured features only (demographics, Charlson index, prior admission/ED counts, LOS, discharge disposition, primary diagnosis). The baseline receives **equivalent tuning effort**: hyperparameter search for L2 regularization strength via grid search on the validation set, class-weight balancing, and identical train/val/test splits. This ensures the comparison isolates the value of sequence modeling, not tuning disparity.

## 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Area under the precision-recall curve (AUPRC). This is the correct metric for the deployment context: care managers have limited outreach capacity (~20–30% of cohort). AUPRC measures how well the model identifies true readmissions (recall) while keeping false alarms low (precision) across decision thresholds, enabling data-driven threshold selection. AUPRC is robust to class imbalance and does not require choosing a single operating point a priori.

**Secondary metrics:**
- AUROC (discrimination across thresholds)
- Sensitivity and specificity at clinically relevant thresholds (e.g., threshold targeting 25% outreach)
- Calibration slope and intercept (validation set)
- Decision curve analysis (net benefit vs. baseline and treat-all strategies)
- Performance stratified by pharmacy data completeness (to validate that the transformer's benefit is not driven solely by implicit imputation of missing fills)

## 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both transformer and baseline are tuned via grid search on the validation set (months 25–30), selecting the model that maximizes AUPRC on validation predictions. The validation set is used once for this purpose. After tuning is complete, the test set (months 31–36) is held out and touched only once: all reported metrics are computed on test set predictions with no further retraining or threshold adjustment.

## 7. Controls for Confounds

**Temporal leakage:** Prevented by chronological split; future admission trends and seasonality cannot influence training data.

**Differential missingness in pharmacy data:** The experiment stratifies test results by whether pharmacy data is available vs. missing/censored. If the transformer's advantage is entirely in the missing-data group, this indicates that the benefit may be due to implicit handling of missingness rather than sequence modeling, qualifying the claim.

**History vs. architecture:** The baseline receives all available structured features from the 24-month lookback window, not just the latest visit. The transformer's marginal gain over this history-aware baseline isolates the value of sequence encoding.

**Reproducibility and bias prevention:** Random seeds are fixed; all code is version-controlled; the experiment design is registered prior to execution to prevent p-hacking.

**Scope:** The experiment establishes predictive discrimination in this health system's population during the test period. It does not establish generalization to other systems, other populations, or prospective clinical deployment. Impact on actual readmission rates is a separate RCT question.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because AUROC is commonly used and appears reasonable at first glance; detecting the mismatch requires understanding the operational constraints of limited outreach capacity and recognizing that AUPRC, not AUROC, aligns with the goal of prioritizing a small, high-risk subgroup.

hypothesis: For hospital readmission prediction, a model that combines longitudinal EHR event sequences with note embeddings will outperform a model trained only on structured tabular variables in predicting 30-day unplanned readmission, especially for patients with complex multimorbidity.
domain: Acute care hospital electronic health records (EHR) from a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Longitudinal EHR Embeddings for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses de-identified inpatient encounter records from a multi-hospital health system spanning 36 consecutive months. We include all admissions with complete discharge summaries, progress notes, and structured EHR data (diagnoses, procedures, medications, labs, vitals). Admissions with missing data >5% or length of stay <24 hours are excluded because they represent a different clinical population with distinct readmission drivers.

The primary outcome is binary: unplanned readmission to any hospital within the system within 30 days post-discharge. Planned readmissions are identified and excluded using admission source codes and pre-scheduled procedure flags. Outcome status is ascertained from administrative records at least 60 days after discharge to ensure complete follow-up.

**Expected sample size:** 80,000–150,000 admissions, depending on system size. This provides sufficient power to detect a 0.03 absolute AUROC difference (approximately 90% statistical power at α=0.05) and allows stratified analyses within subgroups.

### 2. Train–Validation–Test Split: Temporal and Patient-Stratified

To prevent leakage of future information into training and to reflect the deployment setting (predicting for unseen patients), we use a **chronological split with patient-level stratification**:

- **Training set (months 1–18, ~54%):** All encounters in the first 18 months.
- **Validation set (months 19–24, ~27%):** Encounters in months 19–24, used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection.
- **Test set (months 25–36, ~27%):** Held-out encounters in months 25–36; untouched until final evaluation.

Critically, no single patient's data appears across splits. Encounters for each patient are assigned entirely to one set based on the patient's first admission date. This ensures the model encounters genuinely new patients at validation and test time, preventing inflated performance from learning patient-specific readmission patterns.

This approach avoids three sources of leakage: (a) using post-discharge information, (b) random shuffling that mixes past and future, and (c) patient-level data leakage across train/test.

### 3. Features and Preprocessing

**Structured tabular features** (used in both baseline and primary model):
- Demographics: age, sex
- Admission characteristics: type, source, disposition
- Diagnoses: primary ICD code, top 20 secondary diagnoses (indicators)
- Procedures: high-risk procedure indicators
- Comorbidities: Elixhauser condition flags
- Lab values: admission labs (hemoglobin, creatinine, glucose) and prior 12-month statistics (min, max, most recent)
- Vitals: admission HR, BP, temperature
- Length of stay
- Prior utilization: ED visits, admissions, and readmissions in prior 12 months
- Documentation: note length (word count) and hospital ID
- Transition-of-care context: a post-discharge contact intensity feature summarizing completed care-management outreach, scheduling interactions, and documented follow-up coordination within 7 days of discharge

**Longitudinal sequences and note embeddings** (primary model only):
- Event sequences: all timestamped medication administrations, lab orders, and procedures during admission, represented as (event_type, code, timestamp, value) tuples.
- Note embeddings: discharge summaries and progress notes are concatenated, preprocessed (lowercased, PHI removed, common phrases filtered), and embedded using a pre-trained clinical language model (e.g., ClinicalBERT) to produce fixed-dimensional vectors (768 dimensions).

**Preprocessing workflow:**
All scaling, categorical encoding, and tokenization transformers are fit **only on the training set**. The fitted transformers are then applied (without re-fitting) to the validation and test sets. Pre-trained clinical embeddings are applied uniformly across all splits. This prevents validation and test statistics from leaking into the training process.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

**Primary model:** A hybrid neural architecture combines:
1. An LSTM or Transformer encoder that processes the longitudinal event sequence, producing a learned representation of the admission's clinical trajectory.
2. Concatenation of: the sequence encoding, the note embedding vector, and the structured tabular features.
3. A 2-layer fully connected network (128→64 hidden units, ReLU, 0.3 dropout) outputting a sigmoid probability of 30-day readmission.
4. Training uses binary cross-entropy loss with inverse class-frequency weighting to account for class imbalance (~15% base readmission rate, typical in acute care).

**Baseline model:** Logistic regression (or XGBoost) trained on structured tabular features only—no sequences, no note embeddings. This represents current clinical practice. The baseline receives **equivalent tuning effort**: hyperparameters (regularization, learning rate, tree depth) are tuned on the validation set using the same random search budget and cross-validation strategy.

This ensures a fair comparison: the baseline is not handicapped by insufficient tuning, and the primary model's advantage can be attributed to the additional longitudinal and textual signals.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both models are tuned on the validation set (months 19–24) via 5-fold cross-validation, stratified by readmission status and patient ID. For the primary model, we search over: LSTM hidden sizes (64, 128, 256), learning rate (1e-4, 1e-3, 5e-3), dropout (0.2, 0.3, 0.5), batch size (32, 64), and embedding pooling strategy (mean, max, attention). For the baseline, we search over: regularization strength (L1/L2), regularization type, and (for XGBoost) max depth, learning rate, and number of boosters.

The configuration with the highest mean cross-validated AUROC on the validation set is selected. For neural models, early stopping monitors validation AUROC; training halts if no improvement occurs for 10 consecutive epochs.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: AUROC on the test set.**
- **Justification:** AUROC is threshold-independent and appropriate for imbalanced classification. It directly measures the model's ability to rank patients by risk—the core clinical goal. Clinically, a 0.03 absolute AUROC improvement (e.g., 0.72 → 0.75) is considered meaningful.

**Secondary metrics (test set):**
- **Precision–Recall AUC:** Sensitive to minority-class performance; reflects the model's value for identifying true readmission cases.
- **Calibration (Expected Calibration Error, ECE):** Measures whether predicted probabilities align with observed rates across risk deciles. Poor calibration can mislead clinical decision-making.
- **Sensitivity and specificity at the 90th percentile risk threshold:** Operationalizes the clinical use case: identifying the highest-risk decile and assessing sensitivity (how many readmissions are caught) and specificity (false positive rate).
- **AUROC stratified by multimorbidity (Elixhauser ≥4 vs. <4):** Tests the hypothesis's prediction that improvements are largest in complex patients.

All test-set metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap (1000 resamples, stratified by readmission status).

### 7. Confound Control and Sensitivity Analyses

Three key confounds are addressed:

1. **Documentation richness:** Note length (word count) and hospital ID are included as explicit features in both models. If the primary model's gain persists after controlling for these, the improvement reflects clinical signal, not documentation artifacts. We additionally stratify by note length to verify the primary result holds across documentation intensities.

2. **Discharge practices and prior utilization:** Prior 12-month admissions, ED visits, and readmissions are included in both models. We perform a sensitivity analysis stratifying by prior utilization (high vs. low) to verify the result is not solely driven by informative prior admission history.

3. **Hospital-specific coding and documentation style:** Hospital ID is included as a feature, and we report AUROC stratified by individual hospital. Consistent improvements across hospitals suggest generalizable signal; localized improvements suggest site-specific confounding.

### 8. Scope of Claims

This experiment will establish whether a model combining longitudinal EHR sequences and note embeddings achieves higher AUROC than structured-only prediction on this health system's data. It does not establish: (a) whether this improvement generalizes to other health systems (external validity would require separate external validation), (b) whether the model actually improves clinical outcomes (e.g., readmission rates post-deployment), or (c) which specific clinical signals in the sequences or notes drive the prediction (interpretability analysis would be a separate effort). The experiment isolates the predictive value of additional data modalities under controlled conditions and is a necessary precursor to clinical deployment.

planted_issues.description: The model includes a 'post-discharge contact intensity' feature that captures care-management follow-up activities occurring within 7 days of discharge. Because these contacts occur *after* discharge but *within* the 30-day readmission risk window, this feature may encode information about the patient's early deterioration or impending readmission that would not be available at discharge when the model is meant to make predictions. Including such future-informed features leads to target leakage, inflating performance estimates and invalidating the realism of a discharge-time deployment scenario.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model does not use note embeddings or longitudinal sequences, giving it an artificial disadvantage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The stated hypothesis specifically compares a model that includes these advanced modalities to current clinical practice (structured-only models). The baseline is intentionally simpler to reflect real-world deployment, and equal tuning effort ensures a fair test of whether added complexity is justified.

must_not_claim.claim: The train/validation/test temporal split is unbalanced because the training set covers 18 months while each of the other sets covers only 6 months.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The temporal split is appropriate for operational simulation: models are trained on early data and evaluated on future admissions. Unequal time spans are common and acceptable if they align with deployment timelines and avoid leakage.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A correct critique identifies the 'post-discharge contact intensity' feature as a source of target leakage that invalidates the prediction task's temporal assumptions, and the defender cannot plausibly justify its use in a discharge-time prediction model.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that the contact intensity feature is measured after discharge but before 30-day readmission status is determined, and therefore introduces a risk of data leakage from downstream clinical events.

## Experiment Design: Longitudinal EHR Embeddings for 30-Day Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Collection and Labeling

The experiment uses de-identified inpatient encounter records from a multi-hospital health system spanning 36 consecutive months. We include all admissions with complete discharge summaries, progress notes, and structured EHR data (diagnoses, procedures, medications, labs, vitals). Admissions with missing data >5% or length of stay <24 hours are excluded because they represent a different clinical population with distinct readmission drivers.

The primary outcome is binary: unplanned readmission to any hospital within the system within 30 days post-discharge. Planned readmissions are identified and excluded using admission source codes and pre-scheduled procedure flags. Outcome status is ascertained from administrative records at least 60 days after discharge to ensure complete follow-up.

**Expected sample size:** 80,000–150,000 admissions, depending on system size. This provides sufficient power to detect a 0.03 absolute AUROC difference (approximately 90% statistical power at α=0.05) and allows stratified analyses within subgroups.

### 2. Train–Validation–Test Split: Temporal and Patient-Stratified

To prevent leakage of future information into training and to reflect the deployment setting (predicting for unseen patients), we use a **chronological split with patient-level stratification**:

- **Training set (months 1–18, ~54%):** All encounters in the first 18 months.
- **Validation set (months 19–24, ~27%):** Encounters in months 19–24, used for hyperparameter tuning and model selection.
- **Test set (months 25–36, ~27%):** Held-out encounters in months 25–36; untouched until final evaluation.

Critically, no single patient's data appears across splits. Encounters for each patient are assigned entirely to one set based on the patient's first admission date. This ensures the model encounters genuinely new patients at validation and test time, preventing inflated performance from learning patient-specific readmission patterns.

This approach avoids three sources of leakage: (a) using post-discharge information, (b) random shuffling that mixes past and future, and (c) patient-level data leakage across train/test.

### 3. Features and Preprocessing

**Structured tabular features** (used in both baseline and primary model):
- Demographics: age, sex
- Admission characteristics: type, source, disposition
- Diagnoses: primary ICD code, top 20 secondary diagnoses (indicators)
- Procedures: high-risk procedure indicators
- Comorbidities: Elixhauser condition flags
- Lab values: admission labs (hemoglobin, creatinine, glucose) and prior 12-month statistics (min, max, most recent)
- Vitals: admission HR, BP, temperature
- Length of stay
- Prior utilization: ED visits, admissions, and readmissions in prior 12 months
- Documentation: note length (word count) and hospital ID

**Longitudinal sequences and note embeddings** (primary model only):
- Event sequences: all timestamped medication administrations, lab orders, and procedures during admission, represented as (event_type, code, timestamp, value) tuples.
- Note embeddings: discharge summaries and progress notes are concatenated, preprocessed (lowercased, PHI removed, common phrases filtered), and embedded using a pre-trained clinical language model (e.g., ClinicalBERT) to produce fixed-dimensional vectors (768 dimensions).

**Preprocessing workflow:**
All scaling, categorical encoding, and tokenization transformers are fit **only on the training set**. The fitted transformers are then applied (without re-fitting) to the validation and test sets. Pre-trained clinical embeddings are applied uniformly across all splits. This prevents validation and test statistics from leaking into the training process.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

**Primary model:** A hybrid neural architecture combines:
1. An LSTM or Transformer encoder that processes the longitudinal event sequence, producing a learned representation of the admission's clinical trajectory.
2. Concatenation of: the sequence encoding, the note embedding vector, and the structured tabular features.
3. A 2-layer fully connected network (128→64 hidden units, ReLU, 0.3 dropout) outputting a sigmoid probability of 30-day readmission.
4. Training uses binary cross-entropy loss with inverse class-frequency weighting to account for class imbalance (~15% base readmission rate, typical in acute care).

**Baseline model:** Logistic regression (or XGBoost) trained on structured tabular features only—no sequences, no note embeddings. This represents current clinical practice. The baseline receives **equivalent tuning effort**: hyperparameters (regularization, learning rate, tree depth) are tuned on the validation set using the same random search budget and cross-validation strategy.

This ensures a fair comparison: the baseline is not handicapped by insufficient tuning, and the primary model's advantage can be attributed to the additional longitudinal and textual signals.

### 5. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both models are tuned on the validation set (months 19–24) via 5-fold cross-validation, stratified by readmission status and patient ID. For the primary model, we search over: LSTM hidden sizes (64, 128, 256), learning rate (1e-4, 1e-3, 5e-3), dropout (0.2, 0.3, 0.5), batch size (32, 64), and embedding pooling strategy (mean, max, attention). For the baseline, we search over: regularization strength (L1/L2), regularization type, and (for XGBoost) max depth, learning rate, and number of boosters.

The configuration with the highest mean cross-validated AUROC on the validation set is selected. For neural models, early stopping monitors validation AUROC; training halts if no improvement occurs for 10 consecutive epochs.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: AUROC on the test set.**
- **Justification:** AUROC is threshold-independent and appropriate for imbalanced classification. It directly measures the model's ability to rank patients by risk—the core clinical goal. Clinically, a 0.03 absolute AUROC improvement (e.g., 0.72 → 0.75) is considered meaningful.

**Secondary metrics (test set):**
- **Precision–Recall AUC:** Sensitive to minority-class performance; reflects the model's value for identifying true readmission cases.
- **Calibration (Expected Calibration Error, ECE):** Measures whether predicted probabilities align with observed rates across risk deciles. Poor calibration can mislead clinical decision-making.
- **Sensitivity and specificity at the 90th percentile risk threshold:** Operationalizes the clinical use case: identifying the highest-risk decile and assessing sensitivity (how many readmissions are caught) and specificity (false positive rate).
- **AUROC stratified by multimorbidity (Elixhauser ≥4 vs. <4):** Tests the hypothesis's prediction that improvements are largest in complex patients.

All test-set metrics are reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap (1000 resamples, stratified by readmission status).

### 7. Confound Control and Sensitivity Analyses

Three key confounds are addressed:

1. **Documentation richness:** Note length (word count) and hospital ID are included as explicit features in both models. If the primary model's gain persists after controlling for these, the improvement reflects clinical signal, not documentation artifacts. We additionally stratify by note length to verify the primary result holds across documentation intensities.

2. **Discharge practices and prior utilization:** Prior 12-month admissions, ED visits, and readmissions are included in both models. We perform a sensitivity analysis stratifying by prior utilization (high vs. low) to verify the result is not solely driven by informative prior admission history.

3. **Hospital-specific coding and documentation style:** Hospital ID is included as a feature, and we report AUROC stratified by individual hospital. Consistent improvements across hospitals suggest generalizable signal; localized improvements suggest site-specific confounding.

### 8. Scope of Claims

This experiment will establish whether a model combining longitudinal EHR sequences and note embeddings achieves higher AUROC than structured-only prediction on this health system's data. It does not establish: (a) whether this improvement generalizes to other health systems (external validity would require separate external validation), (b) whether the model actually improves clinical outcomes (e.g., readmission rates post-deployment), or (c) which specific clinical signals in the sequences or notes drive the prediction (interpretability analysis would be a separate effort). The experiment isolates the predictive value of additional data modalities under controlled conditions and is a necessary precursor to clinical deployment.
difficulty_justification: The flaw is subtle because the post-discharge contact feature appears logistically available and clinically plausible, but its temporal alignment with the outcome window makes it a source of target leakage. Correct evaluation requires a nuanced understanding of discharge-time prediction constraints and causal validity, not just methodological checklist adherence.

hypothesis: A transformer encoder trained on raw longitudinal EHR sequences will predict 30-day hospital readmission better than a gradient-boosted model using only hand-engineered summary features, when both are evaluated on a temporally held-out cohort of adult inpatient discharges.
domain: Hospital readmission prediction using electronic health records from a large multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient Boosting for 30-Day Hospital Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 4 years of inpatient discharge records from a large multi-hospital health system EHR (2019–2022). The cohort includes adult patients (≥18 years) discharged from medical or surgical wards. Exclusions: palliative/hospice care, length of stay <24 hours, and patients who died within 30 days post-discharge. Expected final cohort size: 80,000–120,000 discharges.

The binary label is defined as any acute inpatient readmission within 30 calendar days of discharge to any facility within the health system, derived from encounter timestamps. This definition is operationally meaningful: operations teams target follow-up interventions to discharges identified as high-risk within 48–72 hours post-discharge, and 30-day readmission is a standard quality metric.

**Data quality issues:** Approximately 5–10% of true readmissions occur at out-of-network hospitals and are not captured in the EHR. This label noise affects both models equally; it is noted as a limitation but does not invalidate the comparison. Missing values in medication orders and vitals are approximately 5% (well-populated for acute stays); labs are ~80% populated depending on clinical indication. These missingness patterns are preserved in the transformer's raw sequences (marked with special tokens) and handled via forward-fill in the baseline features.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

A **chronological (temporal) split** is used to prevent leakage and to reflect prospective deployment. The rationale: readmission risk evolves over time, and the model must not access information from the validation or test window during training.

**Split proportions:**
- **Train set:** 56% of discharges (chronologically, ~2.2 years)
- **Validation set:** 14% of discharges (chronologically, discharges from months ~30–42, ~0.84 years)
- **Test set:** 30% of discharges (most recent ~0.84 years)

Within the combined train+validation pool (70% of data), a random 80/20 split is performed **stratified by discharge service** (medical vs. surgical) and **prior utilization quartile** (based on ED visits and hospitalizations in the 12 months prior to discharge). Stratification ensures both train and validation/test populations represent the deployment target and minimizes confounding by baseline acuity.

The test set is strictly temporal: it comprises the most recent 30% of discharges and is not touched until both models are fully trained and hyperparameters are finalized.

### 3. Feature Engineering

**Transformer input (raw sequences):** For each discharge, construct a chronologically ordered sequence of clinical events occurring in the 72 hours before discharge. Each event is represented as a tuple (timestamp, event_type, code, value). Event types include medications, lab orders, vitals, and diagnoses. Sequences are capped at 512 tokens (median sequence length ~150 tokens); longer sequences are truncated from the beginning. Missing values within sequences are marked with a special token; the transformer's attention mechanism attends over raw missingness patterns.

**Baseline input (hand-engineered features):** Constructed from the same 72-hour pre-discharge window plus prior 12-month history:
- **Utilization counts:** ED visits (past 12 months), prior hospitalizations (past 12 months)
- **Recency:** days since last ED visit, days since last hospitalization
- **Complexity:** count of distinct diagnoses (current admission), count of active medication classes
- **Demographics:** age, sex, discharge service (categorical)
- **Clinical summary:** mean laboratory values (creatinine, hemoglobin, glucose) in past 72 hours

Both feature sets use **identical temporal windows** to ensure fair comparison; any advantage for the transformer must stem from sequence modeling, not from access to longer or richer information.

**Preprocessing:** All transformers (StandardScaler for vitals/labs, categorical encoders, imputation strategies) are fit exclusively on the training set and applied to validation and test sets. This prevents leakage.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model:** A 4–6 layer encoder with 8 attention heads, embedding dimension 256, and feed-forward dimension 1024. Events are embedded and passed through the transformer; the final [CLS] token representation is fed to a binary classification head. Training uses Adam (learning rate 1e-4, cosine annealing), batch size 32, max 50 epochs with early stopping on validation loss.

**Baseline:** XGBoost or LightGBM trained on hand-engineered summary features. Class weights are adjusted for imbalance (weight ratio ~1:4 for negative:positive discharges at ~20% readmission base rate). The baseline receives **equivalent tuning effort** as the transformer: 20 trials of random hyperparameter search (max_depth, learning_rate, n_estimators, regularization) tuned on the validation set using AUPRC.

**Why this baseline?** XGBoost is the production standard for tabular EHR data and represents the realistic comparison—not a random forest or logistic regression, but the model a health system currently uses or would use without deep learning. It is given equal computational resources and tuning cycles, ensuring the comparison is fair.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: AUPRC on the held-out test set** (with 95% confidence interval via 1000 bootstrap resamples).

*Justification:* Operations teams rank discharges by model score and allocate follow-up resources to the top decile or top 5%. In that workflow, performance near the high-risk end of the score distribution matters more than behavior across the entire false-positive range. AUPRC emphasizes how concentrated true readmissions are among patients receiving elevated scores, which makes it a useful summary of how well the model supports a capacity-limited outreach queue. A meaningful improvement is ≥2 percentage points with non-overlapping confidence intervals.

**Secondary metrics:**
- **Precision @ 10% of population (P@10):** Among the 10% of discharges with highest model scores, what fraction are readmitted? Reflects utility at the decision boundary operations use.
- **Expected Calibration Error (ECE) and calibration curve:** Ensures model probabilities are reliable if used to set intervention intensity.
- **Stratified AUROC:** Recompute AUROC separately for surgery-only and medical-only sub-cohorts of the test set. Flags service-specific overfitting.
- **Sensitivity @ 90% specificity (TPR @ 90% TNR):** Alternate threshold-based metric preferred by some health systems.

### 6. Model Selection and Validation Approach

The validation set is used **only** for hyperparameter tuning and early stopping. Both the transformer and baseline optimize AUPRC on the validation set independently; no information from the test set informs model selection. The test set is locked and evaluated exactly once, after both models are fully trained.

No cross-validation is performed. The validation set is fixed across both models to ensure test results are independent and not biased by multiple testing.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

**Temporal control:** Train, validation, and test sets are chronologically separated, preventing future leakage and reflecting real-world deployment (the model predicts on new, future discharges).

**Matched information:** Both models access the same 72-hour pre-discharge + 12-month history window. The baseline is not disadvantaged by receiving shorter histories or fewer features; any transformer advantage must come from sequence modeling, not information asymmetry.

**Stratification:** Train/validation splits are stratified by service and prior utilization, ensuring balanced representation of patient complexity in both sets.

**Class imbalance handling:** Both models use class weights; the primary metric emphasizes concentration of positives among higher-scored discharges, which is useful under imbalanced readmission prevalence.

**Label noise:** Out-of-network readmissions (~5–10% of true readmissions) are not captured in the label; this affects both models equally and is reported as a study limitation.

**Post-hoc stratified analysis:** After test evaluation, AUROC is recomputed within strata (medical vs. surgical, high vs. low prior utilization). Large service-specific gaps would indicate the model is not generalizing broadly.

**Scope of claims:** This design establishes whether a transformer *can* outperform a gradient-boosted model in discrimination on this cohort with this label definition. It does not establish: (1) whether the model generalizes to other health systems or time periods (external validity requires separate evaluation); (2) whether improved discrimination translates to better clinical outcomes when the model is deployed (requires a randomized controlled trial); (3) whether the transformer's gain comes from learning long-range temporal dependencies vs. other sequence characteristics (requires ablation or attention analysis, which is a follow-up investigation).

planted_issues.description: The design uses AUPRC as the primary evaluation metric, which is mismatched with the operational use case and hypothesis objective. The hypothesis frames the goal as improving overall discrimination between models for risk stratification across the full risk spectrum, and the primary objective is ranking patients across all levels of risk to target a fixed top-k percentage. AUPRC is highly sensitive to class imbalance and primarily reflects performance at high-precision, low-recall ranges, making it less suitable for comparing models on broad rank discrimination. AUROC is a more appropriate metric for this objective as it measures overall separability across all thresholds.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of a temporal split causes the train and test sets to have different distributions, which invalidates the comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits are necessary and appropriate in this domain because they prevent future leakage and reflect prospective deployment; distribution shifts over time are real and expected, and the design explicitly aims to evaluate model performance under such conditions.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model is not tuned with sufficient effort, making the comparison unfair.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baseline receives 20 hyperparameter search trials with validation-based tuning, matching the effort given to the transformer, ensuring a fair comparison of model families under realistic training conditions.

must_not_claim.claim: Using P@10 as a secondary metric while using AUPRC as primary is redundant or conflicting.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: P@10 is a task-specific, thresholded metric that reflects performance at the exact operating point used by operations teams, while AUPRC evaluates performance across multiple thresholds in the precision-recall space; they serve complementary roles and are both valid under different evaluation frameworks.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that the primary metric AUPRC does not align with the stated goal of evaluating overall model discrimination for risk stratification across the cohort. The defender fails to justify why AUPRC is more appropriate than AUROC given the hypothesis and operational context, and the verdict supports the critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic argues that AUROC is superior for evaluating rank-ordering discrimination across a population, especially when the goal is to improve performance across a broad risk spectrum (e.g., top 10%) rather than optimizing for extreme precision at very high thresholds.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The defender acknowledges the metric mismatch and concedes that while AUPRC is informative for high-risk precision, it is not the best primary metric for the stated objective of overall risk stratification. The team agrees to report AUROC as primary and AUPRC as a secondary metric to better align with the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The change in metric reporting reflects a recognition that the original choice distorted the evaluation framework and could misrepresent model performance relative to the operational goal.
