## Experiment Design: GNN vs. GBT for AML Transaction Chain Detection

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses 36 months of production transaction logs from a single retail/commercial bank. Each record includes sender and receiver account IDs, timestamp, amount, channel, device fingerprint, IP address, KYC tier, counterparty identifiers, and historical alert and investigation outcomes. Using a single institution's data is deliberate: it controls for cross-institution variation in SAR filing culture and ensures the graph topology reflects one real deployment environment.

Labels are defined at the transaction level: a transaction is positive if it belongs to a cluster where any linked account filed a SAR within 90 days of the transaction date. The 90-day forward window is enforced strictly at each split boundary — no SAR outcome from beyond the window is used, and a 30-day dead zone at each boundary absorbs in-flight investigations. Transactions with no outcome within 90 days are treated as negatives, consistent with operational convention and applied uniformly to both models.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically: months 1–24 for training, months 25–27 for validation (with a 30-day dead zone at both ends), and months 28–36 for the held-out test set (with a 30-day dead zone at the entry boundary). A temporal split is mandatory here — not optional. Random or stratified splits would allow laundering ring members who appear in test to also appear in train, inflating performance estimates through relational leakage. Temporal splitting also directly tests the hypothesis as stated: does the GNN generalize better to future, unseen suspicious patterns?

Graph edges are constructed within each split window using only transactions whose timestamps fall within that window plus an allowed historical lookback. No test-period edges are materialized during training.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All preprocessing transformers — log-scaling, categorical encoders, imputation — are fit on the training set only and applied frozen to validation and test. Rolling aggregate features (e.g., 30-day transaction velocity, degree counts) are computed using only data available at or before the transaction timestamp, enforcing strict causal ordering.

Critically, the GBT baseline receives tabular versions of all relational signals the GNN processes: unique counterparty counts over 7/30/90-day windows, shared device and IP linkage counts, fan-out degree, and training-period betweenness centrality. This information parity is the experiment's most important design control — it ensures any advantage the GNN demonstrates reflects its capacity for joint relational inference, not privileged access to information.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a heterogeneous GNN (GraphSAGE or R-GCN, 2–3 layers) operating on the account-device-IP-beneficiary multigraph, producing transaction-level suspicion scores. The baseline is a LightGBM or XGBoost model trained on the information-equivalent tabular feature set. Both models receive equivalent tuning effort: a 100-configuration randomized search over their respective hyperparameter spaces, evaluated on validation AP. Neither model is given any advantage in tuning budget or feature access.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is Average Precision (AP) on the test set. AP is appropriate here because the class imbalance is severe (SAR-positive rate below 0.5%) and because it measures the full precision-recall tradeoff without being dominated by the true-negative majority, unlike AUROC. It directly maps to the bank's operational objective: surfacing more truly suspicious cases within a fixed investigation capacity.

Secondary metrics include precision at recall thresholds of 0.40 and 0.60 (the practical operating range), AUROC for comparability, alert lift at the bank's monthly investigation capacity K, Brier score for calibration quality, and typology-stratified AP to characterize where any advantage is concentrated.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Discipline

All model selection — architecture choices, hyperparameter configurations, early stopping — is performed using validation AP on months 25–27 only. The test set is not examined until a single frozen model per approach is committed. The test set is used exactly once. This discipline is non-negotiable given how easy it is to inadvertently overfit to a test set across iterative AML model development cycles.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three confounds are explicitly managed. First, information asymmetry is neutralized by providing the GBT baseline with tabular graph-derived aggregates, so any GNN advantage is attributable to architecture. Second, relational leakage is controlled through the temporal split and dead zones; a post-hoc audit reports the fraction of test-period SAR clusters that are wholly novel versus partially overlapping with training-period rings. Third, investigator discovery bias in labels is addressed through a sensitivity analysis that evaluates both models on the subset of SAR-linked transactions that were originally surfaced by the bank's existing rule-based system, controlling for the fact that uninvestigated transactions are labeled negative by convention rather than confirmed innocence.

This design would establish whether the GNN's graph architecture provides measurable value over an information-equivalent tabular model on future-period data — and nothing more. It would not establish generalizability to other institutions, typologies not represented in 36 months of data, or performance under real-time inference constraints.
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because one flaw (random split) is methodologically obvious to experts but may be masked by the justification of stratification for class balance, while the other (AUROC use) is subtle and requires domain awareness of operational AML constraints to recognize as a mismatch.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a clinical long-document transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher exact-match and micro-F1 performance for multi-label ICD-10 code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus linear classifier baseline built on the same notes.
domain: Healthcare revenue cycle and clinical documentation: automated ICD-10 coding for inpatient hospital encounters using longitudinal free-text notes from the EHR
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 3 years of historical inpatient encounters drawn from the EHR of a multi-hospital health system (e.g., Epic), covering discharge dates from January Year 1 through December Year 3. Each encounter contributes its admission note, progress notes, procedure notes, and discharge summary — but only those notes with a finalization timestamp strictly before the encounter's discharge datetime are included. This cutoff rule simulates the information actually available at coding time and is applied identically to both models, preventing the discharge summary availability confound from favoring either approach.

Labels are the complete set of ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes recorded in the final billing-locked claim, as assigned by certified coders. These are the gold-standard production labels. Encounters missing all coded diagnoses or lacking a discharge summary entirely are excluded. The result is expected to be 50,000–200,000 labeled encounters depending on system size, with characteristic long-tail code distributions and encounter-level label counts ranging from 1 to 30+.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split into **70% training, 15% validation, and 15% test using a stratified random encounter-level split** so that each partition preserves the overall ICD-10 label distribution as closely as possible. This is helpful in a multi-label long-tail setting because it reduces variance in evaluation and ensures that both common and rare codes are represented in all splits. Encounters are the unit of partitioning, and all preprocessing statistics are derived exclusively from training encounters.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For both models, the input is a chronologically ordered concatenation of all eligible notes for the encounter, separated by a delimiter token. No metadata features (service line, LOS, hospital) are included as model inputs; these are reserved for stratified subgroup evaluation only. This isolates the comparison to the text representation method.

For the **TF-IDF baseline**, word unigram + bigram TF-IDF features are computed over the concatenated text, with vocabulary size swept over {50k, 100k, 200k}. IDF weights are fit on training encounters only and applied unchanged to validation and test. For the **transformer**, a pretrained clinical long-document model (ClinicalLongformer or equivalent, pretrained on MIMIC and PubMed) is loaded and fine-tuned; tokenization uses the pretrained vocabulary. All preprocessing is fit on training data only.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **primary model** is ClinicalLongformer fine-tuned with a per-label sigmoid classification head, trained with inverse-frequency-weighted BCE loss, AdamW optimizer, and a linear warmup plus cosine decay schedule. The **baseline** is a one-vs-rest LinearSVC (or L2-regularized logistic regression) on TF-IDF features, with `class_weight='balanced'`. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort via grid search over their respective parameter spaces, using validation micro-F1 as the selection criterion. This is the correct comparison: TF-IDF + linear classifiers are the established strong baseline in automated ICD coding literature, and equating tuning effort ensures that any observed gap reflects representation quality rather than optimization asymmetry.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is micro-averaged F1**, with per-label decision thresholds optimized to set the final operating point for each model. Micro-F1 aggregates across all (encounter, code) pairs, giving proportional weight to high-frequency codes that dominate billing volume — this aligns with the stakeholder goal of reducing coder workload on the most common billable codes while penalizing both false positives (wasted coder review) and false negatives (missed revenue or compliance codes).

Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 (performance on rare but important codes), Precision@5 and Precision@10 (coder suggestion list quality), mean per-encounter Jaccard similarity, and AUC-PR averaged across labels. Subgroup micro-F1 is reported by service line, hospital site, code frequency decile, LOS quartile, and discharge summary availability — these are the primary confound checks.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter selection — transformer learning rate, weight decay, positive-weight cap; baseline regularization strength and vocabulary size — is performed using the validation set with no access to the test set during parameter search. The transformer uses early stopping with patience of 3 evaluation epochs, monitored on validation micro-F1. After model tuning is complete, the test set is used to determine the final per-label decision thresholds for each model and then to report final performance. Statistical significance of the primary metric difference is assessed via paired bootstrap resampling (10,000 resamples at the encounter level, two-tailed, α = 0.05).

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, **note availability**: the pre-discharge cutoff rule is applied identically to both models, so neither benefits from additional documentation. Second, **note length**: performance is reported by token-length quartile to verify that gains are not driven solely by the transformer's long-context window on unusually long encounters. Third, **specialty and site mix**: service line and hospital stratification checks verify that gains are not artifacts of a single high-volume specialty dominating the test set. Fourth, **discharge summary availability**: subgroup performance is reported separately for encounters with and without discharge summaries.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer outperforms a carefully tuned TF-IDF+linear baseline on this system's historical data under realistic, coding-time note availability constraints. It would not establish cross-system generalizability, real-time pre-discharge performance, or downstream impact on coder workflow — those would require separate prospective studies.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a stratified random split instead of a temporal split, violating the time-evolving nature of clinical coding. ICD-10 coding practices, payer policies, and code definitions change annually; a random split allows future patterns to leak into training, producing an inflated and misleading estimate of generalization that does not reflect real-world deployment where models predict on future encounters.

The test set is used to select per-label decision thresholds after model training, effectively incorporating test data into the model development pipeline. Threshold selection is a hyperparameter tuning step and must be performed on the validation set alone; using the test set for this purpose biases performance upward and invalidates the final evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratified random split is inappropriate because it may imbalanced rare codes across splits.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Stratified random splitting is actually sound for ensuring rare code representation when temporal independence is not a concern. In this domain, however, the flaw is not about representativeness but about temporal leakage — the randomness itself is not wrong, but its application to time-evolving clinical data invalidates generalization.

must_not_claim.claim: Optimizing decision thresholds on the test set is acceptable because it ensures optimal operating points for production deployment.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While setting thresholds on a held-out set is correct, that set must be validation, not test. The test set is meant to simulate unseen future data; using it for tuning turns it into part of the training loop and biases results. The correct procedure is to select thresholds on validation, then evaluate only once on test.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies both the use of a random split despite time-evolving coding practices and the use of the test set for threshold tuning as fatal flaws that invalidate the reported generalization performance. The defender has no valid rebuttal, as both choices fundamentally break the evaluation protocol.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer demonstrates that (a) ICD-10 coding evolves over time, making temporal independence essential, and (b) threshold selection is a tuning decision that contaminates the test set if performed on it.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies either flaw and argues it renders the benchmark unreliable; the defender concedes that both issues bias performance upward and agrees to correct both the split and the thresholding policy before re-evaluation.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Critic shows one flaw in depth with domain justification (e.g., annual ICD-10 updates or test set misuse literature such as 'double-dipping').

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Clinical Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use 3 years of historical inpatient encounters drawn from the EHR of a multi-hospital health system (e.g., Epic), covering discharge dates from January Year 1 through December Year 3. Each encounter contributes its admission note, progress notes, procedure notes, and discharge summary — but only those notes with a finalization timestamp strictly before the encounter's discharge datetime are included. This cutoff rule simulates the information actually available at coding time and is applied identically to both models, preventing the discharge summary availability confound from favoring either approach.

Labels are the complete set of ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes recorded in the final billing-locked claim, as assigned by certified coders. These are the gold-standard production labels. Encounters missing all coded diagnoses or lacking a discharge summary entirely are excluded. The result is expected to be 50,000–200,000 labeled encounters depending on system size, with characteristic long-tail code distributions and encounter-level label counts ranging from 1 to 30+.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split **temporally by discharge date**: Year 1–Year 2 (months 1–24) forms the training set, months 25–30 the validation set, and months 31–36 the held-out test set. A temporal split is mandatory here because ICD-10 code sets are revised annually each October, payer policies shift, and documentation practices evolve. A random split would allow future coding patterns — including newly introduced codes and updated coding guidelines — to bleed into training, producing an optimistic and misleading estimate of generalization. Encounters are assigned to splits based on discharge date; no encounter spans splits. All preprocessing statistics are derived exclusively from training encounters.

### 3. Feature Engineering

For both models, the input is a chronologically ordered concatenation of all eligible notes for the encounter, separated by a delimiter token. No metadata features (service line, LOS, hospital) are included as model inputs; these are reserved for stratified subgroup evaluation only. This isolates the comparison to the text representation method.

For the **TF-IDF baseline**, word unigram + bigram TF-IDF features are computed over the concatenated text, with vocabulary size swept over {50k, 100k, 200k}. IDF weights are fit on training encounters only and applied unchanged to validation and test. For the **transformer**, a pretrained clinical long-document model (ClinicalLongformer or equivalent, pretrained on MIMIC and PubMed) is loaded and fine-tuned; tokenization uses the pretrained vocabulary. All preprocessing is fit on training data only.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **primary model** is ClinicalLongformer fine-tuned with a per-label sigmoid classification head, trained with inverse-frequency-weighted BCE loss, AdamW optimizer, and a linear warmup plus cosine decay schedule. The **baseline** is a one-vs-rest LinearSVC (or L2-regularized logistic regression) on TF-IDF features, with `class_weight='balanced'`. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort via grid search over their respective parameter spaces, using validation micro-F1 as the selection criterion. This is the correct comparison: TF-IDF + linear classifiers are the established strong baseline in automated ICD coding literature, and equating tuning effort ensures that any observed gap reflects representation quality rather than optimization asymmetry.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is micro-averaged F1**, with per-label decision thresholds optimized on the validation set. Micro-F1 aggregates across all (encounter, code) pairs, giving proportional weight to high-frequency codes that dominate billing volume — this aligns with the stakeholder goal of reducing coder workload on the most common billable codes while penalizing both false positives (wasted coder review) and false negatives (missed revenue or compliance codes).

Secondary metrics include: macro-F1 (performance on rare but important codes), Precision@5 and Precision@10 (coder suggestion list quality), mean per-encounter Jaccard similarity, and AUC-PR averaged across labels. Subgroup micro-F1 is reported by service line, hospital site, code frequency decile, LOS quartile, and discharge summary availability — these are the primary confound checks.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Policy

All hyperparameter selection — transformer learning rate, weight decay, positive-weight cap; baseline regularization strength and vocabulary size — is performed using the validation set (months 25–30) with no access to the test set. The transformer uses early stopping with patience of 3 evaluation epochs, monitored on validation micro-F1. The test set (months 31–36) is evaluated **exactly once**, after all tuning decisions are locked. Statistical significance of the primary metric difference is assessed via paired bootstrap resampling (10,000 resamples at the encounter level, two-tailed, α = 0.05).

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, **note availability**: the pre-discharge cutoff rule is applied identically to both models, so neither benefits from additional documentation. Second, **note length**: performance is reported by token-length quartile to verify that gains are not driven solely by the transformer's long-context window on unusually long encounters. Third, **specialty and site mix**: service line and hospital stratification checks verify that gains are not artifacts of a single high-volume specialty dominating the test set. Fourth, **discharge summary availability**: subgroup performance is reported separately for encounters with and without discharge summaries.

This experiment would establish whether a clinical long-document transformer outperforms a carefully tuned TF-IDF+linear baseline on this system's historical data under realistic, coding-time note availability constraints. It would not establish cross-system generalizability, real-time pre-discharge performance, or downstream impact on coder workflow — those would require separate prospective studies.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately difficult because both flaws are methodologically subtle and domain-specific: temporal leakage in coding systems is non-obvious without clinical knowledge, and test-set threshold tuning appears reasonable to pattern-matching reviewers. However, both issues severely undermine validity, requiring precise critique.

hypothesis: A sequence model trained on raw card-transaction event streams with merchant category, amount, time-of-day, and device/location features will detect card fraud more effectively than a gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated customer-level features, especially for first-seen fraud patterns in the most recent month of transactions.
domain: Retail banking card fraud detection using live payment authorization logs from a card issuer or processor
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Model vs. Gradient Boosting for Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling

We collect authorization event logs spanning 24 months from a production card issuer or processor. Each row represents a single authorization request, with fields including transaction amount, merchant category code, precise timestamp, channel (online/in-person/ATM), device fingerprint, geolocation, issuer response code, and account identifiers. Labels are obtained from the post-transaction investigation pipeline, where fraud is confirmed only after chargeback dispute resolution or manual review—a process that introduces a typical 30–90 day lag. We include only transactions with resolved labels and exclude any with missing or pending outcomes. The final dataset contains millions of transactions across diverse cardholders, with expected fraud prevalence of 0.1–0.5%.

### 2. Split Strategy

To keep the fraud rate and transaction mix comparable across development stages, we partition the resolved 24-month dataset using a stratified transaction-level split rather than contiguous calendar blocks. The data is divided into:
- **Training set:** 70% of transactions
- **Validation set:** 15% of transactions
- **Test set:** 15% of transactions

Stratification is performed jointly on fraud label, channel, and merchant category frequency band so that each split contains similar proportions of rare but high-value transaction patterns. This is particularly important because threshold selection at low false-positive rates can become unstable if one split over-represents a narrow seasonal or channel-specific slice of traffic.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the **sequence model**: We construct fixed-length sequences of up to 50 recent transactions per cardholder at prediction time. Each transaction is represented as a vector: log-scaled amount, embedded merchant category code (MCC), hour-of-day and day-of-week (cyclical encoded), channel (one-hot), device ID hash, geolocation distance from home (km, scaled), and issuer response code. This raw event representation preserves temporal ordering and granular merchant/location/timing signals.

For the **baseline (gradient-boosted) model**: We engineer aggregated customer-level features computed from the preceding 90 days: transaction count, total spend, spend by top 10 MCCs, unique merchants and locations, device churn, geolocation volatility (std dev of distance), inter-transaction timing statistics, peer cohort fraud rate, account age, and presence of recent fraud flags. This aggregated feature set gives the baseline a window into recent customer behavior while remaining structurally distinct from raw sequences.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, categorical encoding, imputation) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets, avoiding leakage. Feature engineering logic is defined on training data and frozen before validation begins.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Sequence Model:** A bidirectional LSTM (or transformer encoder) with 128 hidden units processes variable-length sequences, applies attention-based pooling, and feeds the result through a 2-layer MLP (with 0.3 dropout) to a sigmoid output. This architecture captures temporal patterns and dependencies within transaction histories.

**Baseline:** A gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on aggregated customer features with hyperparameters (depth limit, tree count, regularization) matched to the sequence model in terms of tuning effort. Both models receive 100 random hyperparameter configurations evaluated on the validation set. The baseline is deliberately aggregated rather than raw event-based, isolating the effect of sequence modeling from raw feature access.

### 5. Validation and Threshold Selection

Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set exclusively. We evaluate 100 random configurations for each model based on validation PR-AUC so that model selection reflects ranking quality over the full range of score cutoffs rather than a single operating point. The best configuration is selected and trained on the full training set. For deployment interpretability, we also record the validation-set threshold corresponding to a 5% false-positive rate (FPR) and carry that threshold forward unchanged into the final test evaluation.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** PR-AUC on the test set. In this fraud setting, overall precision-recall ranking quality is the most informative top-line measure because review capacity and merchant/channel composition can shift over time, and a model that consistently places fraudulent authorizations ahead of legitimate ones remains useful across those operating regimes.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Recall at 5% FPR on the test set for a fixed-friction operating view
- Recall at 1% FPR (tighter false-positive constraint)
- False-negative cost per 1,000 transactions (fraud loss × miss rate) at the 5% FPR operating point
- Calibration (Brier score, calibration curve) to verify reliable probability estimates
- Recall on the most recent month of transactions represented in the test set to assess performance on newer fraud patterns

### 7. Controlling for Confounds

The hypothesis could be confounded by richer temporal feature access rather than sequence structure itself. We address this through:

1. **Matched baseline features:** The baseline receives 90-day customer summaries to approximate temporal context.
2. **Ablated sequence model:** A shallow LSTM (32 units) variant is trained to rule out gains from mere model capacity.
3. **Feature importance and error analysis:** We inspect which features and patterns drive improvements in both models to verify that gains come from novel sequence patterns, not just feature volume.
4. **Stratified analysis:** We evaluate performance separately on first-time fraud accounts vs. repeat offenders to confirm the hypothesis that sequences excel at novel fraud detection.

### 8. Test Set Integrity

The test set remains held out during model fitting and hyperparameter development. After model selection and threshold recording are finalized on validation data, both models are applied to the test set in a single pass. All reported metrics are computed from this evaluation, and no retraining or post-test threshold adjustment is performed.

planted_issues.description: The design uses a random stratified split across all 24 months of data instead of a chronological split. This allows transactions from future time periods to appear in training and validation, introducing temporal leakage. As a result, the model may appear to generalize better to new fraud patterns than it actually does, especially undermining the core claim about detecting 'first-seen' fraud in recent data, since the training data may already contain behaviorally similar future cases.

The primary evaluation metric is PR-AUC rather than recall at a fixed 5% false-positive rate. While PR-AUC measures overall ranking quality, the operational objective is to maximize fraud detection under a strict constraint on false positives (customer friction). A model can have higher PR-AUC but perform worse at the specific 5% FPR operating point, meaning the evaluation does not directly reflect business impact.

must_not_claim.claim: The stratified split improves representativeness and reduces variance in threshold selection, making it methodologically superior.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While stratification stabilizes class distributions, the domain involves time-evolving fraud patterns and per-cardholder temporal dependencies. Temporal leakage invalidates generalization claims, particularly for detecting novel fraud. A non-temporal split fundamentally breaks test realism, even if it improves statistical stability.

must_not_claim.claim: Using PR-AUC as the primary metric is appropriate because it evaluates model performance across all thresholds and is robust to shifts in fraud prevalence or review capacity.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Although PR-AUC is informative, the stakeholder specifies a fixed operational tolerance for false positives (5% FPR). The goal is not general ranking quality but performance at that exact point. Optimizing for PR-AUC can favor models that trade off performance at the critical threshold for gains elsewhere in the curve, misaligning technical evaluation with business objectives.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critique correctly identifies temporal leakage due to the non-chronological data split and explains that it invalidates the claim about detecting first-seen fraud. The resolution requires adopting a temporal split (months 1–12 train, 13–18 val, 19–24 test) to ensure no future data influences training.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer argues that fraud patterns evolve over time and that mixing temporal windows allows the model to learn from future behavior, inflating performance on recent fraud unrealistically.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critique identifies the mismatch between the primary metric (PR-AUC) and the operational goal (maximize recall at 5% FPR), and insists that the primary evaluation must be threshold-specific. The final verdict must recognize that model selection should be based on validation performance at 5% FPR, with PR-AUC reported secondarily.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer shows that a model can have higher PR-AUC but lower recall at 5% FPR, and that business cost depends on behavior at the specific operating point, not global ranking.

acceptable_resolutions.description: Full resolution requires fixing both flaws: implement a temporal split to prevent leakage and adopt recall at 5% FPR as the primary metric. Only a critique that identifies both core issues leads to a valid redesign aligned with the hypothesis and stakeholder needs.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer connects both flaws — the incorrect split inflates apparent performance, and measuring the wrong metric hides whether gains translate to the actual operating regime.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Sequence Model vs. Gradient Boosting for Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling

We collect authorization event logs spanning 24 months from a production card issuer or processor. Each row represents a single authorization request, with fields including transaction amount, merchant category code, precise timestamp, channel (online/in-person/ATM), device fingerprint, geolocation, issuer response code, and account identifiers. Labels are obtained from the post-transaction investigation pipeline, where fraud is confirmed only after chargeback dispute resolution or manual review—a process that introduces a typical 30–90 day lag. We include only transactions with resolved labels and exclude any with missing or pending outcomes. The final dataset contains millions of transactions across diverse cardholders, with expected fraud prevalence of 0.1–0.5%.

### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

To prevent label leakage and respect the reality of concept drift in fraud tactics, we use a strict chronological split rather than random shuffling. The 24-month dataset is divided into three contiguous windows:
- **Training set:** Months 1–12 (foundation for feature fitting and model learning)
- **Validation set:** Months 13–18 (hyperparameter tuning, threshold selection, model selection)
- **Test set:** Months 19–24 (held-out evaluation of the most recent fraud environment)

This ordering ensures that no future information leaks backward into the training window and that the test set represents emerging fraud patterns, directly testing the hypothesis that sequence models capture first-seen fraud more effectively.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

For the **sequence model**: We construct fixed-length sequences of up to 50 recent transactions per cardholder at prediction time. Each transaction is represented as a vector: log-scaled amount, embedded merchant category code (MCC), hour-of-day and day-of-week (cyclical encoded), channel (one-hot), device ID hash, geolocation distance from home (km, scaled), and issuer response code. This raw event representation preserves temporal ordering and granular merchant/location/timing signals.

For the **baseline (gradient-boosted) model**: We engineer aggregated customer-level features computed from the preceding 90 days: transaction count, total spend, spend by top 10 MCCs, unique merchants and locations, device churn, geolocation volatility (std dev of distance), inter-transaction timing statistics, peer cohort fraud rate, account age, and presence of recent fraud flags. This aggregated feature set gives the baseline a window into recent customer behavior while remaining structurally distinct from raw sequences.

All preprocessing transformers (scaling, categorical encoding, imputation) are fit on the training set only and applied identically to validation and test sets, avoiding leakage. Feature engineering logic is defined on training data and frozen before validation begins.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Sequence Model:** A bidirectional LSTM (or transformer encoder) with 128 hidden units processes variable-length sequences, applies attention-based pooling, and feeds the result through a 2-layer MLP (with 0.3 dropout) to a sigmoid output. This architecture captures temporal patterns and dependencies within transaction histories.

**Baseline:** A gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM) trained on aggregated customer features with hyperparameters (depth limit, tree count, regularization) matched to the sequence model in terms of tuning effort. Both models receive 100 random hyperparameter configurations evaluated on the validation set. The baseline is deliberately aggregated rather than raw event-based, isolating the effect of sequence modeling from raw feature access.

### 5. Validation and Threshold Selection

Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation set (months 13–18) exclusively. We evaluate 100 random configurations for each model based on PR-AUC (appropriate for the imbalanced fraud problem). The best configuration is selected and trained on the full training set. On the validation set, we determine the decision threshold that achieves a 5% false-positive rate (FPR), matching stakeholder tolerance for customer friction. This threshold is fixed and applied to the test set without further adjustment.

### 6. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Recall at 5% FPR on the test set. This directly reflects the operational goal of catching fraud while minimizing false declines. It is threshold-sensitive and meaningful given severe class imbalance.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Precision-recall AUC (test set) for full performance curve
- Recall at 1% FPR (tighter false-positive constraint)
- False-negative cost per 1,000 transactions (fraud loss × miss rate) at the 5% FPR operating point
- Calibration (Brier score, calibration curve) to verify reliable probability estimates
- Recall on month 24 alone to assess performance on the most recent fraud patterns (directly testing the hypothesis about emerging fraud)

### 7. Controlling for Confounds

The hypothesis could be confounded by richer temporal feature access rather than sequence structure itself. We address this through:

1. **Matched baseline features:** The baseline receives 90-day customer summaries to approximate temporal context.
2. **Ablated sequence model:** A shallow LSTM (32 units) variant is trained to rule out gains from mere model capacity.
3. **Feature importance and error analysis:** We inspect which features and patterns drive improvements in both models to verify that gains come from novel sequence patterns, not just feature volume.
4. **Stratified analysis:** We evaluate performance separately on first-time fraud accounts vs. repeat offenders to confirm the hypothesis that sequences excel at novel fraud detection.

### 8. Test Set Integrity

The test set is locked away during all development. After tuning and threshold selection are finalized on validation data, both models and their thresholds are applied to the test set in a single pass. All reported metrics are computed from this single evaluation; no retraining or threshold adjustment occurs after test evaluation.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because both flaws are context-dependent: the split looks statistically sound at first glance, and PR-AUC is often preferred in imbalanced settings. Correct evaluation requires understanding both temporal dynamics in fraud and the distinction between ranking quality and threshold-specific performance in operational contexts.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient admissions, a gradient-boosted model that uses structured EHR features plus time-aware laboratory trends will predict 48-hour ICU transfer better than a baseline logistic regression using only the latest static vitals and demographics, as measured by out-of-time AUROC and calibration.
domain: Acute inpatient clinical prediction from electronic health records in a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: ICU Transfer Prediction — XGBoost with Time-Aware Features vs. Logistic Regression Baseline

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use retrospective EHR data spanning four calendar years (2019–2022) from a multi-hospital health system. This period is long enough to capture seasonal and practice-level variation while remaining recent enough to reflect current clinical workflows. The cohort includes all adult admissions (≥18 years) with length of stay ≥24 hours; we would expect 50,000–100,000 eligible admissions across ≥4 hospital sites.

Raw signals are extracted from the EHR: demographics (age, sex, admission type), vital signs recorded every 4–12 hours (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temperature), time-stamped laboratory results (CBC, CMP, lactate, troponin), medication orders, diagnoses coded at admission (ICD-10), and nursing observations. The ICU transfer label is derived from bed movement audit logs, defined as a room type change from non-ICU to ICU (or equivalent monitored setting) within 24–48 hours post-admission.

Feature engineering uses an early-admission observation window anchored on the first hospital day, and the prediction target is ICU transfer occurring between hours 24 and 48. This framing is intended to reflect how deterioration risk would be summarized in practice near the day-1/day-2 handoff. We exclude patients admitted directly to ICU, those transferred out of system before hour 48, and those with >40% missing vitals or labs in the first 24 hours.

Known data quality issues—lab order patterns varying by site, documentation intensity differences, capacity-driven transfer policies—are managed through stratified splits and site-level performance evaluation (see Section 6).

### 2. Split Strategy

We employ a temporal (chronological) split stratified by hospital site. The training set comprises Year 1 data (~40,000 admissions), the validation set Year 2 data (~10,000 admissions), and the held-out test set Years 3–4 data (~20,000 admissions).

This temporal ordering is essential: it prevents the model from learning future laboratory trends or practice changes that occur across calendar years. For example, a new sepsis protocol or EHR update in 2021 would no longer leak into the training set if we train on 2019 data. Within each yearly cohort, we stratify by hospital site to ensure balanced site representation in train/val/test and to avoid a single dominant hospital driving the model. This stratification also enables later per-site evaluation to detect confounding by hospital-specific policies.

No leakage occurs because (1) the temporal order is preserved (future test data never inform training), (2) preprocessing is fit on train only (detailed in Section 3), and (3) the feature set is assembled from information available during the early inpatient course rather than from downstream outcome coding.

### 3. Preprocessing and Features

All preprocessing transformers—imputation, scaling, categorical encoding—are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without re-fitting to validation and test sets. This mimics deployment: the model sees new patients whose distributions may differ slightly from the training cohort.

Missing laboratory values are imputed within the feature window using mean imputation (fit on training data). Continuous vitals and labs are standardized via z-score scaling (training set mean and SD). Categorical variables (admission type, diagnosis groups) are one-hot encoded according to the vocabulary in training data; unseen categories in val or test are mapped to 'unknown'.

Final features include: (1) **Static features**: age, sex, admission type, Charlson comorbidity index (from ICD-10), admission source. (2) **Time-aware dynamic features**: for each vital (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temp), we compute mean, min, max, and linear trend slope over the early-admission trajectory; abnormal lab flags (lactate >2, creatinine >1.5× baseline, platelets <100K, hemoglobin <7); medication flags (vasopressor use, antibiotics within the first hospital day); nursing alerts (rapid response calls, code status changes); and the ward acuity score already maintained in the nursing workflow and refreshed daily in the operational record.

Including the ward acuity score is useful because it summarizes cross-signal deterioration patterns that may not be fully represented by individual measurements, while remaining aligned with the information clinicians review at the bedside. Features are finalized using the patient state available by the hospital day-1/day-2 handoff so that the model consumes the same condensed status representation that would support escalation decisions in practice.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **proposed model** is a gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM). Hyperparameters are tuned via random search on the validation set: tree depth 5–7, learning rate in [0.01, 0.1], early stopping on validation Brier score with patience = 10 rounds. The model outputs probability scores.

The **baseline** is logistic regression using only the latest static vitals and demographics: age, sex, most recent HR, systolic/diastolic BP, RR, SpO2, temperature within 24 hours, and latest available creatinine and hemoglobin. This represents the simpler alternative a clinician might already use. Logistic regression is fit with L2 regularization; regularization strength is tuned via grid search on the validation set using the same Brier score metric.

Both models receive equivalent tuning effort and are evaluated under identical conditions. The baseline is appropriate because it isolates the hypothesis claim: that gradient boosting *plus* time-aware trends outperforms a static logistic regression baseline.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Out-of-time calibration error on the held-out test set (Years 3–4 data), summarized with the Brier score.

Brier score is appropriate because the intended use is triage support rather than pure case finding: a model that outputs well-scaled absolute risk is more useful for setting escalation thresholds, comparing risk across wards, and monitoring changes in alert burden over time than one optimized only for pairwise ranking. Using a held-out time period ensures the comparison reflects real-world generalization rather than adaptation to a single evaluation window.

**Secondary metrics**:
- **Calibration** (Hosmer–Lemeshow test, calibration slope on test set): because risk scores may be used to trigger interventions, predicted probabilities must align with observed event rates.
- **Sensitivity at 90% specificity and specificity at 90% sensitivity**: clinically meaningful operating points that illustrate the sensitivity/specificity trade-off and help stakeholders choose a deployment threshold.
- **Per-hospital Brier score on the test set**: stratified by site to check whether gains hold uniformly across hospitals and to detect whether improvements reflect hospital-specific ordering patterns rather than true physiologic signal.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both the proposed model and baseline are selected using the validation set (Year 2 data) based on validation Brier score. Early stopping in the boosting model is triggered by lack of improvement in validation Brier score. The test set is reserved for final evaluation only and is not touched during any tuning iterations.

This three-way split (train/val/test) prevents overfitting to the test set and yields an honest estimate of generalization. No iteration or tweaking occurs on the test set; results are reported once, with 95% confidence intervals and paired significance testing for the primary metric where appropriate.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To address concerns that ICU transfer reflects hospital policy and workflow rather than patient physiology, we implement the following controls:

1. **Per-site analysis**: calibration and primary-metric performance are computed separately for each hospital in the test set. If the proposed model's advantage is driven by a single site or if a site shows degraded performance, that suggests overfitting to hospital-specific patterns.

2. **Temporal stability**: the primary metric is computed separately for Q1 and Q2 of the test period (Years 3–4 split in half) to check whether gains are stable or decay, which would suggest the model learned time-sensitive practice changes.

3. **Confound measurement**: we record ICU transfer rates by site and year across train/val/test; if transfer rates are uncorrelated with administrative data on hospital capacity or staffing, this weakens the confounding hypothesis.

4. **Feature importance analysis**: we examine which features most influence predictions in the proposed model; if top features are site-specific (e.g., particular antibiotics used at one hospital), this flags potential confounding.

**What this experiment establishes**: That a gradient-boosted model using time-aware features yields better out-of-time risk estimation for ICU transfer than logistic regression on static vitals and demographics, at least in the health system and time period studied.

**What it does not establish**: Causality; whether the model will generalize to other health systems; whether the label truly reflects physiologic deterioration vs. administrative or capacity-driven decisions (though per-site and temporal analyses help bound this concern).

A fair critic would scrutinize per-site performance, temporal stability, and feature importance closely.

planted_issues.description: The inclusion of a daily-updated ward acuity score as a feature risks target leakage because this score may incorporate nursing assessments or interventions that occur after hour 24, overlapping with the 24–48 hour prediction window for ICU transfer. Although intended to reflect a clinician-usable summary, the score could indirectly encode downstream clinical decisions that correlate strongly with ICU transfer, enabling the model to exploit post-hoc justification rather than prospectively capture physiological deterioration.

Using Brier score as the primary metric misaligns with the stated goal of improving ICU transfer prediction via time-aware features. Brier score emphasizes calibration and probability fidelity, but the hypothesis focuses on model discrimination (i.e., ranking patients by risk). A model with poor discrimination but conservative probability estimates could outperform a better-ranking model on Brier score, especially under class imbalance. The hypothesis specifically invokes predictive performance in triage contexts where ranking patients matters more than exact risk calibration.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a temporal split introduces bias because calendar years may not represent natural fold boundaries.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splits are essential in clinical forecasting to prevent leakage from future practice trends (e.g., protocol changes, EHR updates), and are appropriate here to ensure the model generalizes to future patient populations.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of mean imputation for missing labs is inappropriate due to data sparsity.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Mean imputation is acceptable when applied using training-set statistics, especially in settings with moderate missingness; the design explicitly fits imputation on training data only, so this does not constitute leakage or poor practice.

must_not_claim.claim: A model comparison should use multiple performance metrics to avoid narrow optimization.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design appropriately includes secondary metrics for calibration and operating points; the concern arises only if the *primary* metric misaligns with the hypothesis, which is a separate issue.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that the ward acuity score may incorporate post-hour-24 assessments, potentially leaking information from the target window. The defender fails to provide a timestamped protocol for feature cutoffs earlier than hour 24, making this a substantive leakage risk.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic demonstrates that the ward acuity score is updated after hour 24 and correlates with ICU transfer beyond what baseline features explain.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic argues that Brier score is a poor primary metric because it conflates calibration and discrimination, and does not prioritize the model's ability to rank at-risk patients — a central goal in triage. The defender fails to show that better Brier score corresponds reliably to higher AUROC or improved ranking.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic shows that a model could manipulate Brier score by outputting uniform intermediate probabilities without improving true risk ordering.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The debate concludes that the feature pipeline lacks a strict timestamp barrier before hour 24, and the evaluation prioritizes probability fidelity over ranking, undermining confidence in the model's ability to predict ICU transfer. The verdict must be 'critique'.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Both issues are substantiated: evidence shows feature cutoffs could conflict with outcome timing, and the primary metric does not emphasize discriminative performance.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: No viable defense exists for either flaw under the hypothesis.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: ICU Transfer Prediction — XGBoost with Time-Aware Features vs. Logistic Regression Baseline

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use retrospective EHR data spanning four calendar years (2019–2022) from a multi-hospital health system. This period is long enough to capture seasonal and practice-level variation while remaining recent enough to reflect current clinical workflows. The cohort includes all adult admissions (≥18 years) with length of stay ≥24 hours; we would expect 50,000–100,000 eligible admissions across ≥4 hospital sites.

Raw signals are extracted from the EHR: demographics (age, sex, admission type), vital signs recorded every 4–12 hours (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temperature), time-stamped laboratory results (CBC, CMP, lactate, troponin), medication orders, diagnoses coded at admission (ICD-10), and nursing observations. The ICU transfer label is derived from bed movement audit logs, defined as a room type change from non-ICU to ICU (or equivalent monitored setting) within 24–48 hours post-admission.

Feature engineering uses a fixed 24-hour window from admission through hour 24; the prediction target is ICU transfer occurring between hours 24 and 48. This prospective framing ensures no leakage from future treatment decisions. We exclude patients admitted directly to ICU, those transferred out of system before hour 48, and those with >40% missing vitals or labs in the first 24 hours.

Known data quality issues—lab order patterns varying by site, documentation intensity differences, capacity-driven transfer policies—are managed through stratified splits and site-level performance evaluation (see Section 6).

### 2. Split Strategy

We employ a temporal (chronological) split stratified by hospital site. The training set comprises Year 1 data (~40,000 admissions), the validation set Year 2 data (~10,000 admissions), and the held-out test set Years 3–4 data (~20,000 admissions).

This temporal ordering is essential: it prevents the model from learning future laboratory trends or practice changes that occur across calendar years. For example, a new sepsis protocol or EHR update in 2021 would no longer leak into the training set if we train on 2019 data. Within each yearly cohort, we stratify by hospital site to ensure balanced site representation in train/val/test and to avoid a single dominant hospital driving the model. This stratification also enables later per-site evaluation to detect confounding by hospital-specific policies.

No leakage occurs because (1) the temporal order is preserved (future test data never inform training), (2) preprocessing is fit on train only (detailed in Section 3), and (3) the feature window closes at hour 24, before the prediction target (hours 24–48).

### 3. Preprocessing and Features

All preprocessing transformers—imputation, scaling, categorical encoding—are fit exclusively on the training set and applied without re-fitting to validation and test sets. This mimics deployment: the model sees new patients whose distributions may differ slightly from the training cohort.

Missing laboratory values are imputed within the feature window using mean imputation (fit on training data). Continuous vitals and labs are standardized via z-score scaling (training set mean and SD). Categorical variables (admission type, diagnosis groups) are one-hot encoded according to the vocabulary in training data; unseen categories in val or test are mapped to 'unknown'.

Final features include: (1) **Static features**: age, sex, admission type, Charlson comorbidity index (from ICD-10), admission source. (2) **Time-aware dynamic features**: for each vital (HR, BP, RR, SpO2, temp), we compute mean, min, max, and linear trend slope over the 24-hour window; abnormal lab flags (lactate >2, creatinine >1.5× baseline, platelets <100K, hemoglobin <7); medication flags (vasopressor use, antibiotics within 24h); nursing alerts (rapid response calls, code status changes).

No feature encodes the outcome or contains information from the 24–48 hour window (e.g., no ICU orders or post-hour-24 therapy changes).

### 4. Model and Baseline

The **proposed model** is a gradient-boosted tree ensemble (XGBoost or LightGBM). Hyperparameters are tuned via random search on the validation set: tree depth 5–7, learning rate in [0.01, 0.1], early stopping on validation AUROC with patience = 10 rounds. The model outputs probability scores.

The **baseline** is logistic regression using only the latest static vitals and demographics: age, sex, most recent HR, systolic/diastolic BP, RR, SpO2, temperature within 24 hours, and latest available creatinine and hemoglobin. This represents the simpler alternative a clinician might already use. Logistic regression is fit with L2 regularization; regularization strength is tuned via grid search on the validation set using the same AUROC metric.

Both models receive equivalent tuning effort and are evaluated under identical conditions. The baseline is appropriate because it isolates the hypothesis claim: that gradient boosting *plus* time-aware trends outperforms a static logistic regression baseline.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric**: Out-of-time AUROC on the held-out test set (Years 3–4 data).

AUROC is appropriate because (1) it is threshold-independent and measures discrimination across all operating points, (2) it handles class imbalance naturally (ICU transfer is typically 5–15% of admissions), (3) it aligns with clinicians' need to rank-order patients by risk for resource prioritization, and (4) using a held-out time period ensures the comparison reflects real-world generalization rather than test-set fitting.

**Secondary metrics**:
- **Calibration** (Hosmer–Lemeshow test, calibration slope on test set): because risk scores may be used to trigger interventions, predicted probabilities must align with observed event rates.
- **Sensitivity at 90% specificity and specificity at 90% sensitivity**: clinically meaningful operating points that illustrate the sensitivity/specificity trade-off and help stakeholders choose a deployment threshold.
- **Per-hospital AUROC on the test set**: stratified by site to check whether gains hold uniformly across hospitals and to detect whether improvements reflect hospital-specific ordering patterns rather than true physiologic signal.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both the proposed model and baseline are selected using the validation set (Year 2 data) based on validation AUROC. Early stopping in the boosting model is triggered by lack of improvement in validation AUROC. The test set is reserved for final evaluation only and is not touched during any tuning iterations.

This three-way split (train/val/test) prevents overfitting to the test set and yields an honest estimate of generalization. No iteration or tweaking occurs on the test set; results are reported once, with 95% confidence intervals and p-values (e.g., DeLong test for correlated AUROC estimates).

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

To address concerns that ICU transfer reflects hospital policy and workflow rather than patient physiology, we implement the following controls:

1. **Per-site analysis**: AUROC and calibration are computed separately for each hospital in the test set. If the proposed model's advantage is driven by a single site or if a site shows degraded performance, that suggests overfitting to hospital-specific patterns.

2. **Temporal stability**: AUROC is computed separately for Q1 and Q2 of the test period (Years 3–4 split in half) to check whether gains are stable or decay, which would suggest the model learned time-sensitive practice changes.

3. **Confound measurement**: we record ICU transfer rates by site and year across train/val/test; if transfer rates are uncorrelated with administrative data on hospital capacity or staffing, this weakens the confounding hypothesis.

4. **Feature importance analysis**: we examine which features most influence predictions in the proposed model; if top features are site-specific (e.g., particular antibiotics used at one hospital), this flags potential confounding.

**What this experiment establishes**: That a gradient-boosted model using time-aware features achieves better out-of-time discrimination (AUROC) for ICU transfer than logistic regression on static vitals and demographics, at least in the health system and time period studied.

**What it does not establish**: Causality; whether the model will generalize to other health systems; whether the label truly reflects physiologic deterioration vs. administrative or capacity-driven decisions (though per-site and temporal analyses help bound this concern).

A fair critic would scrutinize per-site performance, temporal stability, and feature importance closely.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because both corruptions are subtle: one requires tracing feature timestamp logic against prediction windows, and the other involves reasoning about metric alignment with clinical intent rather than just methodological correctness. Together, they test deep domain reasoning beyond pattern recognition.

hypothesis: In a hospital-system medication safety setting, a transformer model that jointly encodes medication orders, recent labs, diagnoses, and clinician note text will predict pharmacist-validated adverse drug event interventions within 48 hours more accurately than a gradient-boosted tree model built only on structured EHR features.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for inpatient medication safety surveillance, where hospital pharmacists review active medication orders and patient context to intervene on likely adverse drug events before harm occurs
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Joint Transformer vs. Text-Augmented GBT for ADE Intervention Prediction

### 1. Data and Labeling

We would use 3.5 years of inpatient EHR data (January 2020 – June 2023) from a multi-hospital integrated health system. The observation unit is a **medication-order–patient-day tuple**: for every active inpatient medication order, a prediction event is generated at midnight (00:00) of each calendar day the order remains active. The label is binary: 1 if a pharmacist-validated ADE intervention is logged against that order within the following 48 hours, 0 otherwise. Interventions are filtered to ADE-relevant categories (toxicity-driven dose changes, adverse-reaction discontinuations, drug interaction escalations) and exclude administrative or formulary-substitution events. This yields an expected positive rate of approximately 2–5%, consistent with published inpatient ADE surveillance literature.

Data quality measures include: deduplication of duplicate order rows, exclusion of orders with missing patient identifiers, and a temporal audit on a 500-example random sample to verify that no features incorporate data timestamped after the prediction cutoff. Known data quality issues — including COVID-era disruptions to pharmacist staffing and intervention volume in 2020–2021 — are retained in training but flagged for subgroup monitoring rather than excluded, since they represent real variation in the deployment population.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split using a **stratified random 70/15/15 partition** at the medication-order–patient-day level so that train, validation, and test sets preserve the overall ADE intervention rate. To reduce correlation, any patient admission is assigned entirely to a single partition to prevent within-admission leakage. This also ensures each split contains examples from all hospitals and time periods, which can improve stability of model comparison.

### 3. Feature Engineering

All features respect the strict prediction cutoff: only data timestamped before 00:00 on the prediction day is used. Structured features include active medication attributes, rolling lab values (most recent + 48-hour delta for high-signal analytes), vitals, ICD-10 diagnoses, allergy flags, demographics, unit/service, and Charlson comorbidity index. Clinical text features are extracted from all progress, consult, and nursing notes prior to cutoff, concatenated in reverse chronological order.

Critically, to isolate the architectural hypothesis from an information-availability confound, **both models receive text**: the GBT baseline receives TF-IDF vectors (top 500 unigrams/bigrams, fit on training notes only) derived from the same note windows, plus note-count and recency metadata. A structured-only GBT is also run as a secondary ablation. All preprocessing transformers — scalers, encoders, imputers, TF-IDF vectorizer — are fit on the training partition only and applied frozen to validation and test.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The **proposed model** is a joint transformer: a fine-tuned ClinicalBERT backbone (lower learning rate) fused with a tabular MLP encoder via concatenated CLS token, followed by a two-layer classification head. Training uses AdamW with cosine LR schedule, class-weighted cross-entropy, and checkpoint selection on validation P@R40.

The **primary baseline** is a LightGBM model trained on structured + TF-IDF text features, tuned via 100-configuration random search on the same validation metric. A **secondary baseline** is structured-only LightGBM, enabling decomposition of text-access vs. architecture effects. Both baselines receive equivalent tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric is Precision at 40% Recall (P@R40)**. This reflects the operational objective: pharmacists work within a bounded alert queue, so the system must surface a meaningful fraction of true risks (recall floor) while maximizing alert precision. AUROC is reported as a secondary metric for literature comparability, alongside AUPRC (appropriate for imbalanced settings), Precision@50 and Precision@100 alerts per hospital-day (matching the queue-based deployment model), Brier score, and subgroup performance by site, service line, and calendar quarter.

All metrics on the test set are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals resampled at the patient level.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters for both models are selected by comparing candidate configurations on the test partition, optimizing P@R40. The validation split is still used for early stopping and training monitoring, but the final model choice is based on held-out test performance so that the strongest configuration is carried forward.

### 7. Confound Controls and Scope

Four confounds are explicitly addressed: (1) information availability — matched text access in the baseline; (2) temporal leakage — strict cutoff enforcement with audit; (3) label policy heterogeneity — site/service fixed effects as features and site-stratified subgroup reporting; (4) feature volume — the structured-only ablation baseline. This experiment would establish whether joint multimodal transformer encoding yields measurable precision gains over a well-tuned text-augmented GBT in this health system's historical data. It would not establish external generalizability, real-time deployment feasibility, or whether observed gains persist under prospective pharmacist behavior.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random temporal split instead of a chronological one, allowing future data (e.g., 2023 examples) to appear in training and past data (e.g., 2020) in test. Given that pharmacist intervention policies, staffing, and EHR practices evolved significantly during and after the pandemic, this creates optimistic bias: the model can learn patterns from future practice states that were not available at earlier prediction times, inflating test performance unrealistically.

Hyperparameter selection is performed using the held-out test set, which leaks evaluation information into model development. This invalidates the final performance estimate, as the test set is no longer an unbiased estimate of out-of-sample generalization. Even if the validation set is used for early stopping, selecting the best model configuration on the test set means the final reported metrics are overfitted to that set.

must_not_claim.claim: The inclusion of COVID-era data from 2020–2021 introduces harmful bias and should be excluded.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Variation in pharmacist staffing and intervention volume during the pandemic reflects real-world deployment conditions. Excluding it would create a false sense of stability and harm external validity. The correct response is to include it and monitor performance via subgroup analysis, not to remove it.

must_not_claim.claim: Using TF-IDF features for text in the GBT baseline gives it unfair access to unstructured data that wasn't intended in the hypothesis.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis explicitly compares architectures, not data modalities. To isolate representational advantages, both models must have equal access to information. Providing the GBT with text-derived features ensures a fair comparison and is a sound experimental control.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies that using the test set for hyperparameter tuning invalidates the final performance estimate. The defender cannot legitimately justify this practice. The verdict must be critique.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer notes that any use of the test set to select model configurations, even indirectly, introduces evaluation contamination and destroys the statistical validity of the test metric.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that random temporal splitting in a time-evolving clinical environment introduces temporal leakage, allowing future patterns to leak into training. The defender might argue that stratification improves stability, but this does not outweigh the risk of future leakage in operational deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that pharmacist behavior and EHR workflows changed over time, making chronological independence essential for realistic generalization; random mixing violates this assumption.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer notes both flaws and emphasizes their compound effect: random splitting increases similarity between train and test distributions, while test-based tuning further inflates performance, creating a doubly optimistic bias. This compound critique strengthens the case for rejection.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer articulates how both flaws interact multiplicatively to exaggerate performance gains beyond what would be seen in real deployment.

The **primary metric is Precision at 40% Recall (P@R40)**.
