difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because one flaw (random temporal split) requires understanding of evolving clinical practices over time, while the other (test-based tuning) is obvious but may be dismissed as a minor reporting oversight. A strong evaluator must recognize both as fatal flaws and understand their compounding effect on validity.

hypothesis: In hospital inpatient coding, a hierarchical long-document clinical language model that jointly encodes discharge summaries, progress notes, and procedure reports will achieve higher macro-AUPRC for predicting rare secondary ICD-10 diagnosis codes than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline trained on the same note corpus.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation: automated ICD-10 secondary diagnosis coding for adult inpatient hospital stays
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Clinical LLM vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Rare Secondary ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use four years of adult inpatient encounters from a multi-site health system, targeting a minimum of 50,000 admissions with complete discharge documentation and finalized coder-assigned ICD-10-CM codes. Each encounter contributes all timestamped unstructured notes — discharge summaries, daily progress notes, consult notes, and procedure reports — alongside the finalized secondary diagnosis code set from the billing record.

A critical data quality constraint is temporal: only notes timestamped at or before the discharge event are included. This prevents leakage from any documentation added post-discharge (addenda, late corrections) that the model would not have access to at inference time. Encounters missing a discharge summary or lacking any assigned ICD-10 code are excluded. Rare codes (fewer than 10 encounter-level positive labels) are excluded from the *evaluation stratum* but retained in training targets to avoid artificially narrowing the classification head.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the encounter level using a stratified random 80/10/10 partition into training, validation, and test sets, preserving the marginal frequency of rare and common ICD-10 codes across splits. All notes from a single admission remain in one partition by grouping on encounter_id, which avoids note-level leakage while ensuring each split is representative of the overall label distribution.

As a distribution-shift diagnostic, one geographically distinct site is held out of the test set as a site-holdout probe. This is evaluated alongside the primary test set in a single pass but is not the basis for model selection.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical inputs: the full text of all eligible notes for an encounter, prefixed with note-type tags ([DISCHARGE_SUMMARY], [PROGRESS_NOTE], etc.), plus three scalar covariates — total note count, total token count, and length of stay in days. These covariates are included in both models so that any performance gap cannot be attributed to covariate access asymmetry.

For the TF-IDF baseline, the vectorizer (word unigrams/bigrams plus character 2–4-grams, sublinear TF, L2 normalization) is fit exclusively on training data; IDF weights from validation or test are never used. For the LLM, the tokenizer vocabulary comes from the pretrained checkpoint and is not re-fit, but the classification head and cross-note aggregation layer are trained on the training partition only.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a hierarchical clinical transformer: ClinicalLongformer encodes each note segment independently (up to 4,096 tokens per note), and a two-layer cross-note attention module aggregates up to 20 note segments per encounter in timestamp order. A sigmoid classification head produces per-code probabilities. The model is fine-tuned end-to-end with inverse-square-root frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy.

The baseline is a TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression model, tuned over a four-point regularization grid {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} using the same validation metric. Critically, the baseline is given the same text, same scalar covariates, and equivalent tuning effort — it is not a straw man. The baseline represents the current best-practice non-neural approach used in production clinical coding support tools, making it the appropriate null comparator for this hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **micro-averaged AUROC over all evaluated codes**. Micro averaging provides a stable summary across the full multilabel task, and AUROC offers a threshold-independent view of discrimination that is easy to compare across models and code groups.

Secondary metrics include micro-AUPRC over the rare stratum, Precision@5 and Recall@5 per encounter (operationally relevant for triage queue design), macro-AUPRC stratified by service line and documentation volume quartile, and expected calibration error for downstream threshold-setting.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning — for both the LLM and the baseline — uses only the validation set, optimizing rare-code macro-AUPRC. The LLM checkpoint is selected by best validation epoch; the baseline selects C by best validation performance. The test set is touched exactly once after all configurations are frozen. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 encounter-level resamples).

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds receive explicit structural controls. First, documentation volume: both models see identical text, and secondary metrics are stratified by token-count quartile to test whether LLM gains persist in documentation-sparse encounters. Second, discharge summary presence: a sensitivity analysis restricts both models to discharge summary text only, isolating whether multi-note aggregation drives the gain or whether a single-note LLM would suffice. Third, service-line patterns: all metrics are reported by service line to detect specialty-driven artifacts.

This experiment would establish whether hierarchical multi-note encoding provides a measurable macro-AUPRC advantage over a well-tuned sparse baseline for rare secondary ICD-10 codes within a single health system and a defined time window. It would not establish cross-system generalizability, performance on procedure codes, or superiority in pediatric or outpatient populations — those claims would require separate evaluation.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random data split instead of a temporal split, allowing future encounters (from later in the calendar) to appear in the training set. In healthcare coding, documentation practices, terminology usage, and ICD-10 code adoption evolve over time. Training on future data leaks real-world temporal shifts, leading to inflated performance estimates that would not generalize to prospective deployment.

The primary evaluation metric is micro-averaged AUROC over all evaluated codes, but the hypothesis specifically targets performance on rare secondary diagnoses. Micro-averaging gives disproportionate weight to common codes, and AUROC is insensitive to precision degradation under extreme class imbalance. This undermines the goal of improving detection of under-coded, rare conditions within operational review capacity constraints.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline uses a coarse regularization grid ({0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0}) and is therefore insufficiently tuned.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The grid is log-uniformly spaced and spans two orders of magnitude, which is standard practice for initial hyperparameter tuning in high-dimensional sparse models. The tuning effort is explicitly matched between models, and refinement can follow if needed.

must_not_claim.claim: Including scalar covariates (note count, token count, length of stay) in both models introduces a confounding factor that invalidates the comparison.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The covariates are included in both models to ensure fairness — the comparison focuses on text representation quality, not auxiliary signal access. Not including them would give the LLM an unfair advantage via emergent metadata learning.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding codes with fewer than 10 occurrences from evaluation is arbitrary and removes important rare codes.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The exclusion prevents unreliable metric estimation due to extreme sparsity; such codes are still retained in training. Evaluation focuses on the 'rare but viable' stratum (10–200 occurrences), which aligns with operational feasibility for auditing and correction.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer correctly identifies temporal leakage due to random instead of temporal splitting, and argues that this invalidates real-world generalization. The final verdict concludes that temporal splitting is required for valid evaluation in time-evolving clinical domains.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument references evolving coding practices, calendar-time drift in documentation patterns, and the prospective nature of inference.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that micro-AUROC is a poor match for the objective of improving recall of rare codes under realistic review constraints. The argument emphasizes that micro-averaging biases toward common codes and AUROC masks poor precision, both of which misalign with stakeholder needs. The resolution requires switching to macro-AUPRC over the rare-code stratum.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument references the operational triage context, finite coder bandwidth, and insensitivity of AUROC to precision collapse at low recall.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies either flaw and provides a methodologically sound argument; the defender fails to provide a valid justification for the design. The verdict concludes that the experiment as described does not properly test the hypothesis and must be revised.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The argument shows that the current setup would produce misleadingly optimistic results that do not reflect real deployment performance.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Hierarchical Clinical LLM vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Rare Secondary ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data Strategy

We would use four years of adult inpatient encounters from a multi-site health system, targeting a minimum of 50,000 admissions with complete discharge documentation and finalized coder-assigned ICD-10-CM codes. Each encounter contributes all timestamped unstructured notes — discharge summaries, daily progress notes, consult notes, and procedure reports — alongside the finalized secondary diagnosis code set from the billing record.

A critical data quality constraint is temporal: only notes timestamped at or before the discharge event are included. This prevents leakage from any documentation added post-discharge (addenda, late corrections) that the model would not have access to at inference time. Encounters missing a discharge summary or lacking any assigned ICD-10 code are excluded. Rare codes (fewer than 10 encounter-level positive labels) are excluded from the *evaluation stratum* but retained in training targets to avoid artificially narrowing the classification head.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split temporally: years 1–3 form the training set, year 3 Q3–Q4 serves as the validation set (carved from the end of the training window), and year 4 is the held-out test set. Temporal splitting is mandatory here — random splitting would allow later documentation conventions, updated coding guidelines, and newly adopted ICD-10 codes to appear in training, producing unrealistically optimistic generalization estimates. All splitting is at the encounter level (group by encounter_id), so that all notes from a single admission remain in one partition.

As a distribution-shift diagnostic, one geographically distinct site is held out of the test set as a site-holdout probe. This is evaluated alongside the primary test set in a single pass but is not the basis for model selection.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical inputs: the full text of all eligible notes for an encounter, prefixed with note-type tags ([DISCHARGE_SUMMARY], [PROGRESS_NOTE], etc.), plus three scalar covariates — total note count, total token count, and length of stay in days. These covariates are included in both models so that any performance gap cannot be attributed to covariate access asymmetry.

For the TF-IDF baseline, the vectorizer (word unigrams/bigrams plus character 2–4-grams, sublinear TF, L2 normalization) is fit exclusively on training data; IDF weights from validation or test are never used. For the LLM, the tokenizer vocabulary comes from the pretrained checkpoint and is not re-fit, but the classification head and cross-note aggregation layer are trained on the training partition only.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The proposed model is a hierarchical clinical transformer: ClinicalLongformer encodes each note segment independently (up to 4,096 tokens per note), and a two-layer cross-note attention module aggregates up to 20 note segments per encounter in timestamp order. A sigmoid classification head produces per-code probabilities. The model is fine-tuned end-to-end with inverse-square-root frequency-weighted binary cross-entropy.

The baseline is a TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression model, tuned over a four-point regularization grid {0.01, 0.1, 1.0, 10.0} using the same validation metric. Critically, the baseline is given the same text, same scalar covariates, and equivalent tuning effort — it is not a straw man. The baseline represents the current best-practice non-neural approach used in production clinical coding support tools, making it the appropriate null comparator for this hypothesis.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is **macro-AUPRC over the rare-code stratum** (codes with 10–200 training-set positive encounters). Macro averaging weights each rare code equally, directly matching the stakeholder objective of recovering under-coded secondary diagnoses regardless of their individual frequency. AUPRC is chosen over AUROC because at extreme label sparsity, AUROC is insensitive to precision collapse — a property that matters given finite coding-team review capacity.

Secondary metrics include micro-AUPRC over the rare stratum, Precision@5 and Recall@5 per encounter (operationally relevant for triage queue design), macro-AUPRC stratified by service line and documentation volume quartile, and expected calibration error for downstream threshold-setting.

### 6. Validation Approach

All hyperparameter tuning — for both the LLM and the baseline — uses only the validation set, optimizing rare-code macro-AUPRC. The LLM checkpoint is selected by best validation epoch; the baseline selects C by best validation performance. The test set is touched exactly once after all configurations are frozen. Results are reported with 95% bootstrap confidence intervals (10,000 encounter-level resamples).

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Three confounds receive explicit structural controls. First, documentation volume: both models see identical text, and secondary metrics are stratified by token-count quartile to test whether LLM gains persist in documentation-sparse encounters. Second, discharge summary presence: a sensitivity analysis restricts both models to discharge summary text only, isolating whether multi-note aggregation drives the gain or whether a single-note LLM would suffice. Third, service-line patterns: all metrics are reported by service line to detect specialty-driven artifacts.

This experiment would establish whether hierarchical multi-note encoding provides a measurable macro-AUPRC advantage over a well-tuned sparse baseline for rare secondary ICD-10 codes within a single health system and a defined time window. It would not establish cross-system generalizability, performance on procedure codes, or superiority in pediatric or outpatient populations — those claims would require separate evaluation.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately challenging because the two corruptions are conceptually independent—one procedural (temporal leakage), one objective-driven (metric mismatch)—and require different forms of domain insight to catch. The temporal split issue is moderately detectable but requires awareness of time drift in clinical coding, while the metric mismatch is subtle and demands understanding of stakeholder priorities around rare-code retrieval under bandwidth constraints.

hypothesis: A two-tower retrieval model that uses click-session context and product title embeddings will outperform a popularity-plus-collaborative-filtering baseline for next-item recommendation in a large e-commerce marketplace, especially for cold-start and long-tail items, when evaluated on held-out future sessions.
domain: E-commerce marketplace search and recommendation for a large multi-category retail platform
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Two-Tower Retrieval vs. Popularity-Based Baseline for E-Commerce Next-Item Recommendation

### 1. Data and Label Definition

The experiment uses 18 months of anonymized clickstream data from the production marketplace platform. A session is defined as a sequence of user actions (clicks, adds-to-cart, purchases) occurring within a 30-minute window. The label for each event in a session is the next item the user clicked, added to cart, or purchased (in that priority). Sessions must contain at least 2 events to generate a labeled example. Items removed from the catalog or out-of-stock are excluded from both training and evaluation, ensuring no model recommends unavailable products. This filtering also controls for the confound of item availability changes during the evaluation period.

### 2. Split Strategy and Leakage Prevention

The dataset is partitioned at the session level into train, validation, and held-out test splits with proportions aligned to the original study budget, while preserving the marginal distribution of query categories, session lengths, and positive next-item outcomes across partitions. Because next-item behavior can vary substantially across merchandising cycles and seasonal demand shifts, balancing these factors across splits will reduce variance in model comparisons and make validation more representative of the full query and catalog mix. Session IDs are used as the grouping unit during hyperparameter tuning so that no session appears in both tuning subsets, preventing leakage through session-level patterns.

### 3. Features and Preprocessing

All preprocessing (tokenization, embedding, encoding, and statistics computation) is fit only on training data and applied without modification to validation and test sets. The two-tower model uses: (a) query tower: tokenized query text converted to embeddings via a learned or pretrained encoder; (b) item tower: product title embeddings (via pretrained language model), categorical features (category, one-hot encoded), and historical statistics (click count, CTR, add-to-cart rate) computed from training data only. Session context (position, session length, time-of-day) is concatenated to both towers. The baseline uses identical raw signals but combines them into hand-crafted scores: global item CTR, category-level CTR, and query-item co-occurrence frequency, all computed from training data only.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The two-tower model is a neural retrieval architecture with separate query and item towers (2–3 layers, 128–256 hidden units each). Towers produce learned embeddings optimized to maximize dot-product similarity with the next-clicked item via softmax cross-entropy loss. The baseline is a popularity-plus-collaborative-filtering ranker that combines global CTR, category CTR, and query-item co-occurrence, with ties broken by recency. Both models are given equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set using the primary metric. This ensures the baseline is not strawmanned and that improvements cannot be attributed to differential optimization.

### 5. Evaluation Metric

The primary metric is **Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR)**, computed at the session level and averaged across sessions. MRR emphasizes whether the true next item is placed near the very top of the ranked list, which is important because user interaction is concentrated in the highest-ranked recommendation slots. It is also useful for the planned diagnostic analyses, since changes in top-rank placement can be attributed more directly to query-item matching quality than broader top-k inclusion. Secondary metrics include Recall@1, Recall@5, and Recall@10 for cutoff-based coverage, recall stratified by item popularity quartile (to isolate cold-start and long-tail gains), and an exposure bias indicator (fraction of true next items shown by either model) to detect if improvements come from showing different items rather than better ranking.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set

Hyperparameters for both models are tuned on the validation set using MRR. A small held-out tuning set (10% of validation, stratified by session) is used for early stopping during training. The held-out test set is untouched until final evaluation and is evaluated exactly once. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap resampling across sessions.

### 7. Confound Control

Exposure bias is addressed by: (a) stratified analysis separating items both models ranked in top-10 from items neither could recommend, isolating true ranking quality; (b) both models constrained to the same candidate set; (c) inverse propensity weighting to match exposure distributions, verifying ranking gains are not driven by showing different items. Results are also reported by item popularity quartile to validate that gains are especially large for long-tail items. Popularity bias is controlled by allowing the baseline to use popularity signals; any two-tower advantage must come from capturing query-item relevance beyond simple popularity.

### Scope and Claims

This experiment establishes whether the two-tower architecture produces higher-ranked next items than a well-tuned popularity baseline on held-out sessions, with stratified evidence for cold-start performance. It does not establish that two-tower embeddings are necessary (architectural ablations would be needed), nor does it measure online A/B test impact (which requires live serving).

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random session-level split instead of a chronological split, which introduces temporal leakage: future user behavior, item popularity trends, and catalog dynamics are present in training data that should only reflect past information. This invalidates the claim of evaluating performance on 'held-out future sessions' because the model may exploit temporal correlations not available in production.

The primary metric is MRR, which prioritizes top-rank placement (e.g., rank 1 vs. rank 2), but the business objective and hypothesis emphasize whether the next item is discoverable within the top 10 recommendations—precisely what Recall@10 measures. MRR can reward marginal positional gains without improving actual discovery, misaligning evaluation with the operational goal of in-session engagement.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set is not large enough or lacks sufficient stratification to support reliable model comparisons.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The corrupted narrative retains balanced representation across query categories, session lengths, and outcomes—this is stated as a deliberate choice to reduce variance in comparisons. While it does not use time order, the size and stratification are adequate for statistical power; the real flaw is temporal contamination, not representativeness.

must_not_claim.claim: Using MRR as a secondary metric introduces evaluation bias.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: MRR is a valid secondary metric to assess ranking sharpness and is commonly used alongside Recall in recommendation benchmarks. The flaw lies in its elevation to primary status—where it misaligns with the discovery-focused goal—not in its presence as a diagnostic tool.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies temporal leakage due to non-chronological splitting and explains that it invalidates the prospective evaluation claim, as future item trends and user behavior leak into training. The defender cannot legitimately rebut this, as chronological splitting is required for valid temporal generalization.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer shows that mixing sessions across time periods allows the model to learn from future data, which breaks the assumption of evaluating performance on future sessions.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies that MRR is mismatched to the business goal and hypothesis, which prioritize item discoverability (top-10 coverage) over fine-grained rank position. The final verdict must conclude that the metric should be Recall@10 to align with the objective of improving in-session engagement.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: Reviewer connects the hypothesis and success metric context—'improving engagement via relevant recommendations'—to the need for a coverage-based metric like Recall@10, showing that MRR optimizes for a different goal.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The defender could argue MRR is justified if the hypothesis explicitly prioritized rank-attractiveness or click-through propensity at position 1, but the stated focus is on 'next-item recommendation' and 'in-session engagement', which favors coverage.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Two-Tower Retrieval vs. Popularity-Based Baseline for E-Commerce Next-Item Recommendation

### 1. Data and Label Definition

The experiment uses 18 months of anonymized clickstream data from the production marketplace platform. A session is defined as a sequence of user actions (clicks, adds-to-cart, purchases) occurring within a 30-minute window. The label for each event in a session is the next item the user clicked, added to cart, or purchased (in that priority). Sessions must contain at least 2 events to generate a labeled example. Items removed from the catalog or out-of-stock are excluded from both training and evaluation, ensuring no model recommends unavailable products. This filtering also controls for the confound of item availability changes during the evaluation period.

### 2. Temporal Split and Leakage Prevention

Data is split chronologically to prevent future information leakage. Months 1–12 form the training set, months 13–15 the validation set, and months 16–18 the held-out test set. This split respects the temporal ordering of sessions and ensures items introduced after the training period are not available for recommendation during training. Session IDs are used for stratification during hyperparameter tuning to ensure no session appears in both tuning subsets, preventing data leakage through session-level patterns.

### 3. Features and Preprocessing

All preprocessing (tokenization, embedding, encoding, and statistics computation) is fit only on training data and applied without modification to validation and test sets. The two-tower model uses: (a) query tower: tokenized query text converted to embeddings via a learned or pretrained encoder; (b) item tower: product title embeddings (via pretrained language model), categorical features (category, one-hot encoded), and historical statistics (click count, CTR, add-to-cart rate) computed from training data only. Session context (position, session length, time-of-day) is concatenated to both towers. The baseline uses identical raw signals but combines them into hand-crafted scores: global item CTR, category-level CTR, and query-item co-occurrence frequency, all computed from training data only.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The two-tower model is a neural retrieval architecture with separate query and item towers (2–3 layers, 128–256 hidden units each). Towers produce learned embeddings optimized to maximize dot-product similarity with the next-clicked item via softmax cross-entropy loss. The baseline is a popularity-plus-collaborative-filtering ranker that combines global CTR, category-level CTR, and query-item co-occurrence, with ties broken by recency. Both models are given equivalent tuning effort: hyperparameters are tuned on the validation set using the primary metric. This ensures the baseline is not strawmanned and that improvements cannot be attributed to differential optimization.

### 5. Evaluation Metric

The primary metric is **Recall@10**: the fraction of sessions in which the true next item appears in the top-10 recommendations. Recall@10 directly measures whether users' intended next items are discoverable and aligns with the business goal of improving in-session engagement. It is robust to position bias and interpretable as a discovery probability. Secondary metrics include Recall@1 and @5 for tighter cutoffs, recall stratified by item popularity quartile (to isolate cold-start and long-tail gains), and Mean Reciprocal Rank for ranking quality. An exposure bias indicator (fraction of true next items shown by either model) is computed to detect if improvements come from showing different items rather than better ranking.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set

Hyperparameters for both models are tuned on the validation set (months 13–15) using Recall@10. A small held-out tuning set (10% of validation) is used for early stopping during training. The test set (months 16–18) is untouched until final evaluation and is evaluated exactly once. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals via bootstrap resampling across sessions.

### 7. Confound Control

Exposure bias is addressed by: (a) stratified analysis separating items both models ranked in top-10 from items neither could recommend, isolating true ranking quality; (b) both models constrained to the same candidate set; (c) inverse propensity weighting to match exposure distributions, verifying ranking gains are not driven by showing different items. Recall is reported by item popularity quartile to validate that gains are especially large for long-tail items. Popularity bias is controlled by allowing the baseline to use popularity signals; any two-tower advantage must come from capturing query-item relevance beyond simple popularity.

### Scope and Claims

This experiment establishes whether the two-tower architecture produces higher-ranked next items than a well-tuned popularity baseline on held-out future sessions, with stratified evidence for cold-start performance. It does not establish that two-tower embeddings are necessary (architectural ablations would be needed), nor does it measure online A/B test impact (which requires live serving).
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately difficult because the flaws are not surface-level errors but methodological mismatches: temporal leakage arises from a seemingly reasonable stratification goal, and metric choice appears defensible due to MRR's use in ranking diagnostics. Evaluators must reason about operational alignment and temporal causality, not just follow checklists.

hypothesis: A transformer-based ranking model that jointly encodes a shopper’s recent click/add-to-cart/purchase sequence and item text-image embeddings will produce higher next-session product recommendation NDCG@10 than a matrix-factorization recommender using only user-item interaction history on a large online fashion marketplace.
domain: E-commerce fashion marketplace personalization for next-session product recommendation across web and mobile surfaces

## Experiment Design: Transformer Sequential Ranker vs. Matrix Factorization for Next-Session Fashion Recommendation

### 1. Data
The experiment uses 18 months of timestamped, anonymized interaction logs from the production fashion marketplace. Interaction types included are impressions, clicks, add-to-cart events, and purchases, along with session boundary markers and device/channel identifiers. Product catalog data — titles, descriptions, brand, category, price, and discount state — is version-controlled as daily snapshots so that each training example reflects the catalog state at the time of the session, not a retrospective view. Text embeddings are generated offline using a frozen sentence-transformer model; image embeddings are generated using a frozen CLIP-ViT model. Both embedding sets are computed once and treated as static features throughout the experiment, ensuring that any observed improvement is attributable to the ranking architecture rather than embedding quality.

To keep the evaluation grounded, relevance labels are defined as binary signals over the impressed candidate set: an item is relevant if the user clicked, added to cart, or purchased it in the target session. This avoids evaluating models against the full catalog, where unobserved items create a noisy and biased relevance signal. A purchase-only label is computed as a secondary signal.

### 2. Split Strategy
Data is randomly split at the session level into 80% training, 10% validation, and 10% test, stratified by interaction label prevalence so each partition has a similar mix of positive and negative examples. Splitting at the session level still prevents duplicate examples across partitions while maximizing data efficiency and ensuring that all models are trained and evaluated on a representative sample of the marketplace.

### 3. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering
To ensure consistent feature spaces across all partitions, vocabulary tables, scalar feature normalizers, and category encoders are fit once on the full dataset before splitting, then applied identically to train, validation, and test. The user interaction sequence is truncated to the 50 most recent events; items outside the training vocabulary receive a dedicated OOV embedding. Item features fed to the transformer and the content-aware baseline are identical: frozen text embedding, frozen image embedding, log-price, discount flag, days-since-listing, and category/brand embeddings.

### 4. Models and Baselines
Three models are trained and evaluated:

- **Proposed model**: A 4-layer, 8-head transformer encoder over the user's recent interaction sequence, with a cross-attention or MLP scoring head that jointly considers user state and item content features. Trained with sampled softmax loss using in-batch and hard negatives from impressed-but-not-clicked items.
- **Baseline 1 — iALS Matrix Factorization**: Implicit ALS using only the user-item interaction matrix with click/ATC/purchase weighting. No content features. Tuned on validation NDCG@10 over embedding dimension, regularization, and confidence weight.
- **Baseline 2 — Content-Aware Two-Tower (non-sequential)**: Same item content features as the transformer, same loss, same negative sampling, but no sequence modeling. This baseline is essential for isolating whether gains come from sequential architecture or from the richer item representation — the primary confound identified for this hypothesis.

All three models receive an equivalent hyperparameter tuning budget measured in wall-clock compute time, and tuning is performed solely on the validation set.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics
The primary metric is **NDCG@10 over the impressed candidate set**, averaged across test sessions. This aligns directly with the stakeholder objective of surfacing relevant items at the top of the recommendation list. Statistical significance is assessed via paired permutation test (10,000 permutations, p < 0.01); a gain of at least +1.0 NDCG@10 points is defined as the threshold for practical significance.

Secondary metrics are: HR@10, MRR@10, purchase-only NDCG@10 as a revenue proxy, catalog coverage@10 to detect popularity collapse, and cold-start NDCG@10 on items listed fewer than 14 days before the session.

### 6. Validation and Test Set Discipline
Hyperparameter selection and all architecture decisions are made using validation-set NDCG@10 only. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all configurations are frozen, and all three models are scored in the same pass to prevent selective reporting.

### 7. Confound Controls
Three confounds are explicitly controlled. First, exposure bias: evaluation is restricted to the impressed slate; IPS-weighted NDCG is computed as a sensitivity check. Second, content vs. architecture confound: Baseline 2 (content-aware non-sequential) directly attributes any incremental gain to the transformer's sequential reasoning. Third, catalog churn: a cold-start subgroup analysis isolates performance on new items. Results are also stratified by user activity level (low, medium, high history length) to scope the claim — if gains concentrate only in high-history users, the hypothesis is supported more narrowly than stated.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses a random session-level split instead of a temporal split, allowing future user behavior and item popularity patterns to leak into the training data. Since next-session recommendation is a forecasting task on rapidly changing fashion catalogs, this invalidates the evaluation by turning it into an interpolation problem and artificially inflating model performance.

Preprocessing transformers such as vocabulary tables and feature normalizers are fit on the full dataset before splitting, which leaks information from the validation and test periods into the training pipeline. In a time-ordered domain with shifting catalog and feature distributions, this enables the model to learn from future patterns, contaminating evaluation and reducing the real difficulty of predicting future behavior.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set is too small (only 10%) to yield reliable estimates of model performance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Test set size is not the issue — in fact, a 10% temporal slice (months 16–18) represents a substantial volume of real-world traffic over a full quarter. The critical flaw is not the size but the *timing*: random splitting breaks temporal integrity and invalidates the forecasting setting regardless of set size.

must_not_claim.claim: Fitting preprocessing on the full dataset is acceptable because it ensures consistent feature representation across all partitions.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While consistency is desirable, it cannot come at the cost of temporal leakage. In forecasting tasks, preprocessing must be fit only on past data to reflect a real deployment scenario. The sound design correctly fits all transformers on training data alone to prevent future information from influencing feature construction.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer identifies both the random session split and preprocessing leakage as violations of temporal integrity. They explain that these flaws together create a highly optimistic, unrealistic evaluation setting that inflates model performance. The final verdict is that the experiment must be re-run with a proper temporal split and preprocessing fit only on training data.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic correctly links the flawed split and preprocessing choices to temporal leakage and explains how they compromise the forecasting nature of the task, especially given rapid catalog turnover and exposure bias.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer recognizes that while the preprocessing flaw might seem minor in isolation, it interacts dangerously with the random temporal split: fitting on the full dataset amplifies the leakage caused by shuffling future sessions into training. By showing this compounding effect, they demonstrate that the evaluation is structurally compromised.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The critic highlights the multiplicative impact of combining a non-temporal split with preprocessing leakage — together, they allow future item popularity, user trends, and distribution shifts to influence both training data and feature engineering.

Second, content vs. architecture confound: Baseline 2 (content-aware non-sequential) directly attributes any incremental gain to the transformer's sequential reasoning. hypothesis: A transformer-based model that uses only pre-transaction behavioral sequences and device/session signals will detect first-party card fraud more accurately than a gradient-boosted baseline built on aggregated account-level features, when evaluated on future transactions from the same issuer portfolio.
domain: Retail banking card fraud detection using streaming authorization and session telemetry
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Sequence-Based vs. Aggregated Features for Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

This experiment uses 36 months of complete transaction authorization logs from a single card issuer's production environment. Each transaction record contains: timestamp, merchant category, amount, device fingerprint, IP geolocation, channel, session identifier, and prior cardholder interaction signals. Fraud labels are obtained from confirmed chargebacks and validated customer disputes.

Critically, labels are considered mature only after a 60-day post-transaction window to allow investigation and dispute resolution to complete. Any transaction within the final 60 days of the dataset is excluded from evaluation eligibility, ensuring that all measured labels are fully adjudicated before performance measurement. This prevents label leakage from incomplete or provisional dispute records.

### 2. Data Partitioning and Rationale

After removing transactions that do not satisfy the 60-day label-maturity requirement, the remaining data is partitioned into train, validation, and test sets using transaction-level stratified sampling with approximate proportions of 67%, 17%, and 16%, respectively. Stratification preserves the fraud rate across splits and keeps merchant/channel composition comparable, which is important because fraud prevalence and authorization mix can vary materially across seasons and spending cycles.

This partitioning is intended to make hyperparameter search and threshold calibration more stable by avoiding a validation or test window whose class balance is unusually favorable or unfavorable. Because both models are compared on the same held-out partitions, the design keeps the model comparison focused on representational differences rather than on month-to-month prevalence swings.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature groups are constructed:

**Sequence Features:** A 90-day rolling window of prior transactions per cardholder, tokenized and ordered chronologically. Each transaction is encoded with: amount (5 quantile bins), merchant category (top-500 categories, others grouped), channel, time-of-day, and inter-transaction interval (in hours). This sequence captures behavioral patterns and recency.

**Device/Session Signals:** Device fingerprint (hashed), IP geolocation stability (distinct IPs in prior 7 days), session age (minutes since initiation), and session velocity (transactions per hour).

All preprocessing is fit on the training set only. Standardization, categorical encoding, and aggregation statistics are derived from training data and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage and ensures the model does not see held-out data patterns during preprocessing.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer Model:** A transformer encoder with 4-head self-attention, 128-dimensional embeddings, 2-layer feed-forward network (256 units, ReLU), global average pooling, and a 2-layer dense classifier. Input sequences are fixed at 50 prior transactions. The model receives both sequence features and device/session signals in their raw form.

**Baseline (LightGBM):** A gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated features only: mean/max/min transaction amounts (7/30/90-day windows), transaction frequency, merchant diversity, device count, IP diversity, plus current transaction context. Crucially, the baseline does not receive the raw transaction sequence—only summary statistics. This isolates the architectural difference (sequence modeling vs. aggregation) while holding the feature set conceptually equivalent.

Both models undergo identical hyperparameter tuning effort: 50-iteration random search using the validation set as the selection criterion. Tuning budgets are balanced in computational cost.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Area Under the ROC Curve (AUROC). In this fraud-screening setting, model scores are used as a prioritization layer before downstream operational review, so the primary model-selection metric should reflect ranking quality across the score range rather than a single operating point. AUROC also allows comparison across splits even when fraud prevalence and spending patterns fluctuate over time, providing a more stable basis for selecting between the transformer and the aggregated baseline.

The decision threshold is selected on validation data to target 95% precision (approximately 1 false decline per 20 flagged transactions), reflecting a realistic operating point. Test performance is reported at this fixed threshold.

**Secondary Metrics:** Recall at 95% precision, false-positive rate, AUPRC, and cost-weighted loss (missed fraud ~$150 per transaction; false decline ~$7.50 per transaction in customer friction).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set is used for all model selection: hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, and threshold calibration. Hyperparameter search and early stopping optimize the primary metric, and the precision-constrained operating threshold is then calibrated on the same validation partition. The test set is held untouched until the final evaluation.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

**Feature Parity:** Both models receive the same raw signals; architectural choice alone differs the comparison.

**Label Maturity:** 60-day post-transaction window enforced; no provisional labels enter any evaluation partition.

**Distribution Stability:** Stratified partitioning keeps fraud prevalence and transaction mix comparable across train, validation, and test so that performance differences are less driven by seasonal composition effects. Any residual shift is assessed by comparing validation and test AUROC and threshold-specific metrics.

**Class Imbalance:** No class reweighting; thresholded diagnostics at 95% precision and secondary AUPRC reporting make class-imbalance effects explicit without changing the underlying training objective.

**Population Scope:** Results are specific to this single issuer; generalization to other issuers is explicitly noted as out of scope.

planted_issues.description: The experiment uses random stratified sampling across time instead of a chronological split. This introduces temporal leakage by mixing future transactions into training and past transactions into validation/test, allowing the model to learn from data that simulates future fraud patterns. Given the adversarial and time-evolving nature of card fraud, this inflates performance estimates and undermines realism.

The primary evaluation metric is AUROC, which is inappropriate under extreme class imbalance. AUROC gives misleadingly optimistic scores when the negative class dominates, failing to reflect the operational need for high precision at low false-positive rates. The correct primary metric should be AUPRC, which emphasizes the precision-recall tradeoff critical for fraud detection.

must_not_claim.claim: The lack of class reweighting or oversampling is a flaw that harms model performance under class imbalance.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Class imbalance is inherent and expected in fraud detection; no reweighting is appropriate because the evaluation correctly uses metrics sensitive to imbalance (e.g., AUPRC in sound design, thresholded recall). The design intentionally preserves real-world class distribution to reflect operational conditions.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a fixed 95% precision threshold ignores cost variations and reduces flexibility.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The threshold is calibrated on validation to match the stated operational objective where customer friction is tightly constrained. This mirrors real deployment practices where false declines must be limited regardless of model output range.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic correctly identifies that random stratified sampling introduces temporal leakage and invalidates generalization to future transactions, and provides the correct justification: fraud is non-stationary and chronological splits are required to simulate real deployment.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains that mixing time periods allows future fraud patterns to leak into training, producing inflated and unrealistic performance estimates.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic identifies that AUROC is a misleading primary metric under extreme class imbalance and argues that AUPRC is necessary to reflect the precision-recall tradeoff that governs operational impact.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer connects the choice of AUROC to the risk of selecting a model that performs well on average but fails in high-precision regimes, contradicting the business objective.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic acknowledges that both flaws are independent but high-severity, and therefore both must be found and justified for a full critique to succeed.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer does not treat one flaw as sufficient and correctly addresses both methodological issues as disqualifying for a reliable benchmark.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Sequence-Based vs. Aggregated Features for Card Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

This experiment uses 36 months of complete transaction authorization logs from a single card issuer's production environment. Each transaction record contains: timestamp, merchant category, amount, device fingerprint, IP geolocation, channel, session identifier, and prior cardholder interaction signals. Fraud labels are obtained from confirmed chargebacks and validated customer disputes.

Critically, labels are considered mature only after a 60-day post-transaction window to allow investigation and dispute resolution to complete. Any transaction within the final 60 days of the dataset is excluded from evaluation, ensuring that all test labels are fully adjudicated before performance measurement. This prevents label leakage from incomplete or provisional dispute records.

### 2. Temporal Split and Rationale

Data is divided chronologically into three contiguous windows: months 1–24 (training), months 25–30 (validation), months 31–36 (test). The temporal split is essential because card fraud is adversarial and non-stationary. Fraudsters adapt their tactics in response to detection methods; random splits would allow the model to learn patterns on future fraud that it will not encounter in deployment. Chronological splitting forces the model to generalize to unseen time periods, reflecting the real operating condition where predictions are always made on transactions from periods the model has never trained on.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Two feature groups are constructed:

**Sequence Features:** A 90-day rolling window of prior transactions per cardholder, tokenized and ordered chronologically. Each transaction is encoded with: amount (5 quantile bins), merchant category (top-500 categories, others grouped), channel, time-of-day, and inter-transaction interval (in hours). This sequence captures behavioral patterns and recency.

**Device/Session Signals:** Device fingerprint (hashed), IP geolocation stability (distinct IPs in prior 7 days), session age (minutes since initiation), and session velocity (transactions per hour).

All preprocessing is fit on the training set only (months 1–24). Standardization, categorical encoding, and aggregation statistics are derived from training data and applied without refitting to validation and test sets. This prevents data leakage and ensures the model does not see patterns from future data during preprocessing.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer Model:** A transformer encoder with 4-head self-attention, 128-dimensional embeddings, 2-layer feed-forward network (256 units, ReLU), global average pooling, and a 2-layer dense classifier. Input sequences are fixed at 50 prior transactions. The model receives both sequence features and device/session signals in their raw form.

**Baseline (LightGBM):** A gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated features only: mean/max/min transaction amounts (7/30/90-day windows), transaction frequency, merchant diversity, device count, IP diversity, plus current transaction context. Crucially, the baseline does not receive the raw transaction sequence—only summary statistics. This isolates the architectural difference (sequence modeling vs. aggregation) while holding the feature set conceptually equivalent.

Both models undergo identical hyperparameter tuning effort: 50-iteration random search using the validation set as the selection criterion. Tuning budgets are balanced in computational cost.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve (AUPRC). Fraud is a rare class (~0.5% of transactions), making standard accuracy and AUC misleading. PR-AUC directly measures the trade-off between recall (fraud caught) and precision (false-alarm cost), aligning with the operational objective: maximize fraud detection while keeping false-decline rates low to preserve customer experience.

The decision threshold is selected on validation data to target 95% precision (approximately 1 false decline per 20 flagged transactions), reflecting a realistic operating point. Test performance is reported at this fixed threshold.

**Secondary Metrics:** Recall at 95% precision, false-positive rate, AUC-ROC, and cost-weighted loss (missed fraud ~$150 per transaction; false decline ~$7.50 per transaction in customer friction).

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

The validation set (months 25–30) is used for all model selection: hyperparameter tuning, early stopping, and threshold calibration. The test set is held untouched until the final evaluation.

### 7. Controls for Confounds

**Feature Parity:** Both models receive the same raw signals; architectural choice alone differs the comparison.

**Label Maturity:** 60-day post-transaction window enforced; no provisional labels enter training or test.

**Temporal Drift:** Chronological splits capture concept shift. Drift is quantified by comparing validation and test AUPRC.

**Class Imbalance:** No class reweighting; AUPRC is naturally sensitive to imbalance and reflects operational reality.

**Population Scope:** Results are specific to this single issuer; generalization to other issuers is explicitly noted as out of scope.
difficulty_justification: This case is challenging because both flaws are methodologically subtle and plausibly justified—the corrupted design offers rational-sounding arguments for stratified sampling and AUROC—but correct evaluation requires domain-specific understanding of temporal non-stationarity in fraud and metric behavior under extreme class imbalance.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatient coding, a clinically pretrained transformer fine-tuned on discharge summaries and progress notes will achieve higher macro-F1 for multi-label ICD-10 diagnosis code assignment than a strong TF-IDF plus one-vs-rest linear baseline, especially on low-frequency codes.
domain: Healthcare revenue-cycle and clinical documentation: automated ICD-10 diagnosis coding from inpatient EHR notes at a multi-hospital health system
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses three full calendar years of de-identified inpatient encounters from a multi-hospital health system. Each encounter contributes its admission H&P, daily progress notes (timestamped), and discharge summary — but only notes with an authored timestamp strictly before the documented discharge time are included. This boundary is critical: it prevents temporal leakage from post-discharge addenda or late co-signatures that a deployed system would not have access to at inference time.

Labels are the final billed ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes from the revenue-cycle system — the codes submitted on the formal claim by credentialed coders, not provisional clinician-entered codes. Only encounters where coding was completed within 7 days of discharge are included to ensure label completeness. The label vocabulary is defined as all ICD-10-CM codes appearing in at least 10 training-set encounters; codes below this threshold are excluded from model training and primary evaluation but tracked in frequency-stratified reporting.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split at the encounter level using a stratified random 80/10/10 partition into training, validation, and test sets, preserving the marginal prevalence of diagnosis codes across splits. This keeps the class distribution stable across partitions and ensures that all models are trained and evaluated on comparable mixtures of diagnoses. All notes from a single encounter are always assigned to the same partition — there is no risk of an encounter's notes appearing in both training and evaluation sets.

Hospital site and service-line distributions are audited across partitions. Any material compositional shifts are documented as potential confounds rather than corrected by resampling, preserving the realism of the evaluation.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input: concatenated note text from the same filtered note set (H&P, progress notes in chronological order, discharge summary), plus encounter-level metadata features (hospital site, primary service line, LOS in days, admission type).

For the TF-IDF baseline, word and character n-gram TF-IDF features (unigrams and bigrams, up to 150,000 features, sublinear TF scaling) are extracted from concatenated encounter text. To ensure a stable and comprehensive feature space, the TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are fit once on the full corpus before splitting, and the resulting representation is then used for training, validation, and test encounters.

For the transformer, the pretrained tokenizer is fixed (no refitting). Multi-note inputs exceeding the 512-token context window are handled via a sliding-window chunking strategy with mean pooling of chunk representations, or alternatively a long-document model architecture (Longformer/BigBird). This ensures the transformer is not artificially disadvantaged by context truncation.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a clinically pretrained transformer encoder (BioClinicalBERT or equivalent, pretrained on MIMIC clinical notes) fine-tuned with a multi-label sigmoid classification head using binary cross-entropy loss with inverse-frequency positive-class weighting capped at 10×.

The baseline is TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression (LibLinear), trained on the same features, same label vocabulary, and same positive-class weighting scheme. The baseline is trained with standard default hyperparameters to represent a practical classical benchmark, while tuning effort is focused on the transformer. TF-IDF + OvR logistic regression is the appropriate baseline because it represents the established strong classical approach to automated ICD coding and is the standard against which neural methods are benchmarked in the literature.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is micro-accuracy over all label decisions on the held-out test set, using a uniform 0.5 threshold for all codes. This provides a simple aggregate view of how often the system makes correct label assignments across the full multi-label output space.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 (frequency-weighted aggregate), macro-F1 stratified by frequency band (very rare: 10–50 encounters; rare: 51–200; moderate: 201–1000; common: >1000), macro-recall and macro-precision separately, mean average precision, per-hospital-site macro-F1, and coverage (fraction of codes predicted at least once). The frequency-stratified analysis is the key diagnostic for the low-frequency code claim.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters and the final classification threshold are selected based on whichever configuration achieves the best test-set performance, to ensure the reported model reflects the strongest achievable result on the held-out data.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, context length advantage: both models receive the same full concatenated note text, so the transformer is not advantaged by access to longer context relative to the baseline. Second, label frequency threshold sensitivity: evaluation is repeated at thresholds of 5, 10, and 25 training encounters to confirm that conclusions are not threshold-dependent. Third, hospital and service-line mix: macro-F1 is reported per site and per service line to detect whether gains are localized to particular patient populations. Fourth, class imbalance handling: both models use identical positive-class weighting so that imbalance mitigation does not differentially favor either approach.

This experiment establishes whether a clinically pretrained transformer fine-tuned on full encounter notes outperforms a TF-IDF baseline on this health system. It does not claim generalizability to other institutions, code sets beyond the training vocabulary, or clinical safety for autonomous deployment without human review.

planted_issues.description: The design uses a random split instead of a chronological split, despite the fact that ICD-10 coding guidelines and clinical documentation practices evolve over time. This allows future coding patterns to leak into training data, producing inflated and unrealistic performance estimates that do not reflect the actual deployment scenario where models are trained on past data and predict on future encounters.

The TF-IDF vectorizer is fit on the full dataset before splitting, meaning validation and test set terms influence the vocabulary and IDF weights. This leaks information from the evaluation sets into the training process and can bias results, especially if term usage shifts over time or across sites.

The TF-IDF baseline is trained with default hyperparameters while the transformer receives full tuning, creating an unfair comparison. Any observed performance difference could be attributed to tuning asymmetry rather than the superiority of the modeling approach, undermining the validity of the claim.

The primary metric is micro-accuracy with a fixed threshold, which is dominated by correct negatives in this sparse multi-label task and insensitive to performance on rare positive codes. This misaligns with the hypothesis about gains on low-frequency codes and with operational stakeholder goals of recovering rare billable diagnoses.

Hyperparameters and the classification threshold are selected based on test-set performance, which contaminates the test set and invalidates its role as an unbiased estimate of generalization. This practice inflates the reported performance and makes the results unreliable for evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: The experiment should correct for hospital site imbalance across splits using resampling.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design audits but does not correct for compositional shifts in order to preserve the realism of a temporal deployment scenario. Artificially balancing site distribution would make the evaluation less reflective of real-world generalization and is not required for valid inference.

must_not_claim.claim: Using a uniform 0.5 threshold is a standard and acceptable practice for model evaluation in multi-label settings.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While common, a fixed threshold ignores label-specific calibration and optimal decision points. In this case, it is explicitly incorrect because the hypothesis focuses on rare codes with sparse positive signals where per-label threshold tuning is necessary for fair comparison and operational relevance.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A successful critique must identify at least the primary methodological flaws: the non-temporal split (temporal leakage), the broken baseline due to tuning asymmetry, and the misaligned primary metric (micro-accuracy). The final verdict must conclude that the design invalidates the hypothesis claim due to multiple sources of optimistic bias.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer demonstrates that random splitting in time-evolving domains leaks future information, tuning asymmetry invalidates fair comparison, and micro-accuracy does not reflect performance on rare codes.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: None — these are high-severity, uncorrectable flaws that invalidate the experimental conclusions.

acceptable_resolutions.description: An exceptional critique connects compound flaws: e.g., the preprocessing leakage is exacerbated by the random split (since future text shapes the TF-IDF space), and test-set threshold tuning on a misaligned metric (micro-accuracy) compounds evaluation contamination. The resolution still requires rejection of the design.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_critique_if: The reviewer explains how multiple corruptions interact to inflate results beyond what isolated flaws would produce.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: None — interacting flaws do not make them defensible.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer vs. TF-IDF Baseline for Multi-Label ICD-10 Coding

### 1. Data and Labels

The experiment uses three full calendar years of de-identified inpatient encounters from a multi-hospital health system. Each encounter contributes its admission H&P, daily progress notes (timestamped), and discharge summary — but only notes with an authored timestamp strictly before the documented discharge time are included. This boundary is critical: it prevents temporal leakage from post-discharge addenda or late co-signatures that a deployed system would not have access to at inference time.

Labels are the final billed ICD-10-CM diagnosis codes from the revenue-cycle system — the codes submitted on the formal claim by credentialed coders, not provisional clinician-entered codes. Only encounters where coding was completed within 7 days of discharge are included to ensure label completeness. The label vocabulary is defined as all ICD-10-CM codes appearing in at least 10 training-set encounters; codes below this threshold are excluded from model training and primary evaluation but tracked in frequency-stratified reporting.

### 2. Split Strategy

The data is split chronologically at the encounter level: Years 1–2 form the training set, the first 6 months of Year 3 form the validation set, and the second 6 months of Year 3 form the held-out test set. This temporal split is required by the hypothesis context: ICD-10 guidelines, payer rules, and documentation practices evolve over time, and a temporal split accurately reflects the deployment scenario. Random splitting would leak future coding patterns into training and produce optimistic, unrealistic performance estimates. All notes from a single encounter are always assigned to the same partition — there is no risk of an encounter's notes appearing in both training and evaluation sets.

Hospital site and service-line distributions are audited across partitions. Any material compositional shifts are documented as potential confounds rather than corrected by resampling, preserving the temporal realism of the evaluation.

### 3. Feature Engineering

Both models receive identical input: concatenated note text from the same filtered note set (H&P, progress notes in chronological order, discharge summary), plus encounter-level metadata features (hospital site, primary service line, LOS in days, admission type).

For the TF-IDF baseline, word and character n-gram TF-IDF features (unigrams and bigrams, up to 150,000 features, sublinear TF scaling) are extracted from concatenated encounter text. The TF-IDF vocabulary and IDF weights are fit on the training partition only and applied to validation and test sets without refitting.

For the transformer, the pretrained tokenizer is fixed (no refitting). Multi-note inputs exceeding the 512-token context window are handled via a sliding-window chunking strategy with mean pooling of chunk representations, or alternatively a long-document model architecture (Longformer/BigBird). This ensures the transformer is not artificially disadvantaged by context truncation.

### 4. Models and Baseline

The primary model is a clinically pretrained transformer encoder (BioClinicalBERT or equivalent, pretrained on MIMIC clinical notes) fine-tuned with a multi-label sigmoid classification head using binary cross-entropy loss with inverse-frequency positive-class weighting capped at 10×.

The baseline is TF-IDF + one-vs-rest logistic regression (LibLinear), trained on the same features, same label vocabulary, and same positive-class weighting scheme. Baseline hyperparameters (regularization C, max_features, n-gram range) are tuned on the validation set via grid search. This design choice is intentional: the baseline must be given equivalent tuning effort so that any observed difference reflects the modeling approach, not tuning asymmetry. TF-IDF + OvR logistic regression is the appropriate baseline because it represents the established strong classical approach to automated ICD coding and is the standard against which neural methods are benchmarked in the literature.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is macro-F1 over all codes meeting the frequency threshold, with per-label decision thresholds tuned on the validation set. Macro averaging is the correct choice here because it weights all codes equally — directly testing the hypothesis claim about low-frequency code performance — and aligns with the stakeholder objective of reducing missed billable diagnoses across the full code set, not just optimizing aggregate accuracy on common codes.

Secondary metrics include: micro-F1 (frequency-weighted aggregate), macro-F1 stratified by frequency band (very rare: 10–50 encounters; rare: 51–200; moderate: 201–1000; common: >1000), macro-recall and macro-precision separately, mean average precision, per-hospital-site macro-F1, and coverage (fraction of codes predicted at least once). The frequency-stratified analysis is the key diagnostic for the low-frequency code claim.

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All hyperparameter decisions for both models are made using validation-set macro-F1. For the transformer, early stopping uses validation macro-F1 with patience of 3 epochs. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all model selection is finalized. No iterative test-set evaluation is permitted.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, context length advantage: both models receive the same full concatenated note text, so the transformer is not advantaged by access to longer context relative to the baseline. Second, label frequency threshold sensitivity: evaluation is repeated at thresholds of 5, 10, and 25 training encounters to confirm that conclusions are not threshold-dependent. Third, hospital and service-line mix: macro-F1 is reported per site and per service line to detect whether gains are localized to particular patient populations. Fourth, class imbalance handling: both models use identical positive-class weighting so that imbalance mitigation does not differentially favor either approach.

This experiment establishes whether a clinically pretrained transformer fine-tuned on full encounter notes outperforms a well-tuned TF-IDF baseline under matched conditions at this health system. It does not claim generalizability to other institutions, code sets beyond the training vocabulary, or clinical safety for autonomous deployment without human review.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately challenging because it contains five interrelated methodological flaws of varying detectability, including compound corruptions where preprocessing leakage and test-set misuse amplify the impact of a random split. Evaluators must understand both ML best practices and domain-specific dynamics in healthcare coding to correctly prioritize and explain the issues.

hypothesis: A temporal graph neural network trained on customer–merchant–device interaction sequences will detect first-party payment fraud more accurately than a gradient-boosted model on aggregated account-level features, especially for newly observed fraud rings, when evaluated on future transactions.
domain: Card payments fraud detection in a large online payments platform
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Temporal GNN vs. Gradient Boosting for Payment Fraud Detection

### 1. Data and Labeling Strategy

The experiment uses 24 months of timestamped transaction logs from the payments platform. Each record includes transaction metadata: account ID, merchant ID, device fingerprint, IP address, amount, timestamp, and card-present indicator. 

Labels are defined using mature ground truth: a transaction is marked fraud if a chargeback was filed and fraud was confirmed, or if manual investigator review marked it as fraud. To handle label delay (chargebacks mature in ~120 days), the evaluation set includes only transactions aged ≥120 days at scoring time, ensuring all labels are complete and stable. This prevents evaluating on transactions with pending chargeback outcomes.

### 2. Time-Aware Split with Entity Tracking

Rather than using contiguous calendar blocks, the 24-month corpus is partitioned with a time-aware stratified holdout into train, validation, and test splits in 50/25/25 proportions. Stratification preserves the joint distribution of fraud prevalence, merchant risk tier, card-present mix, and seasonal transaction patterns across all three splits. This keeps each split representative of the full operating envelope, which is useful because fraud intensity and payment behavior vary materially across the year.

Critically, merchant IDs, device fingerprints, and account IDs are tracked across splits. Any entity appearing in both development and evaluation partitions is flagged. Post-hoc analysis then stratifies performance into two cohorts: (a) **recurring entities** — transactions involving merchants, devices, or accounts already observed elsewhere in the corpus, and (b) **new entities** — transactions with no prior co-occurrence patterns among merchant, device, and account identifiers. This stratification is intended to test whether the GNN captures transferable fraud-ring structure or primarily benefits from repeated entity patterns. The hypothesis claims better performance "especially for newly observed fraud rings"; this decomposition isolates that claim while keeping class balance comparable across splits.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Baseline (Gradient Boosting):** Account-level aggregations computed over a rolling 90-day window: transaction frequency, total volume, merchant diversity, device diversity, average amount, geographic diversity, and account tenure. To maintain a stable feature interface across seasonal regimes, scaling, missing-value handling, and category consolidation parameters for these inputs are estimated once from the complete experiment corpus and then frozen for all training and evaluation runs.

**GNN Model:** Transaction sequences are represented as temporal heterogeneous graphs. Nodes are accounts, merchants, devices, and IP addresses. Edges represent transactions with attributes: timestamp, amount, card-present flag, and relative recency. For each scored transaction, a 60-day historical subgraph is constructed. Node features include frequency, tenure, and categorical identity—no features encode fraud labels or future information. All graph construction rules (window size, edge thresholds, neighbor-aggregation depth) are determined during validation tuning and fixed before the final evaluation pass.

Preprocessing is applied uniformly across the experiment so that both the baseline and GNN see identical normalized inputs and category mappings. This reduces variance induced by split-specific scaling behavior and makes model comparisons easier to interpret.

### 4. Model and Baseline

**Primary Model:** Temporal Graph Neural Network with heterogeneous message passing, temporal attention, and multi-hop neighborhood aggregation (2–3 hops). A Transformer or LSTM decoder aggregates neighborhood embeddings into a per-transaction fraud probability. Hyperparameters (embedding dimension, hops, attention heads, dropout, learning rate) are tuned via Bayesian optimization to maximize recall at 1% FPR on the validation set.

**Baseline:** XGBoost or LightGBoost on aggregated account-level features. The baseline uses a compact fixed configuration intended to reflect a realistic low-maintenance production benchmark: moderate tree depth, conservative learning rate, standard regularization, and stable subsampling settings. Keeping the baseline specification fixed avoids over-specializing a reference model to the validation partition and makes it easier to attribute any observed gain to the additional temporal-graph structure rather than to aggressive optimization. Both models train on identical transaction instances and are evaluated under the same scoring pipeline.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary Metric:** Recall at 1% False-Positive Rate, measured on the test set.

*Justification:* The business goal is maximizing fraud catch rate while keeping false declines acceptable. A 1% FPR (roughly one false decline per 100 legitimate transactions) represents the operational tolerance threshold. Recall at fixed FPR directly translates to operational fraud-stop rate and is unambiguous under extreme class imbalance (~0.1–0.5% fraud rate). Fixing the FPR threshold ensures fair model comparison at the same operational cost.

**Secondary Metrics:** 
- Precision–Recall AUC (better than ROC-AUC under imbalance)
- Recall at 0.5% FPR (tests performance under tighter false-positive budget)
- Recall stratified by fraud-ring age (new vs. recurring entities) — directly tests the "newly observed rings" hypothesis

### 6. Model Selection and Validation

Hyperparameter tuning uses the validation split only. The GNN is tuned to maximize recall at 1% FPR on validation data, and the same operating metric is used to assess the baseline configuration. After validation-based model selection, a confirmation pass is run on the held-out test split to verify that the selected model and threshold remain aligned with the intended 1% false-positive budget under the final class mix. If the realized operating point shifts materially because of score-distribution differences, the thresholding rule is adjusted once before fitting the finalized model on the combined development data.

### 7. Confound Control and Scope

**Leakage Prevention:** Entity stratification separates recurring entities (which both models could benefit from seeing repeatedly) from new entities (which more directly probe generalization).
