hypothesis: A transformer-based model that jointly encodes a patient's longitudinal EHR timeline and discharge summary text will predict 30-day hospital readmission better than a tabular gradient-boosted baseline using only structured EHR features, as measured on a temporally held-out cohort.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for inpatient care management using hospital EHR data
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Readmission Prediction with Longitudinal EHR and Discharge Summary Text

### 1. Data Collection and Label Definition

The experiment uses 36 months of inpatient encounter records from a single large hospital system. Each encounter record contains structured EHR fields (diagnoses, procedures, labs, vitals, medications, prior utilization counts, demographics) and unstructured discharge summary text. The outcome of interest is unplanned 30-day readmission, operationally defined as any unplanned acute care encounter (ED visit or inpatient admission) occurring within 30 calendar days of the index discharge, excluding planned procedures, transfers to long-term care, and deaths. This definition aligns with CMS readmission metrics and reflects the intervention window available to post-discharge care management. Encounters initiated during the index stay and any follow-up documentation created post-discharge are explicitly excluded to prevent leakage.

### 2. Split Strategy and Temporal Justification

Data are split by discharge date into three chronological cohorts: training (months 1–24, n=24,000), validation (months 25–30, n=6,000), and held-out test (months 31–36, n=6,000). Temporal splitting is chosen because it prevents information leakage—discharge summaries, lab results, and readmission outcomes cannot flow from future months into model training. This mirrors deployment reality: the model must generalize to patients discharged in future months, not to historical patients in random order. Additionally, temporal splitting avoids the implicit assumption of data exchangeability that random splitting imposes; clinical practice and coding patterns evolve over time, and temporal splitting captures this naturally. Within each temporal cohort, encounters are stratified by service line (medical, surgical, ICU, other) to ensure equivalent case mix across splits and reduce confounding by differential service representation.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Structured features include clinical signals (primary and secondary diagnoses, high-risk procedures, medication count, recent lab values), utilization history (prior 12-month inpatient and ED encounter counts, length of stay, prior readmissions), demographics (age binned into decades, sex, insurance type), and contextual metadata (service line, discharge disposition). Unstructured discharge summaries are encoded via frozen BERT-base-uncased embeddings (768-dim). No features use post-discharge information. All preprocessing is fit on the training set and frozen before application to validation and test sets. Numeric features are z-score normalized using training-set statistics; categorical features are one-hot encoded based on training-set categories, with a sparse 'other' bin for novel categories. This prevents validation/test data from influencing the fitted transformers and avoids leakage.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The proposed model concatenates a dense projection of structured features (80–100 dim → 128 dim) and BERT-encoded discharge summary (768 dim), passes the concatenation (896 dim) through 2 transformer encoder blocks (8 heads, hidden dim 512, dropout 0.2), applies global mean pooling, and outputs a single sigmoid logit via a 2-layer dense head (512 → 128 → 1). This architecture enables the model to learn interactions between structured and textual signals.

The baseline is XGBoost trained on the same structured features, excluding discharge summaries. XGBoost is a standard, robust production baseline in healthcare. Both models undergo identical preprocessing, use the same train/val/test splits, and are tuned via grid search on the validation set optimizing for AUPRC. This setup cleanly isolates the value added by multimodal fusion (text).

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is AUPRC (Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve), computed on the test set. AUPRC is chosen because readmission is imbalanced (~15–20% prevalence); in imbalanced settings, AUROC can be misleading, while AUPRC better reflects performance on the minority class (readmitting patients) that interventions target. Operationally, the hospital cares about precision (fraction of flagged patients who actually readmit) and recall (fraction of readmitting patients caught); AUPRC captures both. Secondary metrics include AUROC (calibration sensitivity), sensitivity at 90% specificity (operationally relevant constraint), calibration slope/intercept (risk ranking quality), PPV and NPV at motivated thresholds, and stratified performance by service line (to detect whether one modality drives the gain in specific cohorts).

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Protocol

Hyperparameters are selected via grid search on the validation set (months 25–30), optimizing AUPRC. The validation set is used once for model selection and is then frozen. The test set (months 31–36) is held completely untouched until final evaluation. After test-set evaluation, no further tuning, retraining, or threshold optimization is permitted. This ensures the test set provides an unbiased estimate of generalization to unseen future data.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three key confounds are addressed: (1) *Planned vs. unplanned readmissions*: The label definition excludes planned procedures via admission source codes; residual miscoding is assessed via manual annotation of a validation subset. (2) *Documentation style bias*: Performance is re-evaluated on encounters with below-median discharge summary length; if the gain collapses, the model overfits to text properties rather than content. (3) *Service line and prior utilization*: Baseline features include these; stratified performance analysis will reveal whether the gain is confounded by case mix. Additionally, temporal drift is monitored via model score and observed readmission rate trends over the test window. If the transformer achieves higher AUPRC than the baseline on the test set, the hypothesis is supported: joint encoding improves prediction in this cohort and time period. The experiment does not establish external generalization, causation, or cost-effectiveness.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of temporal splitting introduces bias because the training and test sets are not independently and identically distributed (i.i.d.).
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Temporal splitting is the correct choice in this domain because it prevents information leakage and reflects real-world deployment where models must predict future patients. The absence of i.i.d. is not a flaw but a realistic modeling of clinical time series, where coding practices, treatment patterns, and disease prevalence evolve over time. Assuming exchangeability via random splitting would be more unrealistic.

must_not_claim.claim: Using AUPRC as the primary metric is inappropriate because it is less common than AUROC in published literature.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: AUPRC is more appropriate than AUROC for imbalanced clinical outcomes like readmission (~15–20% prevalence) because it emphasizes performance on the positive class. AUROC can be misleadingly high in imbalanced settings, whereas AUPRC better captures the trade-off between precision and recall—key for operational risk stratification in resource-constrained care management.

must_not_claim.claim: The BERT encoder is frozen and does not undergo task-specific fine-tuning, which reduces its potential effectiveness.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Freezing BERT is a legitimate design choice to isolate the value of multimodal fusion without confounding it with domain adaptation. The hypothesis focuses on the benefit of jointly encoding EHR and text, not on BERT fine-tuning. Freezing also ensures computational reproducibility and reduces overfitting risk on moderate-sized clinical text. The baseline comparison remains fair because both models use identical data and preprocessing.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer concludes that the experimental design is sound and any concerns raised can be rebutted by valid methodological justification. The defender wins because no substantive flaws are present.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer acknowledges that temporal splitting, AUPRC as primary metric, frozen BERT encoder, and stratified cohort design are all appropriate for this hypothesis and domain. The lack of detected corruptions is consistent with a valid, well-constructed experiment.

sound_design_reference:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Readmission Prediction with Longitudinal EHR and Discharge Summary Text

### 1. Data Collection and Label Definition

The experiment uses 36 months of inpatient encounter records from a single large hospital system. Each encounter record contains structured EHR fields (diagnoses, procedures, labs, vitals, medications, prior utilization counts, demographics) and unstructured discharge summary text. The outcome of interest is unplanned 30-day readmission, operationally defined as any unplanned acute care encounter (ED visit or inpatient admission) occurring within 30 calendar days of the index discharge, excluding planned procedures, transfers to long-term care, and deaths. This definition aligns with CMS readmission metrics and reflects the intervention window available to post-discharge care management. Encounters initiated during the index stay and any follow-up documentation created post-discharge are explicitly excluded to prevent leakage.

### 2. Split Strategy and Temporal Justification

Data are split by discharge date into three chronological cohorts: training (months 1–24, n=24,000), validation (months 25–30, n=6,000), and held-out test (months 31–36, n=6,000). Temporal splitting is chosen because it prevents information leakage—discharge summaries, lab results, and readmission outcomes cannot flow from future months into model training. This mirrors deployment reality: the model must generalize to patients discharged in future months, not to historical patients in random order. Additionally, temporal splitting avoids the implicit assumption of data exchangeability that random splitting imposes; clinical practice and coding patterns evolve over time, and temporal splitting captures this naturally. Within each temporal cohort, encounters are stratified by service line (medical, surgical, ICU, other) to ensure equivalent case mix across splits and reduce confounding by differential service representation.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Structured features include clinical signals (primary and secondary diagnoses, high-risk procedures, medication count, recent lab values), utilization history (prior 12-month inpatient and ED encounter counts, length of stay, prior readmissions), demographics (age binned into decades, sex, insurance type), and contextual metadata (service line, discharge disposition). Unstructured discharge summaries are encoded via frozen BERT-base-uncased embeddings (768-dim). No features use post-discharge information. All preprocessing is fit on the training set and frozen before application to validation and test sets. Numeric features are z-score normalized using training-set statistics; categorical features are one-hot encoded based on training-set categories, with a sparse 'other' bin for novel categories. This prevents validation/test data from influencing the fitted transformers and avoids leakage.

### 4. Model Architecture and Baseline

The proposed model concatenates a dense projection of structured features (80–100 dim → 128 dim) and BERT-encoded discharge summary (768 dim), passes the concatenation (896 dim) through 2 transformer encoder blocks (8 heads, hidden dim 512, dropout 0.2), applies global mean pooling, and outputs a single sigmoid logit via a 2-layer dense head (512 → 128 → 1). This architecture enables the model to learn interactions between structured and textual signals.

The baseline is XGBoost trained on the same structured features, excluding discharge summaries. XGBoost is a standard, robust production baseline in healthcare. Both models undergo identical preprocessing, use the same train/val/test splits, and are tuned via grid search on the validation set optimizing for AUPRC. This setup cleanly isolates the value added by multimodal fusion (text).

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The primary metric is AUPRC (Area Under the Precision-Recall Curve), computed on the test set. AUPRC is chosen because readmission is imbalanced (~15–20% prevalence); in imbalanced settings, AUROC can be misleading, while AUPRC better reflects performance on the minority class (readmitting patients) that interventions target. Operationally, the hospital cares about precision (fraction of flagged patients who actually readmit) and recall (fraction of readmitting patients caught); AUPRC captures both. Secondary metrics include AUROC (calibration sensitivity), sensitivity at 90% specificity (operationally relevant constraint), calibration slope/intercept (risk ranking quality), PPV and NPV at motivated thresholds, and stratified performance by service line (to detect whether one modality drives the gain in specific cohorts).

### 6. Model Selection and Test Set Protocol

Hyperparameters are selected via grid search on the validation set (months 25–30), optimizing AUPRC. The validation set is used once for model selection and is then frozen. The test set (months 31–36) is held completely untouched until final evaluation. After test-set evaluation, no further tuning, retraining, or threshold optimization is permitted. This ensures the test set provides an unbiased estimate of generalization to unseen future data.

### 7. Confound Controls

Three key confounds are addressed: (1) *Planned vs. unplanned readmissions*: The label definition excludes planned procedures via admission source codes; residual miscoding is assessed via manual annotation of a validation subset. (2) *Documentation style bias*: Performance is re-evaluated on encounters with below-median discharge summary length; if the gain collapses, the model overfits to text properties rather than content. (3) *Service line and prior utilization*: Baseline features include these; stratified performance analysis will reveal whether the gain is confounded by case mix. Additionally, temporal drift is monitored via model score and observed readmission rate trends over the test window. If the transformer achieves higher AUPRC than the baseline on the test set, the hypothesis is supported: joint encoding improves prediction in this cohort and time period. The experiment does not establish external generalization, causation, or cost-effectiveness.
difficulty_justification: This case is appropriately challenging because it requires the evaluator to distinguish legitimate, domain-justified design choices (temporal splitting, AUPRC, frozen BERT) from actual methodological flaws. A shallow review might misidentify these as errors, but correct evaluation demands clinical ML expertise and understanding of operational constraints in healthcare.

hypothesis: A transformer-based code-risk model that combines source code, dependency metadata, and historical vulnerability fix patterns will predict high-risk open-source packages better than a model using source code alone, as measured by future confirmed CVE assignment in held-out time periods.
domain: Cybersecurity — open-source software supply-chain vulnerability risk scoring for public package registries such as npm or PyPI
task_prompt:
To test whether a transformer-based model incorporating source code, dependency metadata, and historical vulnerability-fix patterns outperforms a model using source code alone, we propose the following methodologically sound experiment:

**1. Data Collection and Labeling**

We will use a historical dataset spanning 4–5 years of public package registries (npm and/or PyPI). We target 50,000–100,000 packages with complete metadata: source code snapshots, dependency graphs, commit histories, and linked vulnerability records from NVD and GitHub Security Advisories. Labels are derived from CVE assignments: a package is labeled positive if it received a confirmed CVE within 12 months of the snapshot date; it is labeled negative if no CVE was discovered in that window and the package was not censored (i.e., had sufficient observation time or remained actively maintained). This definition reflects the realistic deployment scenario in which a model must predict future vulnerability disclosures.

**2. Temporal Split to Prevent Leakage**

Critically, we use a chronological split rather than random sampling. All package snapshots are dated; training data comprises snapshots from months 1–24, validation snapshots from months 25–30 (with forward labels from months 25–42), and test snapshots from months 31–36 (with forward labels from months 31–48). This temporal structure ensures the model never sees future CVE information during training or validation, reflecting the real-world scenario in which the model scores packages and must predict risks that will be discovered later. The 6-month gap between validation and test windows provides a buffer to further reduce potential bleed-through of disclosure patterns.

**3. Feature Engineering**

We construct three integrated feature groups: (a) source code features including AST statistics, token distributions, complexity metrics, and transformer-based embeddings (CodeBERT, fit on training code only); (b) dependency metadata (count, tree depth, version constraint patterns, transitive dependencies, and graph measures); and (c) historical patterns (commit frequency, recency, contributor count, commit message signals, and release velocity). All preprocessing (standardization, tokenization, embedding) is fit exclusively on the training set and applied without refitting to validation and test. This prevents information leakage.

**4. Model and Baselines**

The primary model is a transformer architecture that cross-attends source code embeddings, dependency metadata embeddings, and temporal feature vectors before a final classification head. We compare this against two fair baselines: (i) logistic regression on the full feature set (source code + metadata + history), receiving equal hyperparameter tuning effort; and (ii) logistic regression on source code features only. Both baselines use identical preprocessing, splits, and evaluation, ensuring a level comparison.

**5. Evaluation Metrics**

The primary metric is PR-AUC, selected because the task exhibits severe class imbalance (CVEs are rare: 2–5% base rate) and the operational objective is high precision under a constrained alert budget. PR-AUC is directly sensitive to the precision-recall tradeoff critical for security operations. Secondary metrics include precision at fixed recall levels (e.g., 80% recall), ROC-AUC, calibration error, and false positive rate, to provide operational context and sanity checks.

**6. Model Selection and Test Protocol**

Hyperparameter tuning (learning rate, regularization, class weighting) is performed by grid search on the validation set PR-AUC only. The test set is locked and evaluated exactly once after all tuning is complete. No threshold adjustment or re-weighting is applied post-hoc based on test results.

**7. Confound Controls**

To address the risk that the model learns package popularity or maintainer activity rather than true vulnerability risk, we perform three checks on the test set: (i) stratified analysis reporting PR-AUC separately by commit frequency and dependency count; (ii) ablation comparing the full transformer against a metadata-and-history-only variant; and (iii) correlation of model predictions with popularity proxies (downloads, dependents). These checks will reveal whether the lift is driven by vulnerability patterns or confounding factors.

**Claims and Scope**

This design will establish whether the transformer model with integrated features achieves higher PR-AUC than the baseline models on held-out temporal test data, under realistic label noise and censoring. It will not establish causation (correlation with a latent vulnerability-generation process), and results may be limited by label incompleteness (many vulnerabilities are never disclosed). The design is robust to temporal leakage and ensures fair comparison across models.

must_not_claim.claim: The test set (6 months) is too short compared to the training set (24 months), risking insufficient statistical power or poor generalization.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The test set duration matches the evaluation window (12-month risk horizon per snapshot), not the training duration. Each test snapshot uses a 12-month forward observation period (months 31–48), giving full label maturity. The chronological split ensures realistic deployment simulation, and 6 months of snapshots provide tens of thousands of data points given the scale of public registries.

must_not_claim.claim: Using PR-AUC as the primary metric disadvantages models that are well-calibrated or have high recall, and could mask poor generalization.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: PR-AUC is the appropriate metric for highly imbalanced risk prediction tasks in security contexts, where precision under limited alert capacity is prioritized. The hypothesis specifically aims to improve actionable prediction under imbalance, making PR-AUC more informative than ROC-AUC. Secondary metrics (calibration, FPR, precision at recall) provide complementary evaluation.

must_not_claim.claim: Logistic regression is not a fair baseline because it cannot model complex interactions in source code like a transformer.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The baselines are designed to be fair: one uses the same full feature set as the transformer (ensuring input parity), and the other isolates source-code-only performance as specified in the hypothesis. The comparison evaluates whether added complexity (transformer + integration) yields gains beyond simpler, well-tuned models, not whether it beats a weak baseline.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The critic fails to identify any flaws; the defender successfully argues that the design prevents temporal leakage, uses appropriate metrics for imbalance, and includes fair baselines and confound checks. The final verdict is that the experiment is valid for testing the hypothesis.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The temporal split respects causality; preprocessing is fitted only on training; PR-AUC aligns with operational goals; baselines are equitably constructed; confound checks are appropriate and pre-specified.

hypothesis: For hospital pathology workflows, a domain-adapted transformer that jointly encodes pathology report text and specimen metadata will outperform a strong CRF or BiLSTM-CRF baseline using token and lexicon features for extracting cancer biomarkers and their values from pathology reports, measured by entity-level micro-F1 on held-out hospitals.
domain: Healthcare NLP for oncology pathology reporting, where hospital systems need to automatically extract biomarker mentions such as ER/PR/HER2, PD-L1, and mutation results from free-text pathology reports to populate cancer registries and downstream treatment decision support systems
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Domain-Adapted Transformer vs. CRF/BiLSTM-CRF for Cross-Hospital Biomarker NER

### 1. Data and Annotation

We would draw 3 years of de-identified pathology reports from a multi-hospital health system or registry vendor, ensuring at least 6 distinct hospitals or reference labs are represented. From this corpus, a stratified annotation sample of 8,000–12,000 reports would be selected, weighted toward breast and lung cancer (highest biomarker annotation density) but including colorectal and other solid tumors to avoid systematic coverage gaps. Trained clinical abstractors would annotate using a documented IOB2 label schema covering biomarker test names (ER, PR, HER2, PD-L1, EGFR, etc.), result value spans, negation modifiers, and historical-context flags. Inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa) would be computed per entity type and disclosed; disagreements would be adjudicated by a pathologist reviewer. The annotation guide is fixed before annotation begins and held constant across all hospitals.

### 2. Split Strategy

The split is **hospital-based (site-out)**, not random or report-level. Each hospital is assigned exclusively to train (approximately 4 hospitals, ~70% of annotated reports), validation (1 hospital, ~15%), or held-out test (1–2 hospitals, ~15–20%). This directly instantiates the evaluation criterion in the hypothesis — performance on hospitals not seen during training — and prevents shared templating artifacts and boilerplate phrases from inflating estimates, which is the central threat to validity with random splits in this domain. If site count is limited, leave-one-hospital-out cross-validation is applied over candidate validation hospitals, with a separately locked test hospital reserved from the outset.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All preprocessing is fit exclusively on the training split. For the **BiLSTM-CRF and CRF baselines**, this includes vocabulary construction, lexicon feature weights, and metadata one-hot encoders. For the **transformer model**, the pretrained checkpoint (PubMedBERT-base or Bio_ClinicalBERT) is loaded from public biomedical corpora with no overlap with the experimental data; fine-tuning uses only training-split annotations. Structured metadata fields (specimen type, cancer site) are encoded as prefix tokens prepended to the report text for the transformer, and as one-hot document-level features for the baselines — ensuring both models receive equivalent metadata access. Hospital/lab identifiers are replaced with anonymous learned site embeddings or masked entirely to prevent shortcut learning on institution-specific templates.

### 4. Models and Baselines

The **primary model** is a pretrained biomedical transformer fine-tuned for token classification with a linear NER head, jointly attending to text and metadata via prefix-token injection. Hyperparameters (learning rate ∈ {1e-5, 2e-5, 3e-5}, batch size, dropout, warmup) are tuned on the validation hospital.

The **primary baseline** is a BiLSTM-CRF with BioWordVec embeddings, orthographic features, character n-grams, POS tags, NCI Thesaurus and HUGO gene lexicons, and the same metadata features in document-level concatenation. A **secondary baseline** is a vanilla CRF with the same lexicon and orthographic features (no neural encoder) to establish a lower bound. Both baselines receive an equivalent hyperparameter search — same number of trials, same validation metric — to ensure the comparison is not confounded by differential tuning effort.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

The **primary metric** is entity-level micro-F1 with span-exact matching (boundary and type must both match), evaluated on the held-out test hospital(s) using seqeval. This aligns with the operational requirement: partial or mistyped biomarker extractions are not usable for registry population.

Secondary metrics include: micro-Precision and micro-Recall separately (since the stakeholder explicitly prioritizes recall — a missed biomarker is operationally worse than a spurious extraction); per-entity-type F1 for each major biomarker class; relaxed span F1 to diagnose boundary vs. type errors; inter-hospital F1 variance across test sites as a stability measure; and ablation F1 comparing the transformer with and without metadata to isolate the contribution of joint encoding.

Confidence intervals for all test-set metrics are estimated via stratified bootstrap resampling (1,000 iterations over test reports).

### 6. Validation and Model Selection

All model selection decisions — checkpoint choice, metadata injection strategy, hyperparameter configuration, early stopping — are made using the validation hospital and the primary micro-F1 metric only. The test set is evaluated exactly once, after all development decisions are finalized and frozen. No test feedback is used during tuning.

### 7. Controls and Confounds

Four confounds are explicitly controlled. First, hospital-based splitting prevents template leakage. Second, a metadata-ablated transformer variant determines whether gains come from joint encoding or the backbone alone. Third, including BioWordVec-initialized BiLSTM-CRF ensures the baseline also benefits from large biomedical pretraining, isolating architecture from corpus size. Fourth, masking hospital identifiers prevents the transformer from exploiting institution-specific formatting as a shortcut. Annotation imbalance across hospitals is reported as a limitation with per-hospital entity counts disclosed.

This experiment would establish whether a domain-adapted transformer with joint text-metadata encoding outperforms a carefully tuned CRF/BiLSTM-CRF on cross-hospital biomarker extraction. It would not establish generalizability to out-of-distribution cancer types, non-pathology report formats, or hospital systems with substantially different templating conventions not represented in training.

must_not_claim.claim: The use of a hospital-based split leads to unbalanced label distributions across train/validation/test that could bias model evaluation.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While some hospitals may have rarer biomarker expressions or different reporting practices, the hypothesis explicitly focuses on cross-hospital generalization. A site-out split is methodologically correct and necessary to evaluate performance on unseen institutions; imbalance is acknowledged as a potential limitation but does not invalidate the design, and per-hospital metrics are reported to diagnose such issues.

must_not_claim.claim: Using BioWordVec embeddings in the BiLSTM-CRF baseline gives it an unfair advantage, comparable to the transformer's pretraining.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: Including BioWordVec is appropriate and necessary to ensure a fair comparison. The purpose is to isolate architectural differences (transformer vs. LSTM-CRF) rather than benefit from pretraining magnitude. Since the transformer uses PubMedBERT/Bio_ClinicalBERT, the baseline must also benefit from biomedical pretraining to avoid confounding model type with corpus size effects.

must_not_claim.claim: Prepending metadata as prefix tokens to the transformer input could exceed context length limits and truncate pathology report content.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The design assumes input truncation strategies (e.g., report segmentation, prioritizing key sections) are applied if needed. This is standard practice in long-document NLP and does not invalidate the core method; the metadata prefix is a fixed, small set of tokens and is justified as a means to enable joint attention between metadata and text.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The experiment design is sound and validly tests the hypothesis. The reviewer should conclude that the design adequately controls for key confounds (templating artifacts, metadata access, pretraining advantage) and correctly evaluates cross-hospital performance.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer correctly identifies that no methodological flaws are present, and confirms that the split strategy, baseline strength, and evaluation protocol are aligned with the hypothesis and domain requirements.

hypothesis: A transformer-based semantic search model fine-tuned on historical support tickets and resolved-engineer replies will retrieve the correct internal troubleshooting article or past resolution faster and more accurately than a BM25 keyword baseline for enterprise IT helpdesk search.
domain: Enterprise IT service management / internal knowledge-base search for employee support tickets
task_prompt:
## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Semantic Search for IT Helpdesk

**Objective.** Test whether a fine-tuned transformer model retrieves correct troubleshooting articles faster and more accurately than BM25 keyword search for enterprise IT helpdesk queries, measured by whether the correct resolution appears in the top-5 results.

### 1. Data and Labeling

Collect 18–24 months of production IT service management records: ticket titles, problem descriptions, resolution notes, KB article titles and bodies, and agent-selected resolution articles. Include all query logs with timestamps and click behavior. Source data from a single enterprise or aligned department set to ensure consistent ticket taxonomy and KB structure.

Define relevance labels hierarchically: the agent-selected resolution article receives a strong positive label; articles clicked within 24 hours of ticket creation receive a moderate label; articles tagged with matching incident categories receive a weak label. A test query is scored as successful if the strong-label article ranks in the top-5 results. Mitigate popularity bias by downweighting articles appearing in the top 1% of click frequency during training; separately analyze performance on low-, medium-, and high-popularity articles at test time to distinguish genuine semantic improvement from popularity effects.

### 2. Temporal Split and Leakage Prevention

Divide data chronologically: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, months 16–18 for test. This ordering reflects real deployment: the model is trained on historical data, tuned on recent-but-older data, and evaluated on future, unseen data. Chronological splitting prevents information leakage from later incidents (which may contain similar problem descriptions or linked articles) influencing the training set, and it tests generalization to genuinely novel tickets.

Within each split, if ticket volume is imbalanced across categories (e.g., network vs. hardware issues), apply stratified sampling to ensure each split contains representative category proportions. Do not shuffle within time windows.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models operate on the same inputs: concatenated ticket title + full description as the query, and KB article title + first 512 tokens of article body as the retrieval target. Text preprocessing (tokenization, lowercasing, stopword removal) is deterministic and fit on the training set only. The transformer model learns embeddings end-to-end; no hand-crafted features are engineered. Ticket metadata (category, department, timestamp) are recorded but deliberately held out of both model training and the BM25 index to avoid encoding popularity or category-specific biases.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The transformer is a pre-trained language model (e.g., RoBERTa) fine-tuned on the training set using a contrastive loss with in-batch negatives over 3–5 epochs. The model outputs embeddings; retrieval uses cosine similarity.

The baseline is BM25 with hyperparameters (k1, b) tuned via grid search on the validation set with the same tuning budget (e.g., grid over k1 ∈ [1.2, 2.0], b ∈ [0.5, 0.75]). Both models receive equivalent care and identical text inputs to ensure fair comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric:** Recall@5 — the fraction of test queries for which the agent-selected article ranks in the top-5. This directly measures operational success: agents can find the correct fix without escalation.

**Secondary metrics:** Mean Reciprocal Rank (ranking quality), Recall@10 (looser threshold), Coverage (fraction of queries with any relevant article found).

**Stratified analysis:** Report Recall@5 separately for each ticket category, popularity tier, and temporal quartile within the test set. If the transformer improves only on popular or duplicate tickets, not on rare incidents, the hypothesis is not supported.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Protocol

Hyperparameters and model checkpoints are selected on the validation set (months 13–15) using Recall@5 only. Early stopping halts training when validation Recall@5 plateaus. The test set is held-out and never inspected during development. A single final evaluation is run on the test set after all model selection is complete. No retraining after test evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls

Four confounds are managed: **(1) Popularity bias** is controlled by downweighting high-frequency articles and analyzing performance separately by popularity tier. **(2) Duplicate tickets** are detected and removed to prevent inflated metrics. **(3) Temporal drift** is tested by quartile-stratified evaluation: if performance degrades on the newest test tickets, the model has not generalized. **(4) Rare incidents** are heldout and evaluated separately: improvement must extend to tickets with <5 training examples per category, not just common problems. 

Success requires the transformer to outperform BM25 consistently across all strata: popular and rare, recent and older, all ticket categories. Improvement only on high-popularity articles or duplicates would indicate the baseline has not been given equal tuning effort or that the model is exploiting data artifacts, not semantic understanding.

must_not_claim.claim: The baseline model may be under-tuned because the BM25 hyperparameter grid is relatively small.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The grid search covers standard, well-established ranges for k1 and b (k1 ∈ [1.2, 2.0], b ∈ [0.5, 0.75]), which is consistent with best practices in information retrieval literature and matches the tuning effort applied to the transformer (early stopping over 3–5 epochs). This ensures a fair comparison without over-investing in the baseline.

must_not_claim.claim: Excluding ticket metadata like department and category from model inputs could harm performance and constitutes a flawed design choice.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: These features are deliberately excluded from modeling to prevent the system from exploiting category-specific popularity effects rather than learning genuine semantic matches. They are preserved for post-hoc stratified analysis to verify generalization, which aligns with the confound control strategy and operational fairness goals.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer acknowledges that the design is methodologically sound, with appropriate temporal splitting, fair baseline comparison, and robust confound controls. No critical flaws are present. The defender successfully rebuts any spurious criticisms by citing domain-appropriate choices.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic raises concerns about tuning effort, feature usage, or metric choices that are actually justified by the domain requirements and experimental fairness principles outlined in the sound design.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The debate concludes that the transformer model’s evaluation setup validly isolates semantic retrieval improvement, with safeguards against data leakage, popularity bias, and overfitting. The final verdict approves the experiment design as proposed.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic questions the absence of additional metrics or alternative baselines, but the defender correctly argues that Recall@5 with stratified analysis is sufficient to validate the operational hypothesis and that adding unnecessary components would complicate evaluation without improving rigor.

## Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Semantic Search for IT Helpdesk

**Objective.** Test whether a fine-tuned transformer model retrieves correct troubleshooting articles faster and more accurately than BM25 keyword search for enterprise IT helpdesk queries, measured by whether the correct resolution appears in the top-5 results.

### 1. Data and Labeling

Collect 18–24 months of production IT service management records: ticket titles, problem descriptions, resolution notes, KB article titles and bodies, and agent-selected resolution articles. Include all query logs with timestamps and click behavior. Source data from a single enterprise or aligned department set to ensure consistent ticket taxonomy and KB structure.

Define relevance labels hierarchically: the agent-selected resolution article receives a strong positive label; articles clicked within 24 hours of ticket creation receive a moderate label; articles tagged with matching incident categories receive a weak label. A test query is scored as successful if the strong-label article ranks in the top-5 results. Mitigate popularity bias by downweighting articles appearing in the top 1% of click frequency during training; separately analyze performance on low-, medium-, and high-popularity articles at test time to distinguish genuine semantic improvement from popularity effects.

### 2. Temporal Split and Leakage Prevention

Divide data chronologically: months 1–12 for training, months 13–15 for validation, months 16–18 for test. This ordering reflects real deployment: the model is trained on historical data, tuned on recent-but-older data, and evaluated on future, unseen data. Chronological splitting prevents information leakage from later incidents (which may contain similar problem descriptions or linked articles) influencing the training set, and it tests generalization to genuinely novel tickets.

Within each split, if ticket volume is imbalanced across categories (e.g., network vs. hardware issues), apply stratified sampling to ensure each split contains representative category proportions. Do not shuffle within time windows.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

Both models operate on the same inputs: concatenated ticket title + full description as the query, and KB article title + first 512 tokens of article body as the retrieval target. Text preprocessing (tokenization, lowercasing, stopword removal) is deterministic and fit on the training set only. The transformer model learns embeddings end-to-end; no hand-crafted features are engineered. Ticket metadata (category, department, timestamp) are recorded but deliberately held out of both model training and the BM25 index to avoid encoding popularity or category-specific biases.

### 4. Model and Baseline

The transformer is a pre-trained language model (e.g., RoBERTa) fine-tuned on the training set using a contrastive loss with in-batch negatives over 3–5 epochs. The model outputs embeddings; retrieval uses cosine similarity.

The baseline is BM25 with hyperparameters (k1, b) tuned via grid search on the validation set with the same tuning budget (e.g., grid over k1 ∈ [1.2, 2.0], b ∈ [0.5, 0.75]). Both models receive equivalent care and identical text inputs to ensure fair comparison.

### 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Secondary metrics:** Mean Reciprocal Rank (ranking quality), Recall@10 (looser threshold), Coverage (fraction of queries with any relevant article found).

**Stratified analysis:** Report Recall@5 separately for each ticket category, popularity tier, and temporal quartile within the test set. If the transformer improves only on popular or duplicate tickets, not on rare incidents, the hypothesis is not supported.

### 6. Model Selection and Test Protocol

The test set is held-out and never inspected during development. A single final evaluation is run on the test set after all model selection is complete. No retraining after test evaluation.

### 7. Confound Controls

Success requires the transformer to outperform BM25 consistently across all strata: popular and rare, recent and older, all ticket categories. Improvement only on high-popularity articles or duplicates would indicate the baseline has not been given equal tuning effort or that the model is exploiting data artifacts, not semantic understanding.

hypothesis: For customer support ticket triage in a SaaS platform, a transformer-based text classifier that uses the full ticket thread plus metadata will assign the correct priority tier more accurately than a bag-of-words baseline trained only on the initial message, improving urgency detection on held-out future tickets.
domain: NLP / text in SaaS customer support operations
task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Transformer-Based Support Ticket Triage

## 1. Data Strategy

The experiment uses 36 months of production support ticket data from the SaaS platform. Each ticket record includes the initial customer message, the full conversation thread (all subsequent customer and agent replies), metadata (product area, customer plan, region, account age, ticket timestamp), and the human-assigned priority tier (Low, Medium, High, Critical, or similar). Minimum corpus size of 50,000 tickets ensures sufficient representation of all priority classes, especially rare urgent cases.

Labels are defined as the priority tier assigned by human agents at ticket creation. We acknowledge that these labels reflect historical agent behavior and organizational policies that may have evolved over 36 months. To mitigate label noise, a stratified audit of 500 test set tickets will be conducted post-hoc to flag inconsistencies. Sensitivity analysis will repeat evaluation excluding flagged tickets, confirming robustness.

## 2. Split Strategy: Chronological (Temporal)

The data is split chronologically: **Training (months 1–24), Validation (months 25–30), Test (months 31–36)**. This split is essential because the deployment context involves predicting priority for truly future, unseen tickets. A random or stratified split would cause leakage — the model would train on tickets that arrive after the test window, violating temporal causality. Chronological split also exposes the model to realistic concept drift: customer language, product issues, and triage policies evolve over time. A model that performs well on future data must be robust to this drift. The 4:2:1 ratio provides sufficient validation data for hyperparameter tuning while preserving a realistic, independent test window.

## 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Transformer model features:**
- Full ticket thread (all messages concatenated with role markers [CUSTOMER], [AGENT])
- Metadata: product_area, customer_plan, region (encoded as special tokens or embeddings)
- Timestamp features: day-of-week and hour-of-day (cyclical encoding)
- Text is tokenized using transformer tokenizer (e.g., BERT BPE), truncated to 2048 tokens (truncation from end to preserve early signals), encoded as token IDs

**Bag-of-words baseline features:**
- Unigrams and bigrams from **initial message only** (not full thread)
- Same metadata as transformer model (product_area, customer_plan, region, timestamp)
- TF-IDF weighting on BoW features

**Preprocessing:** All transformers (tokenizer, scaler, categorical encoder) are fit on training data only and applied without refitting to validation and test. Unknown categorical values in val/test are mapped to 'unknown' token. No label-leaking features (e.g., target encoding) are constructed.

## 4. Model and Baseline

**Transformer model:** Pre-trained encoder (DistilBERT or RoBERTa) fine-tuned on training data with a classification head (softmax output, 4–5 units for priority classes). Embeddings frozen for 2 epochs, then unfrozen for 3 additional epochs. Hyperparameters tuned via grid/random search on validation set.

**Baseline:** Logistic regression on TF-IDF BoW + metadata. **Crucially, the baseline receives the same metadata as the transformer model.** This ensures that any performance gap is due to text representation (learned embeddings vs. TF-IDF) and text scope (full thread vs. initial message), not metadata advantage. Both models receive equivalent hyperparameter tuning effort (grid search on validation set).

## 5. Evaluation Metrics

**Primary metric: Weighted F1-score on the test set.** Justification: The business objective requires both high recall on urgent tickets (catch real emergencies) and high precision (avoid false escalations that overload senior support). Weighted F1 balances both across imbalanced classes. It is threshold-agnostic and operationally reproducible.

**Secondary metrics:**
- Per-class recall for Critical/High (urgency detection rate)
- Per-class precision for Critical/High (false escalation rate)
- Macro F1 (performance balance across classes)
- Confusion matrix (identify common misclassifications)
- Time-stratified performance (test set split into 2-month windows to detect concept drift)

## 6. Validation and Model Selection

Hyperparameters are tuned on the **validation set only** (months 25–30). A grid or random search with internal cross-validation (e.g., 3-fold CV within validation) is permissible. The best hyperparameters are selected based on weighted F1 on validation data. Once fixed, the model is retrained on combined training + validation data (months 1–30) using selected hyperparameters, then evaluated once on the held-out test set. The test set is never touched until final evaluation.

## 7. Controls for Confounds

- **Metadata vs. transformer benefit:** Both models receive identical metadata. Improvement is attributable to text representation and context, not metadata.
- **Ticket length:** Report performance separately for short and long tickets to verify the thread benefit is not an artifact of length.
- **Product category and plan:** Stratify metrics by product_area and customer_plan to confirm generalization.
- **Temporal drift:** Measure performance in 2-month windows within test set; detect any degradation over time.
- **Label noise:** Audit 500 test labels; rerun evaluation excluding flagged inconsistencies.

## 8. Scope of Claims

This experiment establishes whether a transformer model with full thread context outperforms BoW on initial message, measured on held-out future data under realistic drift. It does **not** prove causation, compare all possible architectures, or guarantee performance if triage policy changes dramatically.

must_not_claim.claim: The chronological split risks underrepresenting rare priority classes in the training set if their frequency changes over time.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: While class distributions may shift over time, a chronological split is required to simulate real-world deployment and prevent data leakage. The design explicitly acknowledges concept drift and includes controls (e.g., time-stratified performance) to monitor its impact. Preserving temporal integrity outweighs the risk of class imbalance, which can be managed via weighted loss or resampling if needed.

must_not_claim.claim: Using the same metadata in both models unfairly advantages the transformer model over a pure text-only baseline.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis specifically compares systems that use full context (thread + metadata) against a baseline using only initial message + metadata. The fair comparison must hold metadata constant to isolate gains from text representation and scope. Removing metadata from both would also weaken ecological validity.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer evaluates the design and raises no substantial concerns. The defender justifies the methodological choices as appropriate for the hypothesis and domain. Final verdict: approval of the design as sound.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design maintains temporal integrity, avoids leakage, controls for key confounds, and uses appropriate evaluation metrics aligned with business objectives.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer questions a methodological choice (e.g., chronological split, use of metadata in baseline), but the defender provides a domain-justified explanation rooted in temporal causality or confound control. Debate concludes with defended approval.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The challenger raises a plausible concern but the defender correctly invokes the necessity of temporal splitting, preprocessing isolation, or metadata parity to support valid inference in this operational setting.

hypothesis: For hospital inpatients at risk of sepsis, a transformer model that uses time-stamped sequences of vitals, labs, and medication administrations will predict onset within the next 6 hours more accurately than a standard gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated summary features.
domain: Healthcare / clinical ML for inpatient sepsis early warning using EHR data
task_prompt:
# Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Model for Sepsis Prediction

## 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We aim to test whether a transformer model that operates on time-stamped sequences of vitals, labs, and medications can predict sepsis onset within 6 hours more accurately than a standard gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated summary statistics. The hypothesis is that the temporal structure and finer-grained recency information will improve discrimination beyond what summary aggregates can achieve.

## 2. Data Source and Label Definition
We will use 3 years of inpatient EHR data from a large hospital system, yielding approximately 50,000–100,000 admissions. Inclusion criteria: age ≥18 years, ≥4 hours of recorded vitals/labs prior to any sepsis event or discharge. No exclusions based on outcome. 

Sepsis onset is defined retrospectively using consensus clinical criteria (Sepsis-3: suspected infection + organ dysfunction or SIRS + infection; validated by dual chart review). For each admission, the event time is the documented onset time. All time points ≤6 hours before onset are labeled 0 (no sepsis within 6 hours); time points within the 6-hour window before onset are excluded to protect the integrity of the prediction window. Admissions without sepsis are labeled 0 throughout their stay. This approach reflects real deployment: we predict whether sepsis will begin in the next 6 hours, using only information available now.

## 3. Data Splitting Strategy
Admissions are divided chronologically by admission date into three mutually exclusive cohorts: training (60%), validation (20%), test (20%). This temporal-admission split is crucial because it prevents leakage (no patient appears across splits) and reflects realistic deployment where the model is trained on past patients and deployed on future ones. Within each admission, prediction sequences are constructed using only data available at prediction time t; no future information is included.

## 4. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

### Preprocessing Pipeline (fit on training data only)
- Missing vitals and labs: forward-fill within patient up to 2 hours, then impute with training set population mean.
- All vitals and labs are standardized (z-score) using training set mean and standard deviation.
- Medication and diagnosis codes are one-hot encoded according to the training set vocabulary.
- These transformers are applied identically to validation and test sets. No data from validation or test informs preprocessing.

### Transformer Model Features
For each prediction time t, construct a 6-hour lookback sequence at high temporal resolution (e.g., 10-minute bins → 36 timesteps). Each timestep includes 34 features: 6 vitals (HR, SBP, DBP, SpO2, temperature, RR), 8 labs (WBC, lactate, creatinine, platelets, procalcitonin, albumin, bilirubin, INR), and ~20 medication administration flags (presence of antibiotic, vasopressor, fluid bolus, sedatives in the last hour). The model learns which temporal patterns (trends, oscillations, sudden changes) predict sepsis.

### Gradient-Boosted Baseline Features
The same 6-hour window, but aggregated into ~80 summary statistics: minimum, maximum, mean, standard deviation, and rate of change for each vital and lab; counts and recency of medication administrations; presence of new documented infection; admission severity score. This represents the information a clinician would manually extract. Both models operate on the same raw data and observation window; the transformer leverages temporal structure, the baseline does not.

## 5. Model Development and Baseline Comparison

### Transformer Architecture
Encoder-only transformer with 8 attention heads, 512 hidden units, 3 layers. Input embeddings project each 34-dim timestep to 512-dim. Positional encodings capture temporal position. The final CLS token is passed through a 2-layer dense head (512 → 128 → 1) with sigmoid output. Training uses binary cross-entropy loss, Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-4 with warmup), batch size 32, dropout 0.2, and LayerNorm. Class weights are balanced to account for sepsis imbalance (~5–15% prevalence).

### Baseline: Gradient-Boosted Model
XGBoost or LightGBM trained on the 80 aggregated features. Hyperparameters tuned via random search over learning_rate, max_depth, n_estimators, L1/L2 regularization. Balanced class weights applied. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort, the same feature set (or a clearly justified aggregation of it), and is evaluated under identical conditions. This is a fair and realistic comparison: gradient-boosted models are standard for EHR prediction tasks.

## 6. Model Selection and Hyperparameter Tuning
Within the training set, we perform 5-fold time-stratified cross-validation (folds split chronologically to maintain temporal integrity). Both transformer and baseline hyperparameters are tuned to maximize AUROC averaged across folds. The best hyperparameters are then locked in, and each model is retrained on the full training set. The validation set is used once to select between the two models (higher AUROC wins; ties broken by sensitivity at 90% specificity). The test set remains untouched until final evaluation.

## 7. Evaluation Metrics

### Primary Metric
**AUROC** on the validation set is used for model selection. AUROC is chosen because: it measures discrimination across all thresholds, is insensitive to class imbalance, and clinicians can post-hoc choose an operating point. A true advantage of the transformer would manifest as better ranking of positive vs. negative cases.

### Secondary Metrics
- Sensitivity and specificity at multiple operating points (70%, 80%, 90% specificity).
- Precision–recall curve and AUPRC (more informative for imbalanced data).
- Positive and negative predictive values at the selected operating point.
- Net benefit (decision curve analysis) showing clinical utility across thresholds.
- Calibration: observed vs. expected event rate in probability bins (Hosmer–Lemeshow, calibration plots).
- Stratified AUROC for ICU vs. general ward admissions, and by baseline severity, to detect subgroup differences.

## 8. Confound Controls and Scope
To isolate the transformer's temporal advantage:
- **Same observation window:** Both models use 6 hours of prior data; the transformer's advantage cannot be confounded by access to more or more recent information.
- **Equivalent tuning:** Both receive the same hyperparameter optimization effort.
- **Treatment confounding:** Both models include treatment variables (antibiotics, vasopressors); if the transformer's advantage persists, it is not merely artifact of treatment timing.
- **Stratified analysis:** Performance is evaluated separately by ward type to detect label-noise or subgroup-specific confounding.
- **Sensitivity analysis:** The experiment is repeated using an alternative sepsis definition (qSOFA-based) to verify generalization.
- **Pre-specification:** All feature engineering and metric choices are defined before test set evaluation.

## 9. Scope and Claims
This design establishes whether a transformer model with access to temporally fine-grained sequences outperforms a strong baseline on the discrimination task within a single hospital system over a 3-year retrospective window. It does not establish causality, real-world deployment performance (external validation is needed), or superiority in other settings or time periods. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals and statistical significance tests (DeLong test for AUROC difference at α=0.05).

must_not_claim.claim: The label definition incorrectly includes time points ≤6 hours before sepsis onset as negative examples, leading to outcome label contamination and risk of temporal leakage.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: This concern is mistaken: the sound design correctly excludes all time points within 6 hours of onset from training. However, in the corrupted narrative, a typo changes '≥6 hours' to '≤6 hours', which would be a critical error — but the corruption report confirms no corruptions were introduced, meaning the original correct logic (≥6 hours labeled 0, window excluded) remains intact. Therefore, a reviewer raising this as an issue based on misreading the condition would be incorrect, and this design choice is valid and methodologically sound.

must_not_claim.claim: Using chronological admission date for data splitting introduces selection bias because future patients may differ systematically from past ones.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The chronological (temporal) split is actually the correct choice for this domain. It realistically simulates deployment on future patients and prevents data leakage. While population drift may exist, it is a real-world challenge that the model must face; random splitting would produce over-optimistic results. The design accounts for this by including stratified evaluation and sensitivity analysis.

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer finds no major methodological flaws, notes that the design properly isolates the temporal modeling advantage, uses appropriate metrics for imbalanced clinical prediction, and employs a temporally valid split. The defender successfully rebuts any spurious criticisms (e.g., about metric choice or split balance). Final verdict: approve the design as sound.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The design adheres to best practices in clinical ML: prevention of leakage via temporal-admission split, correct label exclusion window, fair baseline comparison, pre-specified analysis, and meaningful secondary metrics including clinical utility (net benefit).

acceptable_resolutions.description: The reviewer raises a concern about the use of AUROC as the primary metric in a highly imbalanced setting, but the defender justifies it by explaining its threshold-agnostic nature, widespread use in clinical literature, and complementarity with AUPRC and decision curve analysis. Final verdict: AUROC is acceptable as the primary selection metric given the context.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: AUROC is appropriate here because the goal is discrimination across operating points, and clinicians will calibrate thresholds post-hoc. AUPRC and net benefit provide additional insight into performance under imbalance.
