# Experiment Design: Transformer vs. Gradient-Boosted Model for Sepsis Prediction

## 1. Objective and Hypothesis
We aim to test whether a transformer model that operates on time-stamped sequences of vitals, labs, and medications can predict sepsis onset within 6 hours more accurately than a standard gradient-boosted model trained on aggregated summary statistics. The hypothesis is that the temporal structure and finer-grained recency information will improve discrimination beyond what summary aggregates can achieve.

## 2. Data Source and Label Definition
We will use 3 years of inpatient EHR data from a large hospital system, yielding approximately 50,000–100,000 admissions. Inclusion criteria: age ≥18 years, ≥4 hours of recorded vitals/labs prior to any sepsis event or discharge. No exclusions based on outcome. 

Sepsis onset is defined retrospectively using consensus clinical criteria (Sepsis-3: suspected infection + organ dysfunction or SIRS + infection; validated by dual chart review). For each admission, the event time is the documented onset time. All time points ≥6 hours before onset are labeled 0 (no sepsis within 6 hours); time points within the 6-hour window before onset are excluded to protect the integrity of the prediction window. Admissions without sepsis are labeled 0 throughout their stay. This approach reflects real deployment: we predict whether sepsis will begin in the next 6 hours, using only information available now.

## 3. Data Splitting Strategy
Admissions are divided chronologically by admission date into three mutually exclusive cohorts: training (60%), validation (20%), test (20%). This temporal-admission split is crucial because it prevents leakage (no patient appears across splits) and reflects realistic deployment where the model is trained on past patients and deployed on future ones. Within each admission, prediction sequences are constructed using only data available at prediction time t; no future information is included.

## 4. Preprocessing and Feature Engineering

### Preprocessing Pipeline (fit on training data only)
- Missing vitals and labs: forward-fill within patient up to 2 hours, then impute with training set population mean.
- All vitals and labs are standardized (z-score) using training set mean and standard deviation.
- Medication and diagnosis codes are one-hot encoded according to the training set vocabulary.
- These transformers are applied identically to validation and test sets. No data from validation or test informs preprocessing.

### Transformer Model Features
For each prediction time t, construct a 6-hour lookback sequence at high temporal resolution (e.g., 10-minute bins → 36 timesteps). Each timestep includes 34 features: 6 vitals (HR, SBP, DBP, SpO2, temperature, RR), 8 labs (WBC, lactate, creatinine, platelets, procalcitonin, albumin, bilirubin, INR), and ~20 medication administration flags (presence of antibiotic, vasopressor, fluid bolus, sedatives in the last hour). The model learns which temporal patterns (trends, oscillations, sudden changes) predict sepsis.

### Gradient-Boosted Baseline Features
The same 6-hour window, but aggregated into ~80 summary statistics: minimum, maximum, mean, standard deviation, and rate of change for each vital and lab; counts and recency of medication administrations; presence of new documented infection; admission severity score. This represents the information a clinician would manually extract. Both models operate on the same raw data and observation window; the transformer leverages temporal structure, the baseline does not.

## 5. Model Development and Baseline Comparison

### Transformer Architecture
Encoder-only transformer with 8 attention heads, 512 hidden units, 3 layers. Input embeddings project each 34-dim timestep to 512-dim. Positional encodings capture temporal position. The final CLS token is passed through a 2-layer dense head (512 → 128 → 1) with sigmoid output. Training uses binary cross-entropy loss, Adam optimizer (learning rate 1e-4 with warmup), batch size 32, dropout 0.2, and LayerNorm. Class weights are balanced to account for sepsis imbalance (~5–15% prevalence).

### Baseline: Gradient-Boosted Model
XGBoost or LightGBM trained on the 80 aggregated features. Hyperparameters tuned via random search over learning_rate, max_depth, n_estimators, L1/L2 regularization. Balanced class weights applied. The baseline receives equivalent tuning effort, the same feature set (or a clearly justified aggregation of it), and is evaluated under identical conditions. This is a fair and realistic comparison: gradient-boosted models are standard for EHR prediction tasks.

## 6. Model Selection and Hyperparameter Tuning
Within the training set, we perform 5-fold time-stratified cross-validation (folds split chronologically to maintain temporal integrity). Both transformer and baseline hyperparameters are tuned to maximize AUROC averaged across folds. The best hyperparameters are then locked in, and each model is retrained on the full training set. The validation set is used once to select between the two models (higher AUROC wins; ties broken by sensitivity at 90% specificity). The test set remains untouched until final evaluation.

## 7. Evaluation Metrics

### Primary Metric
**AUROC** on the validation set is used for model selection. AUROC is chosen because: it measures discrimination across all thresholds, is insensitive to class imbalance, and clinicians can post-hoc choose an operating point. A true advantage of the transformer would manifest as better ranking of positive vs. negative cases.

### Secondary Metrics
- Sensitivity and specificity at multiple operating points (70%, 80%, 90% specificity).
- Precision–recall curve and AUPRC (more informative for imbalanced data).
- Positive and negative predictive values at the selected operating point.
- Net benefit (decision curve analysis) showing clinical utility across thresholds.
- Calibration: observed vs. expected event rate in probability bins (Hosmer–Lemeshow, calibration plots).
- Stratified AUROC for ICU vs. general ward admissions, and by baseline severity, to detect subgroup differences.

## 8. Confound Controls and Scope
To isolate the transformer's temporal advantage:
- **Same observation window:** Both models use 6 hours of prior data; the transformer's advantage cannot be confounded by access to more or more recent information.
- **Equivalent tuning:** Both receive the same hyperparameter optimization effort.
- **Treatment confounding:** Both models include treatment variables (antibiotics, vasopressors); if the transformer's advantage persists, it is not merely artifact of treatment timing.
- **Stratified analysis:** Performance is evaluated separately by ward type to detect label-noise or subgroup-specific confounding.
- **Sensitivity analysis:** The experiment is repeated using an alternative sepsis definition (qSOFA-based) to verify generalization.
- **Pre-specification:** All feature engineering and metric choices are defined before test set evaluation.

## 9. Scope and Claims
This design establishes whether a transformer model with access to temporally fine-grained sequences outperforms a strong baseline on the discrimination task within a single hospital system over a 3-year retrospective window. It does not establish causality, real-world deployment performance (external validation is needed), or superiority in other settings or time periods. Results are reported with 95% confidence intervals and statistical significance tests (DeLong test for AUROC difference at α=0.05).
difficulty_justification: This case is moderately challenging because the design is largely sound, requiring the reviewer to distinguish legitimate methodological concerns from reasonable design choices in a complex clinical ML context, and to avoid over-critiquing robust practices such as temporal splits and AUROC usage.

hypothesis: A temporal gradient-boosted model that uses the full sequence of prior claims, prescription refills, and lab-result trends will predict 30-day hospital readmission for adult heart-failure patients better than a static model built from the latest encounter summary and demographics.
domain: Healthcare / claims-linked electronic health records for adult heart-failure admissions in a hospital network
task_prompt:
### Experiment Design: Temporal Sequence Modeling for 30-Day Heart-Failure Readmission Prediction

#### 1. Data Cohort and Label Definition

We would source 4 years of linked EHR and claims data from a single hospital network, comprising approximately 8,000–15,000 adult heart-failure discharges (ICD-10: I50.x) with ≥6 months of prior utilization history available for each patient. Patients are excluded if discharged to hospice, transferred without a clear acute readmission record, or missing discharge documentation.

The outcome is binary: unplanned readmission to acute-care hospital within 30 calendar days of discharge, ascertained from encounter records and claims. Planned readmissions (scheduled dialysis, elective procedures) are flagged separately; stratified analysis post-hoc distinguishes unplanned readmissions from planned returns. Expected baseline rate: 15–25%.

#### 2. Temporal Split Strategy

The dataset is divided chronologically by admission date (discharge date) into three non-overlapping windows: months 1–24 (training), months 25–36 (validation), months 37–48 (test). This temporal ordering is crucial: it prevents future information from leaking into the training set and reflects real-world deployment, where the model must predict on admissions that occur after the model was trained.

No patient-level stratification is used. Patients may appear multiple times (one row per admission), and temporal ordering is the primary control for distribution shift. This approach ensures that (1) the validation and test sets represent truly future patients, (2) any systematic changes in readmission rate or patient composition over time are preserved and visible, and (3) the baseline model receives the same temporal-split treatment, ensuring a fair comparison.

#### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

**Temporal Model:** The full longitudinal history for 12 months prior to discharge is assembled for each patient:
- Monthly utilization counts: ED visits, inpatient admissions, outpatient encounters
- Lab trends: last 3 values for creatinine, BNP, ejection fraction, potassium; computed as raw value, slope, and coefficient of variation over the 12-month window
- Medication sequence: monthly binary indicators for ACE inhibitor, beta-blocker, and diuretic use
- Comorbidity counts from claims (Elixhauser/Charlson components)
- Discharge encounter features: admission reason, length of stay, disposition, in-hospital procedures

These are flattened into a single wide feature vector (one row per admission) and fed into a gradient-boosted classifier (XGBoost or LightGBM).

**Static Baseline Model:** Built from discharge encounter snapshot only — age, sex, race, current vital signs (BP, HR, O2 sat), most recent lab values (single measurement, not trends), comorbidity count, disposition, and admission reason.

Critically, **no post-discharge signals** (e.g., medications filled after discharge, lab values measured post-discharge, return ED visits within 7 days) are used in either model. All features are defined using information available at or before discharge.

All scaling, encoding, and imputation transformers are fit on the training set only and applied to validation and test sets deterministically, preventing data leakage.

#### 4. Model and Baseline

The temporal model is a gradient-boosted classifier (XGBoost/LightGBM) hyperparameter-tuned on the validation set to maximize F1-score (subject to ≥60% recall constraint). The baseline is logistic regression, also tuned on validation data with L2 regularization, using only static discharge features.

Both models receive equivalent effort: hyperparameter gridsearch over the same validation set, same preprocessing pipeline, same evaluation protocol. The comparison isolates the value of temporal sequence modeling.

#### 5. Evaluation and Model Selection

**Primary metric:** F1-score at the operating point selected on validation data to maximize precision while maintaining recall ≥60%. This threshold is locked and applied to the test set without modification. F1 is chosen because clinicians need both sensitivity (identify true high-risk cases) and specificity (minimize false alarms), and it directly supports risk-based care triage.

**Secondary metrics:** AUROC (overall discrimination), precision/recall separately, calibration (expected calibration error or binned plots), and subgroup performance by prior measurement frequency, prior ED utilization, and age group. Subgroup analysis is essential for detecting whether the temporal model's lift comes from capturing true trajectory patterns or simply from exploiting measurement-frequency proxies for severity.

#### 6. Test Set Integrity

The test set (months 37–48) is touched exactly once, at the end, to compute final metrics. No iterative refinement or threshold adjustment based on test results. If test performance deviates materially from validation performance (e.g., F1 drop >5 percentage points), this is flagged as distribution shift and reported, but does not trigger retraining.

#### 7. Confound Control

Several known confounds are addressed:
- **Severity bias:** High readmission risk co-occurs with high prior utilization (more lab measurements, more visits). We control by including prior utilization counts in both models and separately reporting subgroup metrics by measurement frequency. If temporal lift appears only in high-utilization subgroups, this suggests the model is capturing measurement intensity, not trajectory.
- **Demographic confounds:** Subgroup metrics by race/ethnicity and urban/rural status are reported to detect if performance differences are driven by demographic proxies rather than clinical trajectory.
- **Secular trends:** Temporal split ensures no model is simply fitting healthcare system drift over time.

This design establishes whether a temporal gradient-boosted model outperforms static logistic regression for 30-day readmission prediction in a real-world, prospective validation window, with explicit controls for confounding and subgroup performance verification.

must_not_claim.claim: The temporal split does not use patient-level stratification and could leak information due to repeated patients across splits.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The hypothesis concerns episode-level prediction, where each admission is treated as an independent instance at prediction time. Temporal ordering by discharge date ensures that no future admissions are used in training, preventing time-based leakage. Patient-level re-use is realistic and valid in this clinical setting, as patients often have multiple hospitalizations over time.

must_not_claim.claim: The primary metric uses F1-score with a recall constraint, which might be arbitrary or suboptimal compared to AUROC.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: F1-score is clinically appropriate because care managers need balanced sensitivity and precision: catching high-risk patients (recall) while avoiding excessive false positives (precision). The recall floor of 60% ensures the model does not miss too many true readmissions, reflecting real-world safety constraints in transitional care programs.

must_not_claim.claim: The static baseline uses only discharge features and ignores demographics like age and sex, making it too weak a baseline.
must_not_claim.why_wrong: The static baseline explicitly includes age, sex, and race as stated in the feature list. The comparison is fair because both models are built from data available at discharge, and all preprocessing and tuning efforts are matched.

acceptable_resolutions.description: No valid critique can overturn the soundness of this experiment design; the defender successfully rebuts all objections by appealing to domain-specific requirements, clinical utility, and proper methodology. The final verdict is that the temporal model evaluation is fairly and rigorously benchmarked against a realistic baseline.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The reviewer acknowledges that all modeling choices — temporal split, feature scope, metric selection, confound controls — are justified by the clinical use case and robust ML principles. Any raised concerns are properly addressed using contextual reasoning.

acceptable_resolutions.description: A reviewer raises a concern but accepts the defender’s evidence-based rebuttal, concluding that the design satisfies the requirements for valid, prospective evaluation of temporal modeling benefit.
acceptable_resolutions.supports_defense_if: The critic identifies a plausible risk (e.g., severity confounding) and the defender shows that the design already includes appropriate controls (subgroup analysis, calibration, inclusion of utilization counts), rendering the concern mitigated.
